_meta.description: Orthodox Menaion (fixed-cycle commemorations). Keyed by canonical Menaion month-day (MM-DD). New Calendar jurisdictions observe each entry on its corresponding Gregorian date; Old Calendar jurisdictions observe it 13 days later in civil terms (1900-2099). The Menaion entries themselves are universal.
_meta.license: Theosis-owned editorial content. Names and dates are historical/liturgical facts; summary prose is original.
_meta.coverage: May (full month) — first implementation slice. Other months populated incrementally.

05-01.title: Prophet Jeremiah
05-01.summary: The weeping prophet of Jerusalem, who foretold the captivity of his people and the new covenant written on the heart. Stoned by the exiles in Egypt for his witness.

05-02.title: St. Athanasius the Great, Archbishop of Alexandria
05-02.summary: The fourth-century defender of the Nicene faith against the Arians, five times exiled for confessing the Son consubstantial with the Father. Today commemorates the translation of his relics.

05-03.title: Martyrs Timothy and Maura
05-03.summary: The reader Timothy and his wife Maura, newly wed, were martyred together in Egypt under Diocletian for refusing to surrender the Scriptures. Also commemorated: St. Theodosius of the Kiev Caves.

also.name: St. Theodosius of the Kiev Caves
also.summary: Eleventh-century abbot of the Kiev Caves Lavra and father of Russian cenobitic monasticism.

also.name: Martyr Diodorus

05-04.title: Martyr Pelagia of Tarsus
05-04.summary: A young woman of fourth-century Tarsus who refused the emperor's son in marriage to keep her vow to Christ, and was placed in a heated bronze bull where she gave up her soul in song.

05-05.title: Great-martyr Irene of Macedonia
05-05.summary: Born to a pagan king and called from idols to Christ in girlhood; her courage under torture brought thousands to faith. Her feast is kept with particular joy in the Greek tradition.

05-06.title: Righteous Job the Long-suffering
05-06.summary: The patriarch of Uz, who lost his children, his health, and all his goods in a single day and yet would not curse God. The Church reads from his book on Holy Friday, the day of patient suffering.

also.name: Prophet Job the Long-suffering

05-07.title: Commemoration of the Sign of the Precious Cross at Jerusalem
05-07.summary: In 351, a luminous cross of light appeared over Jerusalem from Golgotha to the Mount of Olives, seen by the whole city. St. Cyril of Jerusalem reported the sign to the emperor.

05-08.title: Apostle and Evangelist John the Theologian
05-08.summary: The disciple whom Jesus loved, who alone of the Twelve died in peace. The Church commemorates today the dust that rose annually from his tomb at Ephesus, healing the sick.

05-09.title: Translation of the Relics of St. Nicholas the Wonderworker to Bari
05-09.summary: In 1087, sailors translated the relics of St. Nicholas, archbishop of Myra, from his violated tomb to the city of Bari in Italy, where they remain and continue to issue myrrh.

05-10.title: Apostle Simon the Zealot
05-10.summary: One of the Twelve, called the Zealot in distinction from Simon Peter. Tradition sends him preaching as far as Britain, where he received the crown of martyrdom.

05-11.title: Holy Equal-to-the-Apostles Cyril and Methodius
05-11.summary: Brothers from Thessalonica who in the ninth century brought the Gospel and the liturgy to the Slavic peoples, devising the alphabet that bears their name and translating the Scriptures into Slavonic.

also.name: Hieromartyr Mocius of Amphipolis

05-12.title: St. Epiphanius of Cyprus
05-12.summary: The fourth-century archbishop of Salamis and a tireless adversary of heresy. Also commemorated: St. Germanus, Patriarch of Constantinople, who defended the holy icons against the first iconoclasm.

also.name: St. Germanus, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Eighth-century defender of the holy icons against the first iconoclasm.

also.name: St. Theodore the Bishop of Cyrenia

05-13.title: Martyr Glyceria
05-13.summary: A virgin of Trajanopolis in second-century Thrace who confessed Christ before the prefect, smashed the idol of Zeus, and was given to the lions, who would not touch her.

05-14.title: Martyr Isidore of Chios
05-14.summary: A Roman officer of the third century, betrayed as a Christian by his pagan father. Beheaded on Chios after refusing to sacrifice; his well still marks the place.

05-15.title: St. Pachomius the Great
05-15.summary: The fourth-century desert father of Egypt who received from an angel the rule of cenobitic life, gathering the monks of Tabennisi into the first true monastery.

05-16.title: St. Theodore the Sanctified
05-16.summary: The favorite disciple of St. Pachomius and his successor as abbot of Tabennisi. The Church remembers him a day after his teacher.

05-17.title: Apostle Andronicus of the Seventy
05-17.summary: Greeted by St. Paul in the letter to the Romans as 'my kinsman and fellow prisoner, of note among the apostles.' He labored with St. Junia in spreading the Gospel through Pannonia.

05-18.title: Martyr Theodotus of Ancyra and the Seven Virgins
05-18.summary: An innkeeper of Ancyra who hid Christians during Diocletian's persecution; condemned for retrieving and burying the bodies of seven aged virgin-martyrs drowned in a lake.

05-19.title: Hieromartyr Patrick, Bishop of Prusa
05-19.summary: A second-century bishop of Bithynia, martyred with three of his presbyters after a sermon at the hot springs of Prusa in which he turned the praise of pagan miracles back to the God who made the waters.

05-20.title: Martyr Thalleleus
05-20.summary: A young physician of Cilicia in the third century who healed the sick without payment and was beheaded for refusing to sacrifice. The model for the unmercenary saints.

05-21.title: Holy Equal-to-the-Apostles Constantine and his mother Helena
05-21.summary: The emperor who saw the sign of the Cross before the Milvian Bridge in 312 and granted peace to the Church, and his mother who in old age sought out and recovered the True Cross in Jerusalem.

also.name: Helen, Equal-to-the-Apostles, mother of Constantine

05-22.title: Martyr Basiliscus
05-22.summary: Nephew of St. Theodore the Tyro and a soldier of the Roman army, martyred in Comana at the start of the fourth century. St. John Chrysostom in his exile prayed at his tomb the night before he died.

05-23.title: St. Michael the Confessor, Bishop of Synnada
05-23.summary: A ninth-century Phrygian hierarch who suffered exile and harsh confinement for defending the veneration of icons under the second iconoclasm.

05-24.title: St. Symeon the Stylite of the Wonderful Mountain
05-24.summary: The sixth-century Antiochene ascetic who lived sixty-eight years on a pillar near Antioch, gathering crowds with his prophetic counsel and miracles of healing.

05-25.title: Third Finding of the Honorable Head of John the Baptist
05-25.summary: The relic of the Forerunner's head, hidden again during the iconoclast turmoil of the ninth century, was rediscovered in Comana around 850 and translated to Constantinople.

05-26.title: Apostle Carpus of the Seventy
05-26.summary: Companion of St. Paul, who entrusted to him the parchments and cloak in the second letter to Timothy. He served as bishop of Beroea in Thrace and reposed in peace.

05-27.title: Hieromartyr Helladius
05-27.summary: A third-century bishop in the East, scourged and burned for confessing Christ during Diocletian's persecution. Few details survive beyond the constancy of his witness.

05-28.title: St. Nicetas the Confessor, Bishop of Chalcedon
05-28.summary: An eighth-century hierarch who endured beating, imprisonment, and exile under the iconoclast emperors rather than yield the icons to fire.

05-29.title: Virgin-martyr Theodosia of Tyre
05-29.summary: An eighteen-year-old of Tyre who in the year 308 approached condemned Christians to give them words of comfort, and was at once seized, tortured, and cast into the sea.

05-30.title: St. Isaac of Dalmatia
05-30.summary: The fourth-century abbot who rebuked the emperor Valens on his march against the Goths for closing the churches; the emperor died as Isaac foretold, and the monastery of Dalmatia rose at his prayer.

05-31.title: Apostle Hermas of the Seventy
05-31.summary: One of the apostles greeted in the closing chapter of the letter to the Romans. Tradition makes him bishop of Philippopolis in Thrace; sometimes identified with the author of The Shepherd.

06-01.title: Martyr Justin the Philosopher
06-01.summary: The second-century apologist who, finding Christ to be the answer his philosophy had been seeking, opened a school of Christian thought in Rome and gave his life under Marcus Aurelius for the truth he had come to love.

06-02.title: St. Nicephorus the Confessor, Patriarch of Constantinople
06-02.summary: A ninth-century hierarch who fought the iconoclasts both with the pen and by personal suffering, deposed by Emperor Leo the Armenian and dying in exile after fourteen years of confession.

06-03.title: Martyr Lucillian and those with him
06-03.summary: A converted pagan priest of Byzantium who, with the four young men he had baptized, was crucified for refusing to deny Christ under Aurelian.

06-04.title: St. Metrophanes, first Patriarch of Constantinople
06-04.summary: The fourth-century hierarch who from his deathbed received word of the great victory of the First Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, and reposed soon after in peace.

06-05.title: Hieromartyr Dorotheus, Bishop of Tyre
06-05.summary: A bishop of immense learning who endured exile under Diocletian, returned to his see under Constantine, and was beaten to death under Julian the Apostate at over a hundred years of age.

06-06.title: St. Hilarion the New of Dalmatia
06-06.summary: Abbot of the Dalmatian monastery in Constantinople and a defender of the holy icons under Leo the Armenian, who suffered scourgings and imprisonment for confessing them.

06-07.title: Hieromartyr Theodotus of Ancyra
06-07.summary: The fourth-century bishop of Ancyra in Galatia, beheaded under Maximian for confessing Christ and converting many through his preaching.

06-08.title: Translation of the Relics of Great-martyr Theodore the Tyro (Stratelates)
06-08.summary: The fourth-century soldier-martyr's body, having been returned to his native Euchaita after his passion, was translated with great honor and continued to work signs at his shrine.

06-09.title: St. Cyril, Archbishop of Alexandria
06-09.summary: The fifth-century pillar of Orthodoxy who at the Third Ecumenical Council in Ephesus defended the title Theotokos against Nestorius, and whose treatises remain a foundation of Christology.

06-10.title: Hieromartyr Timothy, Bishop of Prussa
06-10.summary: A fourth-century bishop in Bithynia, beheaded by Julian the Apostate for refusing to recall his rebukes against the imperial relapse into paganism.

06-11.title: Holy Apostles Bartholomew and Barnabas
06-11.summary: Bartholomew, one of the Twelve, preached as far as India and was flayed in Armenia; Barnabas, of the Seventy, was the companion of Paul and is honored as the founder of the Church of Cyprus, where he was stoned.

also.name: St. Barlaam of Khutyn, Wonderworker of Novgorod
Apostle Bartholomew

06-12.title: St. Onuphrius the Great
06-12.summary: The fourth-century desert father of the Egyptian Thebaid who lived sixty years in the wilderness clothed only in his own hair, and whose life was preserved for the Church by Paphnutius. Also commemorated: St. Peter of Athos.

also.name: St. Peter of Mount Athos
also.summary: Ninth-century desert ascetic of Athos, the holy mountain's first known hermit.

06-13.title: Martyr Aquilina of Byblos
06-13.summary: A twelve-year-old maiden of Phoenicia who under Diocletian was tortured for confessing Christ and beheaded after enduring with quiet courage what would have broken many.

06-14.title: Prophet Elisha
06-14.summary: The disciple of Elijah who received a double portion of his spirit; he raised the Shunammite's son, cleansed Naaman the Syrian, and from his very grave brought a dead man back to life.

06-15.title: Prophet Amos
06-15.summary: The eighth-century shepherd of Tekoa whom God called from his flocks to prophesy against the prosperous indifference of Israel and to announce the day when justice would roll down like waters.

06-16.title: St. Tikhon the Wonderworker, Bishop of Amathus
06-16.summary: A fifth-century bishop of Cyprus, beloved by the poor and revered for his gift of healing and the steady wisdom by which he governed his see.

06-17.title: Martyrs Manuel, Sabel, and Ishmael
06-17.summary: Three brothers of Persian noble birth, ambassadors to Constantinople, who under Julian the Apostate refused to share in his pagan sacrifices and were beheaded.

06-18.title: Martyr Leontius
06-18.summary: A Greek soldier of Tripoli in Phoenicia under Vespasian who, with two others he had brought to Christ, was tortured and beheaded for refusing the imperial sacrifice.

06-19.title: Holy Apostle Jude, brother of the Lord
06-19.summary: Author of the Epistle of Jude and one of the Twelve, called the kinsman of the Lord. He preached through Mesopotamia and Persia and met his end by martyrdom in Armenia.

06-20.title: Hieromartyr Methodius, Bishop of Patara
06-20.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop and theologian whose dialogue The Symposium remains one of the earliest Christian works on virginity. He was beheaded for the faith under Maximinus.

06-21.title: Martyr Julian of Tarsus
06-21.summary: A young noble of Cilicia under Diocletian who, after long imprisonment and many torments, was sewn into a sack with vipers and cast into the sea.

06-22.title: Hieromartyr Eusebius, Bishop of Samosata
06-22.summary: A fourth-century defender of Nicene Orthodoxy against Arianism, exiled under Valens and slain by a tile thrown from a rooftop by a heretical woman as he returned to his see.

06-23.title: Martyr Agrippina of Rome
06-23.summary: A young Roman of the third century who under Valerian endured being scourged with iron flails and gave up her soul in confession; her relics were later translated to Sicily.

06-24.title: Nativity of the Honorable Forerunner John the Baptist
06-24.summary: The birth of the Forerunner, foretold by Gabriel to the priest Zacharias in the temple. At his birth, the tongue of his father was loosed in prophecy, and the child grew strong in spirit, awaiting his showing to Israel.

06-25.title: Virgin-martyr Febronia of Nisibis
06-25.summary: A young nun of Nisibis whose teacher had raised her for Christ from childhood, and who in 304 endured the most brutal torments under Diocletian without yielding her vow of virginity.

06-26.title: St. David of Thessalonica
06-26.summary: A sixth-century ascetic who lived three years in the branches of an almond tree outside the walls of Thessalonica, drawing the city to repentance and reposing in peace at his cell.

06-27.title: St. Sampson the Hospitable
06-27.summary: A wealthy physician of Rome who in the sixth century gave away all his goods to open a hospital at Constantinople, healing the bodies of the poor and gently leading their souls to Christ.

06-28.title: Translation of the Relics of the Unmercenaries Cyrus and John
06-28.summary: The fourth-century Egyptian physicians who healed without payment and were beheaded under Diocletian; their relics were brought to Manuthi near Alexandria to drive out the cult of an idol.

06-29.title: Holy and All-Praised Chief Apostles Peter and Paul
06-29.summary: The two coryphaei of the apostolic college, who having labored together for the Gospel were crowned in martyrdom at Rome on the same day under Nero — Peter crucified head-downward, Paul beheaded.

also.name: Paul, Apostle to the Nations

06-30.title: Synaxis of the Holy, Glorious, and All-Praised Twelve Apostles
06-30.summary: The day after the chiefs of the apostles, the Church names together all twelve of those whom the Lord first called to Himself and sent into the world to make of all nations His disciples.

also.name: Apostle Peter
Apostle Andrew the First-Called
Apostle James, son of Zebedee
Apostle John the Theologian
Apostle Philip
Apostle Bartholomew
Apostle Thomas
Apostle Matthew
Apostle James, son of Alphaeus
Apostle Jude, brother of the Lord
Apostle Simon the Zealot
Apostle Matthias

07-01.title: Unmercenaries Cosmas and Damian of Rome
07-01.summary: Brothers and physicians of third-century Rome who healed the sick and refused all payment, declaring that they had received freely and so must freely give. They reposed in peace after long labors.

07-02.title: Placing of the Honorable Robe of the Most Holy Theotokos at Blachernae
07-02.summary: In the fifth century, the robe of the Theotokos was translated from Galilee to Constantinople and enshrined at Blachernae, where it became the city's most powerful protection in every danger.

07-03.title: Martyr Hyacinth of Caesarea in Cappadocia
07-03.summary: A young chamberlain of the emperor Trajan, betrayed by his fellow servants as a Christian, who in prison refused to break his fast even unto death and was crowned at the age of twenty.

07-04.title: St. Andrew of Crete, Archbishop of Crete
07-04.summary: The seventh-century author of the Great Canon read in Great Lent — a masterpiece of penitential poetry that walks the soul through the whole of Scripture in the voice of personal contrition.

also.name: St. Andrew of Crete, Archbishop
also.summary: Hymnographer of the Great Canon.

07-05.title: St. Athanasius of Mount Athos
07-05.summary: The tenth-century founder of the Great Lavra and organizer of cenobitic monasticism on Athos under Nicephorus Phocas, reposed when the dome of his church collapsed upon him as he inspected its construction.

07-06.title: St. Sisoes the Great
07-06.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt whose dying brethren saw him conversing in radiance with the saints and the Lord, asking only that he be given a little more time for repentance.

07-07.title: Great-martyr Kyriake of Nicomedia
07-07.summary: A maiden born to pious parents on the Lord's Day (whence her name), who under Diocletian withstood fire, beasts, and the sword, and gave up her soul in peace at the moment of her execution.

07-08.title: Great-martyr Procopius of Caesarea in Palestine
07-08.summary: An officer of the Roman army who, while marching against the Christians, was met with the vision of a cross of light and converted; under Diocletian he endured prolonged torture and was beheaded.

07-09.title: Hieromartyr Pancratius, Bishop of Tauromenium in Sicily
07-09.summary: A disciple of the apostle Peter, sent as the first bishop of Tauromenium in Sicily, who after years of evangelization was stoned by pagans for casting down their idols.

07-10.title: The Forty-Five Martyrs of Nicopolis in Armenia
07-10.summary: A company of Christians from Nicopolis who in 319 were burned together at the stake by Licinius after enduring the breaking of their legs and many days of starvation.

07-11.title: Holy Equal-to-the-Apostles Great Princess Olga of Kiev
07-11.summary: Princess of Kiev who in her old age traveled to Constantinople and was baptized by the patriarch, taking the name Helen. The first Christian ruler of the Rus', whose grandson Vladimir would complete the conversion of the land she had begun.

also.name: Martyr Euphemia of Chalcedon (commemoration of her confirmation of the Tome at the Fourth Council)

07-12.title: Martyrs Proclus and Hilary
07-12.summary: Uncle and nephew of Callipolis in Asia Minor who under Trajan refused the imperial sacrifices and were beheaded together for the confession of Christ.

also.name: St. Paisios the Athonite
also.summary: Twentieth-century elder of Mount Athos.

07-13.title: Synaxis of the Holy Archangel Gabriel
07-13.summary: The summer feast of the archangel who proclaimed the conception of the Forerunner to Zacharias, the conception of the Lord to the Virgin, and stands ever near the throne of the Most High.

07-14.title: Apostle Aquila of the Seventy
07-14.summary: Together with his wife Priscilla, host of the apostle Paul at Corinth and Ephesus, instructor of Apollos in the way more perfectly, and remembered by Paul as 'fellow workers in Christ Jesus.'

07-15.title: Holy Equal-to-the-Apostles Great Prince Vladimir of Kiev
07-15.summary: Grandson of Olga, baptized at Cherson in 988 and called the Enlightener of the Rus' for bringing the whole nation into the Christian faith. He abolished capital punishment and opened the storehouses of his treasury to the poor.

also.name: Martyrs Cyricus and his mother Julitta
also.summary: A widow of Iconium and her three-year-old son, martyred at Tarsus under Diocletian.

07-16.title: Hieromartyr Athenogenes, Bishop of Heracleopolis
07-16.summary: A fourth-century bishop in Cappadocia who composed the evening hymn 'O Gladsome Light' as he was led to execution under Diocletian, and was beheaded with ten of his disciples.

07-17.title: Great-martyr Marina of Antioch in Pisidia
07-17.summary: A fifteen-year-old who under Diocletian endured monstrous torments and visions of the demon in serpent form, defeating him by the sign of the Cross before being beheaded — the icon of victory over the powers.

also.name: Holy Royal Martyrs of Russia
also.summary: Tsar Nicholas II, Tsarina Alexandra, and their five children, slain at Yekaterinburg in 1918.

07-18.title: Martyr Emilian of Silistria
07-18.summary: A young Christian slave in Thrace who under Julian the Apostate publicly broke the idols of his master's town and, refusing to deny it, was burned at the stake on the banks of the Danube.

also.name: Holy New Martyr Grand Duchess Elizabeth
also.summary: Founder of the Convent of Martha and Mary in Moscow; cast alive into a mine-shaft at Alapayevsk.

07-19.title: St. Macrina, sister of St. Basil the Great
07-19.summary: The eldest sister and spiritual teacher of the great Cappadocian fathers, whose life under her mother Emmelia at Annisi shaped the household from which Basil, Gregory of Nyssa, and Peter of Sebaste came forth.

also.name: St. Seraphim of Sarov
also.summary: Uncovering of his relics in 1903.

07-20.title: Holy and Glorious Prophet Elijah the Tishbite
07-20.summary: The fiery prophet of the ninth century BC who at Carmel called down fire from heaven against the priests of Baal and was taken up alive in a chariot of fire — the herald who is to return before the great Day of the Lord.

07-21.title: Sts. Symeon the Fool-for-Christ and John
07-21.summary: Two sixth-century friends who began as pilgrims at the Jordan and separated for a life of solitude; Symeon returned to Emesa playing the holy fool to hide his virtues, John remained an unknown ascetic.

07-22.title: Holy Myrrh-bearer and Equal-to-the-Apostles Mary Magdalene
07-22.summary: She from whom the Lord cast out seven demons and who stood at the Cross, came first to the empty tomb, and brought the Resurrection news to the apostles. She is the first to whom the risen Christ appeared.

07-23.title: Martyrs Trophimus, Theophilus, and thirteen with them
07-23.summary: Christians of Lycia under Diocletian who, after the breaking of their bones and being cast into prison, were beheaded together for refusing the imperial decree of sacrifice.

07-24.title: Holy Passion-Bearers Boris and Gleb
07-24.summary: Sons of Great Prince Vladimir, who at his death refused to take up arms against their elder brother Sviatopolk for fear of shedding kindred blood. Both were murdered by Sviatopolk's men. The first canonized saints of the Russian Church and the icon of non-resistance to evil.

also.name: Great-martyr Christina of Tyre
also.summary: A noble maiden of Tyre under Diocletian whose three rounds of torture ended in her piercing through with arrows.

07-25.title: Dormition of Righteous Anna, mother of the Most Holy Theotokos
07-25.summary: The repose of the grandmother of the Lord, who with Joachim received the promise of the Virgin in their old age and so became the root from which the Mother of God grew.

07-26.title: Hieromartyr Hermolaus and those with him
07-26.summary: An aged presbyter of Nicomedia who instructed the young Panteleimon in the faith and baptized him; under Maximian he was beheaded with two fellow priests.

07-27.title: Great-martyr and Healer Panteleimon
07-27.summary: A young physician of Nicomedia, converted by his teacher Hermolaus, who healed all who came to him without payment and was beheaded under Maximian — when his blood touched the ground, the olive tree to which he was bound burst into fruit.

07-28.title: Holy Apostles and Deacons Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, and Parmenas
07-28.summary: Four of the seven first deacons named in Acts, ordained by the apostles to serve the daily ministration. Tradition names Prochorus the companion of John the Theologian in his exile at Patmos.

07-29.title: Martyr Callinicus of Cilicia
07-29.summary: A traveling preacher of Christ in Asia Minor under Diocletian, made to walk in iron sandals studded with nails for thirty leagues and then cast into a furnace at Gangra.

07-30.title: Apostles Silas, Silvanus, Crescens, Epenetus, and Andronicus
07-30.summary: Five of the Seventy named in Paul's letters: Silas the prophet, Silvanus the bishop of Thessalonica, Crescens of Galatia, Epenetus 'the firstfruits of Achaia,' and Andronicus 'of note among the apostles.'

07-31.title: Righteous Eudokimos of Cappadocia
07-31.summary: A Cappadocian officer of the ninth century whose holiness was concealed beneath the duties of his rank; his sanctity was revealed only after his repose, when miracles began at his tomb.

08-01.title: Procession of the Honorable Wood of the Life-giving Cross — Seven Maccabee Martyrs
08-01.summary: The Cross is carried in procession at the beginning of the Dormition Fast, recalling its placing for the healing of the sick in old Constantinople. Also commemorated: the seven Maccabee brothers, their mother Solomonia, and their teacher Eleazar.

also.name: The Seven Holy Maccabees, their mother Solomonia, and their teacher Eleazar
Uncovering of the Relics of St. Seraphim of Sarov

08-02.title: Translation of the Relics of the Protomartyr and Archdeacon Stephen
08-02.summary: The body of the first martyr, hidden after his stoning in Jerusalem, was recovered in the fifth century through the vision of the priest Lucian and translated to a church on Mount Zion.

08-03.title: St. Anthony the Roman, Wonderworker of Novgorod
08-03.summary: A twelfth-century Roman ascetic who was miraculously carried on a stone across the seas to the Volkhov River near Novgorod, where he founded a monastery and reposed in peace.

08-04.title: The Seven Holy Youths of Ephesus
08-04.summary: Seven Christian soldiers of Ephesus under Decius who, fleeing persecution, slept hidden in a cave for nearly two centuries and woke in the reign of Theodosius the Younger to confirm the Resurrection of the body to a doubting age.

08-05.title: Forefeast of the Holy Transfiguration; Martyr Eusignius
08-05.summary: The eve of the Transfiguration, when the Church prepares to ascend with the Lord to the mountain. Also commemorated: the soldier Eusignius of Antioch, beheaded under Julian the Apostate at one hundred and ten.

08-06.title: Holy Transfiguration of Our Lord, God, and Saviour Jesus Christ
08-06.summary: Christ leads Peter, James, and John up Mount Tabor and is transfigured before them: His face shines as the sun, His garments become white as light, and Moses and Elijah converse with Him of His coming exodus at Jerusalem. The voice of the Father proclaims, 'This is My beloved Son, hear ye Him.'

08-07.title: Hieromartyr Dometius the Persian
08-07.summary: A fourth-century Persian convert and monk of Nisibis who under Julian the Apostate, refusing to come out of his cave, was stoned to death together with two of his disciples.

08-08.title: St. Emilian the Confessor, Bishop of Cyzicus
08-08.summary: A ninth-century hierarch who at the Constantinopolitan synod of 815 refused to subscribe to the iconoclast decree and was driven from his see into a long exile.

08-09.title: Holy Apostle Matthias
08-09.summary: Chosen by lot to fill the place of Judas among the Twelve, he preached the Gospel in Judea, Cappadocia, and on the shores of the Caspian Sea, and was stoned and beheaded for the faith.

also.name: Glorification of St. Herman of Alaska, Wonderworker of America
also.summary: Monk of Valaam who came with the first Orthodox mission to Alaska in 1794.

08-10.title: Hieromartyr and Archdeacon Lawrence of Rome
08-10.summary: The archdeacon of Pope Sixtus II at Rome under Valerian; entrusted with the goods of the Church, he distributed them to the poor and presented those poor to the prefect as the true treasure of the Church, for which he was roasted alive on a gridiron.

08-11.title: Martyr Euplus the Deacon of Catania
08-11.summary: A deacon of Catania in Sicily under Diocletian who carried the book of the Gospels openly through the streets and, when arrested, was beheaded with that very book pressed to his breast.

08-12.title: Martyrs Photius and Anicetus of Nicomedia
08-12.summary: An uncle and his nephew at Nicomedia under Diocletian who, declaring Christ before the emperor's tribunal, were cast into a furnace and crowned together.

08-13.title: St. Tikhon, Bishop of Voronezh and Zadonsk
08-13.summary: An eighteenth-century Russian hierarch and spiritual writer whose simple counsel — 'be silent, and let God act' — formed generations of Russian piety. His relics rest at Zadonsk.

08-14.title: Forefeast of the Dormition; Holy Prophet Micah
08-14.summary: The eve of the Dormition. Also commemorated: Micah of Morasthi in the eighth century BC, who foretold the birthplace of the Messiah at Bethlehem and said what the Lord requires of man.

08-15.title: The Dormition of Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary
08-15.summary: The end of the earthly life of the Mother of God. The apostles, gathered miraculously to her bedside, accompanied her body to Gethsemane; on the third day, when Thomas came, the tomb was found empty, for she had been taken up by her Son to the place He had prepared.

08-16.title: Translation of the Image of Christ Not-Made-by-Hands from Edessa
08-16.summary: The cloth on which the Lord wiped His face and which bore His image, sent by Him to King Abgar of Edessa and translated in 944 from Edessa to Constantinople — the first icon of Christ, made by His own act.

08-17.title: Martyr Myron of Cyzicus
08-17.summary: A presbyter of Achaia under Decius who, finding pagans seizing a Christian congregation on the Nativity day, defended his flock and was tortured to death with peculiar cruelty.

08-18.title: Martyrs Florus and Laurus of Illyria
08-18.summary: Twin stonemasons of the second century, commissioned to build a pagan temple in Illyria; they made of it a church instead, and were cast with their fellow workers into a deep well for confession of the Cross.

08-19.title: Martyr Andrew Stratelates and 2593 with him
08-19.summary: A Roman general in Cilicia under Maximian who, after a great victory attributed to Christ, was baptized with the soldiers of his command and, denounced as Christians, were beheaded together in the passes of the Taurus.

08-20.title: Holy Prophet Samuel
08-20.summary: The last of the judges and the first of the prophets in the regular line, dedicated by his mother Hannah from the womb, who anointed both Saul and David and judged Israel through long faithful years.

08-21.title: Holy Apostle Thaddaeus of the Seventy
08-21.summary: One of the Seventy, sometimes identified with Judas the brother of the Lord. He brought the image of the Lord to King Abgar at Edessa, baptized the king with his household, and reposed in peace at Beirut.

08-22.title: Martyr Agathonicus of Nicomedia
08-22.summary: A Greek of Nicomedia who under Maximian led many of his fellow citizens to Christ before being beheaded in Thrace together with several of those he had taught.

08-23.title: Leavetaking of the Dormition; Hieromartyr Irenaeus of Lyons
08-23.summary: The closing day of the eight-day feast of the Dormition. Also commemorated: the second-century bishop and disciple of Polycarp who from Gaul defended the apostolic tradition against the gnostics.

also.name: Hieromartyr Irenaeus of Lyons
also.summary: Second-century bishop and disciple of Polycarp who from Gaul defended the apostolic tradition against the gnostics.

08-24.title: Hieromartyr Eutyches, disciple of John the Theologian
08-24.summary: A hearer of the apostle John and afterwards of Paul, who confessed the faith with such constancy that even hunger, lions, and the rack would not silence him; he reposed at last in peace.

08-25.title: Return of the Relics of the Apostle Bartholomew; Apostle Titus of the Seventy
08-25.summary: The body of Bartholomew, cast into the sea, came in time to rest at Beneventum. Also commemorated: Titus, fellow worker of Paul and first bishop of the Cretans, to whom one of Paul's letters is addressed.

08-26.title: Martyrs Adrian and Natalia
08-26.summary: An imperial officer at Nicomedia under Maximian, converted by the witness of the Christians he was assigned to register, and his young wife who supported him through every torment until he was crowned.

08-27.title: St. Poemen the Great
08-27.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt whose sayings, gathered in the Apophthegmata, distill the wisdom of the cell: 'A man teaches his neighbor by example, not by speech.'

08-28.title: St. Moses the Black of Scetis
08-28.summary: A formerly Ethiopian highway robber who in repentance fled to the Scetis desert, struggled long under the guidance of Macarius, and became a priest whose meekness drew his own former companions to Christ.

also.name: St. Moses the Black of Scetis

08-29.title: The Beheading of the Honorable Forerunner and Baptist John
08-29.summary: Herod, having taken his brother's wife Herodias, was reproved by John; at her daughter's dance and the king's rash oath, the Forerunner's head was given on a platter — the Friend of the Bridegroom slain for proclaiming the law of God. A strict fast.

08-30.title: Sts. Alexander, John, and Paul the New, Patriarchs of Constantinople
08-30.summary: Three hierarchs of Constantinople, all defenders of Orthodoxy: Alexander against Arius at Nicaea; John (Chrysostom's successor of like name) against the Origenists; Paul the New against iconoclasm.

08-31.title: Placing of the Honorable Sash of the Theotokos in Constantinople
08-31.summary: The sash worn by the Mother of God, brought from Jerusalem and laid up in the church of Chalcoprateia at Constantinople in the reign of Arcadius, where it healed multitudes.

09-01.title: Indiction — Beginning of the Church Year; St. Symeon the Stylite
09-01.summary: The first day of the ecclesiastical year, blessed in the church and asking God's mercy upon the year to come. Also commemorated: the fifth-century Syrian ascetic who lived thirty-seven years atop a pillar at Telanissos, drawing the world up to Christ from on high.

also.name: St. Symeon the Stylite the Elder
Indiction: Beginning of the Church Year
Righteous Joshua the son of Nun
also.summary: Successor of Moses who led Israel across the Jordan into the promised land.

also.name: Martyrs Callista, Evodus, and Hermogenes of Nicomedia
also.summary: Three siblings beheaded together at Nicomedia in the early fourth century.

also.name: Hieromartyr Ammon the Deacon and the Forty Holy Virgin-martyrs of Heraclea
also.summary: A deacon and forty maidens of Heraclea in Thrace whose teacher he was; all crowned together under Licinius.

also.name: Martyr Aeithalas the Deacon of Persia
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian deacon who chose death rather than worship the sun.

also.name: Venerable Martha of Cappadocia
also.summary: Mother of Symeon the Stylite, who reposed in peace at Antioch in 428.

also.name: Venerable Evanthia
St. Dionysius Exiguus
also.summary: Sixth-century Scythian monk at Rome whose calculations gave the Church its reckoning of the years of the Lord.

also.name: St. Symeon Stylites of Lesbos
also.summary: A ninth-century stylite of Lesbos who continued in his own land the asceticism of his Syrian namesake.

also.name: Day of Prayer for God's Creation
also.summary: A modern observance kept on the first day of the church year for the renewal of the created order.

09-02.title: Martyr Mamas of Caesarea
09-02.summary: An orphaned shepherd-boy of Cappadocia in the third century whose lambs and wild animals lived together at his feet; under Aurelian he was given to the beasts and finally pierced through with a trident.

also.name: Righteous Eleazar and Phineas
also.summary: Aaron's son and grandson, of the priestly line that succeeded him at the altar of the Lord.

also.name: Martyrs Diomedes, Julian, Philip, Eutychianos, Hesychius, Leonides, Eutychios, Philadelphos, Melanippos, and Parthagape of Nicomedia
also.summary: Ten witnesses of Christ at Nicomedia under Diocletian, put to death each by his own torment — fire, water, sword, gridiron, and stone.

also.name: Martyrs Aeithalas and Ammon of Adrianople
also.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Thrace scourged to death together for the name of Christ.

also.name: St. John IV the Faster, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: The strict ascetic-patriarch of Constantinople from 582 to 595, the first to bear the title Ecumenical, who reposed leaving only a wooden spoon.

also.name: Venerable Kosmas the Hermit
also.summary: A seventh-century Cretan ascetic confessor remembered for his solitude and life of prayer.

09-03.title: Hieromartyr Anthimus, Bishop of Nicomedia
09-03.summary: The bishop of Nicomedia at the time of Diocletian's great persecution who, after the destruction of his cathedral, was found in hiding and beheaded together with many of his flock.

also.name: Deaconess Phoebe of Cenchreae
also.summary: The servant of the church at Cenchreae near Corinth whom the apostle Paul commended to the Romans at the close of his epistle.

also.name: Hieromartyr Aristion, Bishop of Alexandria Scabiosa
also.summary: A second-century bishop of Alexandria Scabiosa in Cilicia, witness of Christ in the days of the apostolic fathers.

also.name: Hieromartyr Theophilus the Deacon
also.summary: A deacon of Nicomedia who suffered together with St. Anthimus and many of the great Nicomedian church.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Basilissa of Nicomedia
also.summary: A nine-year-old girl of Nicomedia who endured fire and the beasts unharmed in 309 and at length reposed in peace at her prayers.

also.name: Martyrs Zenon, Chariton, and Archontius
Venerable Theoctistus of Palestine
also.summary: A fifth-century anchorite of the Judean desert and companion of Euthymius the Great, abbot of the first lavra they founded together.

also.name: St. Constantine III, Emperor of New Rome
also.summary: Seventh-century Byzantine emperor and son of Heraclius, remembered for his piety and brief reign.

also.name: St. Tervel, Khan of Bulgaria
also.summary: The early-eighth-century Bulgarian khan baptized into the faith who came to the defense of Constantinople against the Arab siege of 718.

09-04.title: Hieromartyr Babylas of Antioch; Prophet Moses the God-Seer
09-04.summary: The third-century bishop of Antioch who barred Emperor Philip from entering the church until he repented. Also commemorated: Moses, who led Israel from Egypt and received the Law on Sinai face to face with God.

also.name: Holy Prophet Moses the God-Seer
also.summary: Leader of Israel out of Egypt, who received the Law on Sinai face to face with God.

also.name: Martyrs Thathuil and Bebaia of Edessa
also.summary: Brother and sister of second-century Edessa martyred together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Sarbellus
also.summary: An early witness of Edessa stoned for confessing Christ.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Hermione of Ephesus
also.summary: A daughter of the apostle Philip the Deacon, who reposed at Ephesus after long labors of healing and prophecy.

also.name: Saints Theotimos and Theodoulos of Ephesus
also.summary: Two executioners converted by St. Hermione who themselves received the crown of martyrdom.

also.name: St. Petronius of Ephesus
also.summary: A second-century disciple of John the Theologian who reposed at Ephesus where his teacher had labored.

also.name: Martyr Christodoula and her three sons
also.summary: A mother of the third century who suffered together with her three children for the name of Christ.

also.name: The Three Thousand Six Hundred and Twenty-Eight Martyrs of Nicomedia
also.summary: A vast company of believers slain together at Nicomedia under Diocletian.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Charitina of Amisus
also.summary: A virgin of fourth-century Amisus on the Black Sea coast who suffered under Diocletian for her confession.

also.name: Venerable Petronius of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century disciple of Pachomius the Great in the cenobia of upper Egypt.

09-05.title: Holy Prophet Zechariah, father of John the Baptist
09-05.summary: The aged priest in the temple to whom Gabriel announced the conception of the Forerunner, slain by Herod's soldiers between the temple and the altar when he would not betray the hidden child.

also.name: Righteous Elizabeth, mother of John the Forerunner
also.summary: The kinswoman of the Theotokos whose long barrenness God remembered, who took her child into the wilderness to save him from Herod's sword.

also.name: Martyrs Sarbel and Barbe of Edessa
also.summary: A second-century brother and sister of Edessa who shared one confession and one martyrdom.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Rhais of Alexandria
also.summary: A maiden of fourth-century Alexandria who suffered under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Juventinus and Maximinus of Antioch
also.summary: Two soldiers in the imperial guard at Antioch beheaded under Julian the Apostate for refusing to eat what had been offered to idols.

also.name: Martyrs Urban, Theodore, Medimnus, and the seventy-seven priests and deacons of Nicomedia
also.summary: A company of clergy of Nicomedia under the Arian Valens, burned alive in the ship that bore them into exile.

also.name: Hieromartyr Abdas, Bishop in Persia
also.summary: A fifth-century Persian bishop whose destruction of a fire-temple drew down the wrath of Sapor II and his own martyrdom.

09-06.title: Commemoration of the Miracle of the Archangel Michael at Chonae
09-06.summary: When pagans turned a river to flood the church and shrine of the Archangel at Chonae in Phrygia, the deacon Archippus prayed; the Archangel struck the rock with his staff and the waters were swallowed up.

also.name: Venerable Archippus the Deacon of Hierapolis
also.summary: The deacon whose prayer at Chonae moved the Archangel to deliver the church from the rising flood.

also.name: Martyrs Romulus and the eleven thousand soldiers with him
also.summary: A Roman officer in early-second-century Armenia who confessed Christ with eleven thousand of his men under Trajan.

also.name: Martyrs Cyriacus, Faustus, Abibus, and ten others of Alexandria
also.summary: A company of Alexandrian Christians beheaded together for the name of Christ around the year 250.

also.name: Hieromartyr Cyril, Bishop of Gortyna in Crete
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Gortyna who, having confessed under Diocletian and survived, was at length beheaded in old age.

also.name: Martyrs Eudoxius, Zeno, Macarius, and the one thousand one hundred and four soldiers with them
also.summary: A company of Christian soldiers at Melitene of Armenia who in the fourth century forfeited honor and life rather than offer sacrifice.

also.name: Venerable David of Hermopolis
also.summary: A sixth-century desert father in upper Egypt who from being a brigand was converted to long penance and a life of prayer.

09-07.title: Forefeast of the Nativity of the Theotokos; Martyr Sozon of Cilicia
09-07.summary: The eve of the Nativity of the Mother of God. Also commemorated: Sozon, a shepherd-boy of Cilicia who broke a great golden idol and gave its fragments to the poor, and under Maximian gave up his soul in the flames.

also.name: Apostles Evodius and Onesiphorus of the Seventy
also.summary: Two of the Seventy: Evodius the second bishop of Antioch after Peter, and Onesiphorus whom Paul names in the second letter to Timothy.

also.name: Martyr Eupsychios of Caesarea
also.summary: A young man of second-century Caesarea in Cappadocia tortured and beheaded under Hadrian for confessing Christ.

also.name: Pope Stephen I of Rome and companions
also.summary: The third-century bishop of Rome martyred in the persecution of Valerian, slain seated in his own chair at the celebration of the Liturgy.

also.name: Venerable Kassiani the Hymnographer
also.summary: A ninth-century noblewoman of Constantinople who, refused the imperial throne by Theophilus, entered a convent and composed many of the Church's hymns.

also.name: Venerable Peter the Cappadocian, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
also.summary: A tenth-century abbot of the monastery near Triglia in Bithynia who succeeded its founder.

also.name: Venerable Luke of Lycaonia, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
also.summary: Third abbot of the same Bithynian monastery, who reposed after the year 975.

09-08.title: The Nativity of Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary
09-08.summary: The birth of the Mother of God to the aged Joachim and Anna of Nazareth, the answer to long prayer and the breaking of barrenness. Today the gate is built through which the Sun of righteousness will come into the world.

also.name: Martyrs Rufus and Rufianus
Martyrs Severus and Artemidorus
Martyrs Eusebius, Nestablus, Zeno, and Nestor
also.summary: Four believers of Gaza torn to pieces by the pagan mob in 362 under Julian the Apostate.

09-09.title: Sts. Joachim and Anna, the Holy God-bearing Ancestors of God
09-09.summary: The righteous parents of the Theotokos, whose long faithfulness in barrenness God remembered. They are honored on the day after their daughter's birth as the root and ground of her holiness.

also.name: St. Anna, mother of the Theotokos
Venerable Theophanes the Confessor and Faster of Mount Diabenos
also.summary: An ascetic of the third century renowned for the severity of his fasting and his confession of Christ before the persecutors.

also.name: Martyr Severian of Sebaste
also.summary: A senator of fourth-century Sebaste who visited the Forty Martyrs in prison and was stretched on the rack and stoned for his confession.

also.name: St. Rufus of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Bishop of Thessaloniki in the early fifth century, a defender of orthodoxy in the wake of the Third Ecumenical Council.

09-10.title: Martyrs Menodora, Metrodora, and Nymphodora
09-10.summary: Three virgin sisters of Bithynia of the early fourth century, ascetics in the wilderness who were brought before Frontonus and one by one tortured to death for their virginity and confession.

also.name: Apostles Apelles, Lucius, and Clement of the Seventy
also.summary: Three of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles: Apelles bishop of Heraclea, Lucius bishop of Laodicea in Syria, and Clement of Sardis.

also.name: Hieromartyr Barypsabas of Dalmatia
also.summary: A hermit of Dalmatia in the second century slain by robbers for the vial of the Lord's blood entrusted to his keeping.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Ia of Persia and the nine thousand with her
also.summary: A captive of fourth-century Persia who under Sapor II led many to Christ and was at length beheaded with thousands of converts.

also.name: Empress Pulcheria of New Rome
also.summary: Fifth-century empress and sister of Theodosius II, defender of orthodoxy against Nestorius and convener of the Fourth Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Saints Peter and Paul, Bishops of Nicaea
also.summary: Two ninth-century bishops of Nicaea who together steered the church of that city in the years following the Seventh Council.

09-11.title: St. Theodora of Alexandria
09-11.summary: A fifth-century noblewoman who in repentance for a single sin disguised herself as a man and lived hidden as a monk in a male monastery for many years, her secret revealed only at her repose.

also.name: Martyrs Demetrius, Evanthia, and Demetrian of Skepsis
also.summary: A first-century household of Skepsis on the Hellespont — father, mother, and son — who suffered together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Diodorus, Didymus, and Diomedes of Laodicea
also.summary: Three companions martyred at Laodicea under Julian the Apostate in the middle of the fourth century.

also.name: Venerable Paphnutius the Confessor of Egypt
also.summary: A confessor of the Egyptian Thebaid imprisoned in the mines under Diocletian; the eyeless witness who at Nicaea stood for the marriage of the clergy.

also.name: Venerable Euphrosynus the Cook
also.summary: A humble ninth-century cook in a monastery of Palestine whose hidden holiness was revealed in a vision granted to one of the elders.

also.name: Venerable Elias the Cave-dweller of Calabria
also.summary: A tenth-century hermit of Calabria in southern Italy who passed his days in a cave with his spiritual father Arsenius.

also.name: Venerable-martyr Theodora of Vasta
also.summary: A maiden of tenth-century Peloponnese who lived disguised as a soldier and at her death was buried where a chapel and a stand of trees still grow upon her grave.

09-12.title: Hieromartyr Autonomus, Bishop in Italy
09-12.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Italy who fled the persecution of Diocletian to Bithynia, founded a church there, and was slain at the altar by pagans of Soreoi after celebrating the Liturgy.

also.name: Leavetaking of the Nativity of the Theotokos
also.summary: The closing day of the eight-day feast that began on the eighth of September.

also.name: Hieromartyr Coronatus, Bishop of Iconium
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Iconium in Lycaonia beheaded under the governor Perennius.

also.name: Martyrs Theodore and Oceanus
Martyrs Macedonius, Tatian, and Theodulus of Phrygia
also.summary: Three Phrygian Christians who under Julian the Apostate were laid on burning gridirons for their refusal of the sacrifices.

also.name: Martyr Julian of Galatia and forty companions
also.summary: A Galatian Christian beheaded with forty of his fellows in the persecution under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Venerable Daniel of Thasos
Venerable Andronicus of Atroa
also.summary: A ninth-century monk of Bithynia in the days of the iconoclast emperors.

09-13.title: Commemoration of the Dedication of the Church of the Resurrection at Jerusalem; Forefeast of the Cross
09-13.summary: The great basilica built by Constantine over the Tomb of the Lord at Jerusalem, dedicated in 335 on this day; the next day the Cross is lifted up over the gathered Church.

also.name: Hieromartyr Cornelius the Centurion
also.summary: The Roman captain at Caesarea baptized by Peter — the first Gentile received into the Church — who reposed bishop of his city.

also.name: Martyr Aristides the Athenian
also.summary: A philosopher of second-century Athens whose apology for the faith addressed to Hadrian is the earliest extant.

also.name: Martyr Straton of Nicomedia
Martyr Seleucus of Galatia
Martyrs Elias, Zoticus, Lucian, Valerian, Macrobius, and Gordian of Tomis
also.summary: Six companions martyred together at Tomis on the Black Sea coast under Licinius.

also.name: St. Eulogius I, Patriarch of Alexandria
also.summary: Sixth- and seventh-century patriarch of Alexandria, friend and correspondent of Pope Gregory the Great.

also.name: Venerable Peter of Atroa
also.summary: A monastic father of ninth-century Bithynia who suffered under the second iconoclasm.

09-14.title: The Universal Exaltation of the Precious and Life-giving Cross
09-14.summary: The discovery of the Cross by St. Helena in Jerusalem and its solemn lifting up by the patriarch Macarius for the whole people to behold. A day of strict fasting in honor of the Lord's Passion.

also.name: Repose of St. John Chrysostom
also.summary: His memorial is transferred from the day of his death (407) to November 13, because today belongs entirely to the Cross.

also.name: Martyr Papas of Lycaonia
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Lycaonia tortured and nailed to a tree for his confession.

also.name: Martyrs Theocles and Valerianus
St. Placilla, Empress of New Rome
also.summary: Wife of Theodosius the Great, remembered by Gregory of Nyssa for her almsgiving and personal service to the sick.

also.name: St. Maria of Tarsus

09-15.title: Great-martyr Nicetas the Goth
09-15.summary: A fourth-century convert of the Gothic nation, baptized by Theophilus of the Goths, who was burned alive by his pagan kinsman Athanaric for refusing to abandon Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Maximus, Theodotus, and Asclepiodota of Adrianopolis
Martyr Porphyrius the Mime
also.summary: An actor at Caesarea who in mocking Christian baptism on stage was himself touched by grace, confessed Christ, and was beheaded under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Venerable Joseph, Abbot of Alaverdi
also.summary: A sixth-century Syrian father of Georgian monasticism, one of the Thirteen Holy Fathers who came from Antioch into Iberia.

also.name: Venerable Philotheos the Presbyter, Wonderworker of Asia
also.summary: A tenth-century priest of Asia Minor known for his many miracles and his peace under unjust suffering.

09-16.title: Great-martyr Euphemia the All-praised
09-16.summary: The fourth-century maiden of Chalcedon, tortured with peculiar cruelty under Diocletian, whose tomb in 451 would later receive and confirm the Tome of the Fathers at the Fourth Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Martyr Sebastiana of Heraclea
also.summary: A first-century disciple of the apostle Paul beheaded at Heraclea in Thrace under Domitian.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Melissa of Marcianopolis
Martyrs Victor and Sosthenes of Chalcedon
also.summary: Two soldiers at Chalcedon converted by the courage of St. Euphemia who shared in her crown.

also.name: Venerable Dorotheus the Hermit of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of the Thebaid known for his unbroken silence and ceaseless prayer.

also.name: Martyrs Isaac and Joseph of Karnu
also.summary: Two believers of ninth-century Georgia put to death at Tiflis by the Saracens for refusing Islam.

also.name: Martyr Ludmilla of Bohemia
also.summary: Tenth-century grandmother of Wenceslaus of Bohemia, strangled at her prayers by her pagan daughter-in-law's assassins.

09-17.title: Martyrs Sophia and her three daughters Faith, Hope, and Love
09-17.summary: A widow of Rome under Hadrian who, having raised her three young daughters in Christ, watched them tortured one by one; the children went bravely to their crowns, and three days later their mother gave up her soul at their grave.

also.name: Martyr Faith
Martyr Hope
Martyr Love
Sophia and her Daughters Faith, Hope, and Love
Hieromartyrs Heraclides and Myron, Bishops of Tamassos in Cyprus
also.summary: Two first-century bishops of Cyprus consecrated by the apostle Paul who at last received the crown by fire.

also.name: Martyr Agathocleia
also.summary: A maidservant of the third century, scourged and her tongue cut out for refusing her pagan mistress; given over to the fire.

also.name: Martyr Theodota of Nicaea
Martyrs Lucy and her son Geminian of Rome
also.summary: A widow of Rome and her son beheaded together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: The One Hundred and Fifty-six Martyrs of Palestine
also.summary: A great company of believers — bishops, clergy, monastics, and laity — slain in Palestine in the persecution of Maximinus Daia.

09-18.title: St. Eumenius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Gortyna
09-18.summary: A bishop of Crete in the seventh century, a gentle ascetic and worker of miracles, who reposed at Thebes after a journey to support persecuted brethren.

also.name: Martyr Ariadne of Phrygia
also.summary: A maidservant of second-century Phrygia who fled into the mountains rather than join her master's pagan rites and was hidden in a cleft of the rock.

also.name: Martyrs Sophia and Irene of Egypt
Martyr Castor of Alexandria

09-19.title: Martyrs Trophimus, Sabbatius, and Dorymedon
09-19.summary: Three Christians of Antioch under Probus who refused the pagan sacrifices at Daphne; Sabbatius died under the lash, Trophimus and Dorymedon were beheaded together.

also.name: Martyr Zosimas the Hermit of Cilicia
also.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of the wilderness of Cilicia who suffered under the pagan persecutors.

09-20.title: Great-martyr Eustathius Placidas, his wife Theopiste, and his children
09-20.summary: A Roman general under Trajan converted by the vision of a Cross between the antlers of a stag on the hunt; reduced to poverty, separated from his family, then restored to command, he and his household were finally roasted alive in a brazen bull.

also.name: Great-martyr Eustathius Placidas
Martyrs Theopiste, Agapius, and Theopistus
also.summary: Wife and two sons of Eustathius, given over to the fire with him in the brazen ox at Rome.

also.name: Martyrs Artemidorus and Thallos
Martyr John the Confessor and the forty companions with him
also.summary: A confessor of Egypt or Palestine in the late third century beheaded together with forty of his fellows.

also.name: Saints Theodore, Euprepius, and the two Anastasii, disciples of Maximus the Confessor
also.summary: Seventh-century confessors who shared with St. Maximus his last sufferings under the monothelite emperor Constans II.

also.name: Hieromartyr Hypatius, Bishop of Ephesus, and Andrew the Presbyter
also.summary: A bishop of Ephesus and his priest, confessors of the holy icons under Leo III the Isaurian in the eighth century.

also.name: Venerable John the God-bearer of Crete
also.summary: An eleventh-century desert monk of Crete who reposed in a cave at Cape Akrotiri.

also.name: Venerable Meletius, Bishop of Cyprus

09-21.title: Leavetaking of the Exaltation of the Cross; Apostle Quadratus of the Seventy
09-21.summary: The closing day of the eight-day feast of the Cross. Also commemorated: Quadratus, who under Hadrian addressed his apology for the faith to the emperor and reposed bishop of Athens.

also.name: Holy Prophet Jonah
also.summary: The eighth-century prophet sent to Nineveh who in three days in the belly of the great fish prefigured the Lord's burial and rising.

also.name: Martyr Eusebius of Phoenicia
also.summary: A second-century believer of Phoenicia who of his own will came forward and confessed Christ before the judge.

also.name: Six Holy Martyrs at Melitene
Martyrs Eusebius, Nestabus, Zeno, Nestor, and Busiris of Gaza
also.summary: Three brothers and two companions of Gaza torn to pieces by the pagan mob under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Martyr Bassa of Tyre
Venerable Jonah the Sabbaite
also.summary: Ninth-century presbyter of Mar Saba in Palestine, father of the hymnographers Theodore and Theophanes the Branded.

also.name: Venerables Isaacius and Meletius, Bishops in Cyprus

09-22.title: Hieromartyr Phocas of Sinope
09-22.summary: A bishop of Sinope in the second century who, hearing that soldiers had come to seize him, dug his own grave the night before, set them a meal in his house, and at dawn revealed himself to be the man they sought.

also.name: Prophet Jonah
Martyr Phocas the Gardener of Sinope
also.summary: A pious gardener of fourth-century Sinope who fed his executioners at his table the night before going freely to his beheading.

also.name: Martyrs Isaac and Martin
St. Peter of Constantinople, the former Tax-Collector
also.summary: A hard-hearted publican of sixth-century Africa who, struck by a vision of his deeds weighed against a single loaf, gave away all his goods and lived hidden as a slave.

09-23.title: Conception of the Honorable Forerunner John the Baptist
09-23.summary: The conception of John, foretold by Gabriel to Zacharias in the temple six months before the conception of the Lord. The angel went forth from the holy place and the aged barrenness was undone.

also.name: Saints Xanthippe and Polyxena of Spain
also.summary: Two sisters of first-century Spain, disciples of the apostles, who labored in evangelism in the days of the apostolic preaching.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Irais of Alexandria
also.summary: A maiden of Alexandria beheaded under Diocletian after long torture for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Andrew, John, Peter, and Antoninus of Syracuse
also.summary: Four believers of Sicily — a father, his son, and two companions — martyred in Africa by the Saracens in the ninth century.

09-24.title: Protomartyr and Equal-to-the-Apostles Thecla
09-24.summary: A noblewoman of Iconium converted by the apostle Paul whose witness she followed through fire, beasts, and exile; she labored as an evangelist and reposed in old age in a cave at Seleucia.

also.name: Repose of St. Silouan the Athonite
also.summary: Russian monk of the Athonite Russikon (1866–1938) whose hidden grief for the world and prayer for all sons of Adam gave the modern Church a fresh witness to ceaseless prayer.

also.name: Saint Persa
also.summary: A holy woman commemorated together with Thecla in the Synaxarion.

also.name: Venerable Coprius of Palestine
also.summary: A sixth-century monk of Palestine raised from infancy in the wilderness, who reposed in peace at his lavra.

09-25.title: St. Euphrosyne of Alexandria; Repose of St. Sergius of Radonezh
09-25.summary: Euphrosyne of fifth-century Alexandria, who disguised as a young monk lived thirty-eight years in a male monastery beside the father who searched for her. Also commemorated: the great fourteenth-century abbot of Russia, builder of the Holy Trinity Lavra.

also.name: Repose of St. Sergius of Radonezh, Abbot of Holy Trinity Lavra
also.summary: Fourteenth-century father of Russian monasticism and builder of the Holy Trinity Lavra north of Moscow.

also.name: Monk-martyr Paphnutius of Egypt and the five hundred and forty-six with him
also.summary: An Egyptian father of the early fourth century who under Diocletian led many hundreds of his fellow Christians together to martyrdom.

also.name: Martyrs Paul and Tatta of Damascus and their five children
also.summary: A household of fourth-century Damascus — parents and five children — all crowned together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Paphnutius of Alexandria
also.summary: Father of Euphrosyne the Younger of Alexandria, who at her revelation passed his last years a monk in the cell that had been hers.

also.name: Venerable Theophilos the Confessor, Archbishop of Ephesus
also.summary: A confessor of the icons under the second iconoclasm and shepherd of the Ephesian church in the ninth century.

also.name: St. Arsen the Great, Catholicos of Georgia
also.summary: A ninth-century catholicos of the Georgian church remembered for his learning, his hymnody, and his peace with his suffering people.

09-26.title: Repose of the Holy Apostle and Evangelist John the Theologian
09-26.summary: The disciple whom Jesus loved, who at Ephesus alone of the apostles was not slain but laid himself down in his grave and was taken — and from his tomb each year a fine dust ascended which healed the sick.

also.name: Righteous Gideon, Judge of Israel
also.summary: The judge who delivered Israel from Midian with three hundred men and whose fleece bedewed with the morning dew is read in the Church as a figure of the Virgin.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Thekla, Mariamne, Martha, Mary, and Ennatha of Persia
also.summary: Five Persian nuns beheaded together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of fire.

also.name: Martyr Kyra

09-27.title: Martyr Callistratus and the forty-nine soldiers with him
09-27.summary: A Christian soldier in third-century Rome whose unmoved patience under torture converted forty-nine of his comrades; they were sewn into sacks and cast into the sea, and the sacks broke open at the shore.

also.name: Apostles Mark, Aristarchus, and Zenas of the Seventy
also.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul: Mark (also called John) of Bibloupolis in Phoenicia, Aristarchus of Thessalonica, and Zenas the lawyer of Diospolis.

also.name: Martyrs Epicharis and Gaiana of Rome
also.summary: A senator's wife and her companion at Rome beheaded under Diocletian after long torture for their confession of Christ.

also.name: Hieromartyr Philemon and Martyr Fortunatus
also.summary: A bishop and his companion crowned together for the faith.

also.name: Fifteen Martyrs cast into the Sea
St. Flavian I, Archbishop of Antioch
also.summary: Late-fourth-century archbishop of Antioch and friend of John Chrysostom, defender of the Antiochene see against Arian intrusion.

also.name: Venerable Ignatius, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
also.summary: Tenth-century abbot of the Bithynian monastery near Triglia who succeeded Peter and Luke in its leadership.

09-28.title: St. Chariton the Confessor
09-28.summary: A confessor under Aurelian who survived his tortures, settled in the Judean wilderness, and founded the first lavra at Pharan beyond Jericho — the father of Palestinian monasticism.

also.name: Holy Prophet Baruch
also.summary: Disciple and scribe of the prophet Jeremiah, who recorded the prophet's words and was led with him into exile in Egypt.

also.name: Martyrs Eustathius and Callinicus of Rome
Martyrs Alexander, Alphius, Zosimas, and twenty-seven companions
also.summary: Three brothers of fourth-century Pisidia and a great company put to death together under the pagan persecutors.

also.name: St. Alkison, Bishop of Nicopolis
also.summary: A bishop of sixth-century Nicopolis in Epirus, defender of the Council of Chalcedon against the Monophysite party.

also.name: St. Wenceslaus, Prince of Bohemia
also.summary: Tenth-century prince of Bohemia raised by his grandmother Ludmilla in the faith, treacherously slain at the church door by his brother Boleslaus.

09-29.title: St. Cyriacus the Hermit
09-29.summary: A sixth-century desert father of Palestine, disciple of Euthymius and abbot at the Souka lavra, whose counsel guided many through the Origenist controversies of his day.

also.name: The Eighty Holy Martyrs of Byzantium
also.summary: Eighty priests and clergy of fourth-century Constantinople burned alive in the ship that bore them into exile by the Arian Valens.

also.name: Martyrs Tryphon, Trophimus, Dorymedon, and the one hundred and fifty with them
also.summary: A great company of Christians of Palestine slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Theodota of Philippi
Martyr Petronia
Martyr Gudelia of Persia
also.summary: A noblewoman of fourth-century Persia who under Sapor II refused the worship of the sun and brought many of her household to Christ before being flayed alive.

also.name: Martyrs Dada, Gabdelas, and Casdoe of Persia
also.summary: Kinsmen of King Sapor II of Persia whose conversion to Christ brought them to torment and death at his hand.

also.name: Venerable Theophanes the Merciful of Gaza
also.summary: A holy man of Gaza known for spending all his substance upon the poor and for sheltering the homeless under his own roof.

09-30.title: Hieromartyr Gregory the Illuminator of Armenia
09-30.summary: The fourth-century apostle of Armenia, who having endured fourteen years in a pit at Ardashes was raised to baptize King Tiridates and bring the whole nation to the faith of Christ.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Rhipsima, Gaiana, and their thirty-five companions
also.summary: A Roman noblewoman who with her abbess and companions fled to Armenia from Diocletian and were beheaded there by Tiridates before his own conversion.

also.name: Martyrs Stratonicus and Mardonius

10-01.title: The Protection of Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary
10-01.summary: St. Andrew the Fool-for-Christ and his disciple Epiphanius beheld the Mother of God at Blachernae spreading her veil over the people in prayer — the Pokrov, the maternal sheltering remembered by all Slavic Orthodoxy.

also.name: St. Romanos the Melodist
also.summary: Sixth-century deacon of Constantinople and author of the great body of Byzantine kontakia.

also.name: Apostle Ananias of Damascus
also.summary: One of the Seventy and bishop of Damascus, who at the Lord's word laid hands on the blinded Saul and gave him sight and baptism.

also.name: Martyr Domninus of Thessalonica
also.summary: A fourth-century believer of Thessalonica who under Maximian was beaten with sinews and his bones broken for the name of Christ.

also.name: St. Mirian, King of Iberia, and Queen Nana, Equal-to-the-Apostles
also.summary: The fourth-century Georgian king and his queen, brought to the faith by the labors of St. Nina, who together led their nation to baptism.

also.name: Venerable-martyr Michael, Abbot of Zovia, and the thirty-six monks with him
also.summary: An eighth-century abbot of a monastery near Sebaste in Armenia and his community, slain together for refusing Islam.

also.name: Venerable Gregory of Khandzta, Wonderworker of Georgia
also.summary: A ninth-century father of Georgian monasticism in the Klarjeti wilderness who shaped the renewed cenobitic life of Iberia.

10-02.title: Hieromartyr Cyprian and Martyr Justina
10-02.summary: A former pagan magician of Antioch converted when his spells against the virgin Justina were broken by her prayer; bishop and virgin alike were beheaded together at Nicomedia.

also.name: Saint Damaris of Athens
also.summary: The woman of Athens converted with Dionysius the Areopagite at the apostle Paul's preaching from the Areopagus.

also.name: Martyr Theoctistus of Nicomedia
also.summary: A young Christian of Nicomedia beheaded with St. Cyprian and St. Justina for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs David and Constantine, Princes of Argveti
also.summary: Two Georgian princes who in 740 refused to renounce Christ before Murvan the Deaf and were thrown into the Rion river.

also.name: Blessed Andrew the Fool-for-Christ of Constantinople
also.summary: Tenth-century Slavic-born ascetic who took on the foolishness for Christ at Constantinople and beheld with Epiphanius the Pokrov vision at Blachernae.

also.name: Venerable Theophilus the Confessor
also.summary: An eighth-century confessor of Constantinople who endured many tortures under the iconoclast emperors.

10-03.title: Hieromartyr Dionysius the Areopagite, Bishop of Athens
10-03.summary: A member of the Athenian council converted by the apostle Paul on the hill of Mars and consecrated first bishop of Athens. Tradition makes him a missionary to Gaul, where he was beheaded at the place that bears his name.

also.name: Companions of St. Dionysius: Rusticus the Priest and Eleutherius the Deacon
also.summary: The presbyter and the deacon who labored with Dionysius in Gaul and were beheaded with him outside Paris.

also.name: Hieromartyr Dionysius the Great, Bishop of Alexandria
also.summary: Third-century patriarch of Alexandria, disciple of Origen and confessor under Decius and Valerian, who endured exile and finally reposed in his see.

also.name: Martyrs Theoctistus, Theagenes, and Theoteknos
Saint John the Chozebite, Bishop of Caesarea
also.summary: A sixth-century desert father of the wadi Chozeba in Palestine, made bishop of Caesarea against his will.

also.name: Venerable Hesychius the Silent of Mount Horeb
also.summary: A sixth-century hesychast of Sinai whose unbroken silence and ceaseless prayer shaped the Sinaitic monastic tradition.

10-04.title: Hieromartyr Hierotheus of Athens
10-04.summary: Together with Dionysius, a disciple of Paul at Athens and one of its first bishops, present at the dormition of the Theotokos and martyred in the apostolic age.

also.name: Hieromartyr Hierotheus, first Bishop of Athens
also.summary: A disciple of the apostle Paul, present at the dormition of the Theotokos.

also.name: St. Theodore the Wonderworker of Tamassos in Cyprus
also.summary: A second-century bishop of Tamassos in Cyprus renowned for his miracles.

also.name: Martyrs Gaius, Faustus, Eusebius, and Chaeremon, Deacons of Alexandria
also.summary: Four deacons of third-century Alexandria slain together for the name of Christ under Valerian.

also.name: Martyrs Domnina, Berenice, and Prosdoce of Syria
also.summary: A mother and her two daughters who in flight from the persecutors of Antioch in 303 leapt together into a river rather than be defiled.

also.name: Martyrs Adauctus and his daughter Callisthene of Ephesus
also.summary: A nobleman of fourth-century Ephesus and his daughter, who in flight disguised as a man preserved her faith and at last reposed in peace.

also.name: Venerable Paul the Simple
also.summary: A simple Egyptian farmer of the fourth century who at sixty came to Anthony the Great for the monastic life, whose obedience surpassed his master's instruction.

also.name: Venerable Ammon of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Nitria, disciple of Anthony the Great, who founded the great laura on the Mountain of Nitria.

also.name: Hieromartyr Peter of Capitolia
also.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Bostra in Arabia, slain by the Saracens for his witness.

also.name: Venerable John Lampadistes of Cyprus
also.summary: A tenth-century Cypriot ascetic blinded by his unfaithful betrothed's family who reposed in peace at the age of twenty-two.

also.name: Sts. Vladimir Yaroslavich and his mother Anna of Novgorod
also.summary: An eleventh-century prince of Novgorod and his Swedish mother Ingegerd, the lady of Yaroslav the Wise, who together rest in the cathedral of Holy Wisdom there.

10-05.title: Martyr Charitina of Amisus
10-05.summary: A noble orphan of Pontus raised by a Christian senator, who under Diocletian was scourged, had her teeth and hair torn out, and was at last cast into the sea — but coming up alive, was beheaded.

also.name: Hieromartyr Hermogenes the Wonderworker, Bishop of Samos
also.summary: An early bishop and wonderworker whose relics are venerated in Cyprus.

also.name: Martyr Mamelta of Persia
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian woman converted by a vision while she still served the fire-temple, stoned by the priests who had been her companions.

also.name: Venerable Gregory of Khandzta
also.summary: Ninth-century father of Georgian monasticism in the Klarjeti wilderness whose Vita was written by Giorgi Merchule.

also.name: Venerable Cosmas, Abbot in Bithynia
also.summary: A tenth-century abbot of a monastery in Bithynia, remembered for his patient leadership.

also.name: Venerables Damian the Healer, Jeremiah, and Matthew of the Kiev Caves
also.summary: Three eleventh-century fathers of the Kiev Caves Lavra — Damian who healed by the holy oil, Jeremiah and Matthew who saw into the hearts of their brethren.

also.name: St. John Mavropous, Metropolitan of Euchaita
also.summary: Eleventh-century hymnographer and metropolitan of Euchaita whose vision joined the Three Hierarchs in their common feast.

10-06.title: Holy Apostle Thomas
10-06.summary: Called the Twin, who would not believe without seeing, and seeing cried 'My Lord and my God.' Tradition sends him preaching as far as India, where he founded the Church and was pierced through with a spear.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Erotheis of Cappadocia
also.summary: An early Cappadocian maiden who, kindled with love for Christ, came out victorious over the flames in which she was given to die.

10-07.title: Martyrs Sergius and Bacchus
10-07.summary: Two officers of high rank in the army of Maximian, exposed as Christians and stripped of their honors; Bacchus was beaten to death, Sergius beheaded after being made to run in shoes lined with nails.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Julian the Presbyter and Caesarius the Deacon of Terracina
also.summary: A priest and a deacon of first-century Italy slain at Terracina on the Tyrrhenian coast.

also.name: Hieromartyr Eusebius the Priest and Martyr Felix of Terracina
St. Leontius the Governor of Terracina
also.summary: The Roman governor of Terracina converted by the witness of Julian and Caesarius, who shared in their martyrdom.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Pelagia of Tarsus
also.summary: A third-century maiden of Tarsus betrothed to the emperor's son who, refusing the union to keep her vow to Christ, was placed in a heated bronze bull and gave up her soul in song.

also.name: Hieromartyr Polychronius of Gamphanitus and his companions
also.summary: A fourth-century priest and the ten companions martyred with him for Christ.

10-08.title: St. Pelagia the Penitent
10-08.summary: A celebrated dancer of fifth-century Antioch, converted by the sermon of Bishop Nonnus at the doors of a church; she put on man's clothing and lived hidden on the Mount of Olives until her sex was discovered at her repose.

also.name: Hieromartyr Artemon, Presbyter of Laodicea
also.summary: A priest of fourth-century Laodicea in Asia who, having served the church from his youth, received the crown of martyrdom in old age by fire.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Pelagia of Antioch
also.summary: A fifteen-year-old maiden of Antioch in 303 who, surrounded by soldiers come to defile her, ran up to the rooftop and threw herself into the river rather than lose her purity.

also.name: Venerable Thaïs of Egypt, the former harlot
also.summary: A fourth-century courtesan of Egypt converted by the elder Paphnutius, who enclosed her three years in a cell of bricks and at her repose saw her soul carried up.

10-09.title: Holy Apostle James, son of Alphaeus
10-09.summary: One of the Twelve, brother of Matthew the Evangelist, who preached through Judea and Syria. He was crucified at Ostracine in Egypt, where he had labored in his old age.

also.name: Righteous Forefather Abraham and his nephew Lot
also.summary: The father of all believers who left his homeland at God's word, and his nephew whose righteous soul was vexed in the city of Sodom.

also.name: St. Demetrius, Bishop of Alexandria
also.summary: Bishop of Alexandria in the early third century who watched over Origen as a young man and reposed in peace after a long episcopate.

also.name: Confessor Publia the Deaconess of Antioch
also.summary: A fourth-century deaconess of Antioch who under Julian the Apostate sang the psalms aloud against the emperor as he passed and was struck for it without flinching.

also.name: Martyrs Juventinus and Maximinus of Antioch
also.summary: Two soldiers of the imperial guard at Antioch beheaded under Julian the Apostate for refusing to eat what had been offered to idols.

also.name: Venerables Andronicus and Athanasia of Egypt
also.summary: A sixth-century husband and wife of Antioch who upon the death of their children left the world together for the Egyptian desert.

also.name: Venerable Peter of Galatia

10-10.title: Martyrs Eulampius and Eulampia
10-10.summary: A brother and sister of Nicomedia under Maximian who, denounced for refusing to share in the imperial sacrifices, walked together to the lions and to the sword.

also.name: St. Pinytus, Bishop of Knossos
also.summary: Second-century bishop of Knossos in Crete, an early church writer commended by Eusebius for his sound counsel.

also.name: Martyr Theotecnus of Antioch
St. Maharsapor of Persia
also.summary: A fifth-century Persian Christian imprisoned three years for his confession and at length starved to death in his cell.

also.name: Venerable Bassian of Constantinople
also.summary: A fifth-century Syrian ascetic of Constantinople, founder of a monastery there and a fearless adversary of Nestorius.

also.name: Venerable Theophilus the Confessor
