also.summary: An eighth-century confessor of Bulgaria who endured the persecution of the iconoclast Leo the Isaurian.

10-11.title: Holy Apostle Philip, one of the Seven Deacons
10-11.summary: Not the apostle of the Twelve but one of the seven appointed by the apostles to serve the Hellenist widows in Acts; he baptized the Ethiopian eunuch and Simon Magus, and was bishop of Tralles in Asia Minor.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Zenaida and Philonilla of Tarsus
also.summary: Two kinswomen of the apostle Paul who in first-century Cilicia took up the medical art for the sake of the poor and at length received the crown of martyrdom together.

also.name: St. Nectarius, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: A senator of Tarsus made archbishop of Constantinople in 381 and confirmed in office by the Second Ecumenical Council over which he then presided.

also.name: Patriarchs Arsacius and Sisinius of Constantinople
also.summary: Two patriarchs of fifth-century Constantinople who succeeded in turn after the deposition of John Chrysostom.

also.name: Venerable Theophanes Graptus the Branded, Bishop of Nicaea
also.summary: A ninth-century confessor of the icons whose forehead was burned with iambic verses by the iconoclast Theophilus and who at the triumph was made bishop of Nicaea.

10-12.title: Martyrs Probus, Tarachus, and Andronicus
10-12.summary: Three Christians of Cilicia under Diocletian who endured eleven distinct torments before the prefect Numerian and at last were thrown to the beasts at Anazarbus.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Anastasia of Rome
also.summary: A third-century maiden of Rome who, raised in a convent of consecrated virgins, was beheaded under Valerian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Domnina of Anazarbus
also.summary: A maiden of Cilicia under Diocletian whose feet were burned to the bone before she gave up her soul to God.

also.name: Venerable Cosmas the Hymnographer, Bishop of Maiuma
also.summary: Eighth-century foster-brother of John of Damascus, author of many of the great festal canons of the Church and bishop of Maiuma near Gaza.

also.name: St. Theodotus, Bishop of Ephesus
St. Jason, Bishop of Damascus
Venerable Theosebius the God-bearer of Arsinoe in Cyprus
also.summary: An ascetic of Cyprus remembered for his bearing of the Spirit and the gentleness of his counsel.

also.name: Venerable Symeon the New Theologian
also.summary: Eleventh-century abbot of St. Mamas at Constantinople, whose hymns and discourses on the conscious experience of divine light shape Orthodox spirituality to this day.

10-13.title: Martyrs Carpus, Papylus, Agathonike, and Agathodorus
10-13.summary: Carpus the bishop, Papylus the deacon, and two others of Pergamum under Decius who were dragged behind chariots, scourged, and finally beheaded together for refusing to sacrifice.

also.name: Martyr Florentius of Thessalonica
also.summary: An early Christian of Thessalonica burned alive for his confession.

also.name: Martyr Dioscorus
Hieromartyr Benjamin the Deacon of Persia
also.summary: A fifth-century deacon of Persia released from prison on condition of silence about Christ and, refusing the terms, returned to martyrdom under Yazdegerd I.

also.name: St. Nicetas the Confessor of Paphlagonia
also.summary: A ninth-century patrician of Paphlagonia who set aside his honors for the cause of the holy icons and reposed in exile.

also.name: St. Luke of Demena
also.summary: Tenth-century Sicilian abbot who passed his last years founding monasteries in southern Italy.

10-14.title: Martyrs Nazarius, Gervasius, Protasius, and Celsus
10-14.summary: Four Italian martyrs of the apostolic age remembered at Milan, whose tomb was revealed to Ambrose in a vision and whose translation drew the city out in procession.

also.name: Martyr Peter Apselamus of Eleutheropolis
also.summary: A young Christian of Palestine under Maximinus Daia, of a noble family of Eleutheropolis, who at twenty was burned for his confession.

also.name: Hieromartyr Silvanus of Gaza and the forty companions with him
also.summary: A priest of fourth-century Gaza beheaded with forty of his fellows after he had been worked in the porphyry mines until he could see no longer.

also.name: Venerable Cosmas the Hymnographer, Bishop of Maiuma
also.summary: Eighth-century author of the great festal canons and foster-brother of John of Damascus.

also.name: Venerable Parasceva of Epibatae
also.summary: Eleventh-century maiden of Thrace who labored long in the desert beyond the Jordan and whose relics now rest at Iași in Romania.

10-15.title: St. Euthymius the New of Thessalonica
10-15.summary: A ninth-century monk of Athos, founder of a monastery on the holy mountain, who in his last years went up to Constantinople to confess the icons under the resurgent iconoclasts.

also.name: Martyrs Sarbelus and Bebaia of Edessa
also.summary: A brother and sister of second-century Edessa who shared one confession and one martyrdom.

also.name: Hieromartyr Lucian, Presbyter of Antioch
also.summary: Early-fourth-century priest of Antioch, founder of the catechetical school there, who confessed Christ before Maximinus Daia and died in prison after long fasting.

also.name: Venerable Barsus the Confessor, Bishop of Edessa
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Edessa exiled by the Arian Valens, who reposed in his place of banishment far from his see.

also.name: St. Theophilus I, Pope of Alexandria
also.summary: Patriarch of Alexandria from 385 to 412 and uncle of Cyril, a vigorous defender of orthodoxy whose name stands at the head of many of the early canons.

also.name: St. Sabinus the Wonderworker, Bishop of Catania
also.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Catania in Sicily who, longing for solitude, withdrew to a hermitage where he received many gifts of healing.

10-16.title: Martyr Longinus the Centurion
10-16.summary: The Roman officer who stood at the foot of the Cross and confessed 'truly this was the Son of God.' He was beheaded under Tiberius after preaching in his native Cappadocia.

also.name: Martyrs Isaurus and Aphrodisius, companions of St. Longinus
also.summary: Two of the soldiers under Longinus at Golgotha who shared in his confession and in his crown.

also.name: Martyrs Leontius, Dometius, Terence, and Domninus
also.summary: Four early martyrs given up together to the fire for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Malus the Hermit

10-17.title: Holy Prophet Hosea
10-17.summary: The eighth-century BC prophet of the northern kingdom whose own marriage to Gomer became the parable of God's love for unfaithful Israel — a love that does not let go.

also.name: St. Lazarus the Four-Days-Dead
also.summary: Bishop of Kition on Cyprus, friend of the Lord.

also.name: Holy Martyrs and Unmercenaries Cosmas and Damian of Arabia
also.summary: Two unmercenary physicians of fourth-century Cilicia martyred together for healing the sick freely in the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Antigonus, Loukianos, Terentius, Nicomedes, and Theophanis
Martyr-queen Shushanik of Georgia
also.summary: Fifth-century princess of Armenia and queen of the prince Varsken of Georgia, beaten and imprisoned by him for refusing to renounce Christ when he embraced Zoroastrianism.

also.name: Hieromartyr Andrew of Crete
also.summary: An eighth-century monk of Crete who came to Constantinople to confront Constantine V in the imperial palace over the holy icons and was clubbed to death by the iconoclast mob.

10-18.title: Holy Apostle and Evangelist Luke
10-18.summary: The physician and companion of Paul who wrote the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles, painted the first icon of the Theotokos, and reposed bishop of Thebes in Boeotia at the age of eighty-four.

also.name: Hieromartyr Mnason of Cyprus
also.summary: An early disciple from Cyprus who in the Acts hosted Paul on his last journey to Jerusalem and at length received the crown of martyrdom.

also.name: The Forty Children-Martyrs
Hieromartyr Cyriacus, Chorepiscopus of Jerusalem
also.summary: The Jewish guide of Helena who revealed to her the place of the Lord's Cross, was baptized, became bishop, and under Julian the Apostate was at last martyred.

also.name: Venerable Julian the Hermit
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Mesopotamia who lived in the wilderness near the Euphrates.

also.name: Venerables Symeon and Theodore, Founders of the Mega Spilaion Monastery, with the Shepherdess Euphrosyne
also.summary: Two fourth-century brothers who, led by the shepherdess Euphrosyne to a cave-icon of the Mother of God painted by St. Luke, founded the Great Cave Monastery in the Peloponnese.

10-19.title: Holy Prophet Joel
10-19.summary: The ninth-century BC prophet of Judah who foretold the locust as a sign of divine visitation and the pouring out of the Spirit upon all flesh in the last days, fulfilled at Pentecost.

also.name: Apostle Mnason of Cyprus
also.summary: An early disciple of Cyprus mentioned in the Acts as the host who received Paul on his way to Jerusalem.

also.name: Hieromartyr Felix the Presbyter and Eusebius the Deacon
Martyr Varus and the six monks with him
also.summary: A fourth-century soldier in Egypt who stood with six imprisoned monks and joined them in their crowning under Maximinus Daia.

also.name: Blessed Cleopatra and her son John of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century noblewoman of Egypt who brought up the body of St. Varus to her ancestral land and lost her young son in death the day she translated his relics.

also.name: Hieromartyr Sadoc of Persia and the one hundred and twenty with him
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop in the Persian church and his clergy and people, all beheaded together under Sapor II.

also.name: Venerable Prochorus of the Pchinja
also.summary: Tenth-century desert father of the Bulgarian-Macedonian wilderness on the river Pchinja.

10-20.title: Great-martyr Artemius of Antioch
10-20.summary: A noble general under Constantine the Great who refused to share in Julian the Apostate's restoration of idolatry, was stripped of his command, and was crushed beneath a great stone at Antioch.

also.name: Martyrs Zebinas, Germanus, Nicephorus, and Antoninus of Caesarea
also.summary: Four young men of Caesarea in Palestine who in 308 of their own will came forward to confess Christ and were given to the beasts and the sword.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Manatho of Scythopolis
also.summary: A maiden of fourth-century Scythopolis in Palestine burned alive for refusing the sacrifice to idols.

also.name: Martyrs Aborsam and Senoe of Persia
Hieromartyr Barsabias and his ten disciples of Persia
also.summary: A fourth-century abbot of a Persian monastery and his community, slain together under Sapor II for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Matrona of Chios
also.summary: Fifteenth-century maiden of the island of Chios who lived hidden in a hollow olive tree and at length founded a community where her tomb is honored to this day.

also.name: Righteous Child-martyr Artemius of Verkola
also.summary: Twelfth-year-old farmer's son of northern Russia struck dead by lightning in the fields in 1545, whose body remained incorrupt and worked many wonders.

10-21.title: St. Hilarion the Great
10-21.summary: A native of Gaza and the disciple of Anthony, who brought the desert life from Egypt to Palestine and afterwards to Cyprus, where he reposed in the wilderness at Paphos.

also.name: Hieromartyr Socrates the Presbyter and Theodote the Deaconess of Ancyra
also.summary: A priest and a deaconess of third-century Ancyra in Galatia who suffered persecution together.

also.name: Martyrs Dasius, Gaius, and Zoticus of Nicomedia
also.summary: Three Christian soldiers of Nicomedia drowned together in the sea for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Darius, Zosimus, Paul, and Secundus of Nicaea
The Sixty-three Martyrs of Jerusalem
also.summary: A great company of clergy and monastics of Jerusalem slain together for the name of Christ in 724.

also.name: Iconoclast-era Martyrs Andrew, Stephen, Paul, and Peter
also.summary: Four confessors of the eighth century who suffered under Constantine V the dung-named for the holy icons.

also.name: Venerable Jacob, Abbot of Batheos Rhyakos Monastery

10-22.title: Equal-to-the-Apostles Abercius, Bishop of Hierapolis
10-22.summary: A second-century bishop of Phrygia whose journeys for the faith reached as far as Rome and Edessa; his epitaph, still extant, is one of the earliest Christian inscriptions to confess the Mystery.

also.name: Hieromartyr Alexander, Bishop of Adrianople, and his companions
also.summary: A bishop of second-century Adrianople and the believers — Heraclius, Anna, Elizabeth, Theodota, and Glyceria — who suffered with him.

also.name: The Seven Holy Youths (Seven Sleepers) of Ephesus
also.summary: Seven young men of third-century Ephesus walled up in a cave under Decius who awoke two centuries later as a sign of the resurrection of the dead.

also.name: Martyr Theodoret of Antioch
also.summary: A presbyter of fourth-century Antioch beheaded under Julian the Apostate for refusing to surrender the church treasures.

also.name: Venerables Lot and Rufus of Egypt
also.summary: Two desert fathers of fifth-century Egypt remembered together in the sayings of the elders.

10-23.title: Holy Apostle James the Brother of the Lord, first Bishop of Jerusalem
10-23.summary: The son of Joseph by his first marriage who presided over the council of the apostles at Jerusalem and composed the most ancient eucharistic liturgy. He was thrown from the pinnacle of the Temple and clubbed by a Jewish mob.

also.name: Venerable Petronius of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century disciple of Pachomius the Great in the cenobia of upper Egypt.

also.name: Saint Ignatius, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: A ninth-century patriarch of Constantinople — son of the emperor Michael I — twice driven from his see in the rivalry with Photius and twice restored to it.

also.name: Venerable Macarius the Roman of Mesopotamia
also.summary: An ascetic of the eastern wilderness commemorated for his life of hesychast prayer beside the river of Mesopotamia.

10-24.title: Martyr Arethas and his companions of Najran
10-24.summary: A noble of sixth-century Arabia, slain with hundreds of fellow Christians by the Jewish king Dhu Nuwas of Himyar for refusing to renounce Christ — the martyrs whose blood prepared the way for Christian Ethiopia.

also.name: Martyr Sebastiani
also.summary: A first-century disciple of the apostle Paul from Marcianopolis who at length received the crown of martyrdom.

also.name: Martyrs Sotiricus and Valentinos
also.summary: Two believers of Asia Minor dragged together over sharp stones for their confession of Christ.

also.name: Hieromartyr Akakios the Presbyter
St. Proclus, Archbishop of Constantinople
also.summary: A fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople, disciple of John Chrysostom, who in 438 brought home his master's relics in solemn procession.

also.name: Blessed Elesbaan (Caleb), King of Ethiopia
also.summary: Sixth-century Ethiopian king who avenged the martyrs of Najran and at length put off his crown to live in repentance as a monk.

10-25.title: Martyrs Marcian and Martyrius the Notaries
10-25.summary: Two notaries of the Patriarch Paul of Constantinople under the Arians who, refusing to surrender the records of the Nicene Council, were beheaded for the truth.

also.name: Righteous Tabitha of Joppa
also.summary: The widow of Joppa whom the apostle Peter raised from the dead, as the book of Acts recounts.

also.name: Martyrs Pappias, Diodorus, and Claudianus of Attaleia
also.summary: Three believers of third-century Pamphylia beheaded together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Anastasius the Fuller of Salona
also.summary: A Roman fuller of third-century Salona in Dalmatia who painted a cross on the door of his shop and was beheaded for refusing to deny Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Faustus, Basil, and Lucianus of Darion
Two Holy Martyrs of Thrace
also.summary: Two Christians of Thrace crushed together between great stones for their confession of Christ.

also.name: St. George the Canon-writer, Bishop of Amastris
also.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Amastris on the Black Sea, hymnographer and defender of his people from a great Russian raid.

10-26.title: Great-martyr Demetrius the Myrrh-streamer of Thessalonica
10-26.summary: A young proconsul of Thessalonica under Galerius, openly catechizing the people of his city, who was speared in the bathhouse where he had been imprisoned and whose tomb has streamed myrrh ever since.

also.name: Martyr Lupus, slave of St. Demetrius
also.summary: The faithful servant of Demetrius at Thessalonica who, having dipped his master's tunic in the saint's blood and worked many wonders with it, was himself beheaded for the same confession.

also.name: Commemoration of the Great Earthquake at Constantinople in 740
Venerable Athanasius of the Medikion Monastery
also.summary: A ninth-century abbot of the Medikion monastery in Bithynia who together with Nicephorus suffered exile under the second iconoclasm.

10-27.title: Martyr Nestor of Thessalonica
10-27.summary: A young Christian of Thessalonica blessed by Demetrius from prison who went out and slew the imperial gladiator Lyaeus in single combat; for this confession of Christ he was beheaded.

also.name: Saint Claudia Procula, wife of Pontius Pilate
also.summary: The wife of the Roman procurator who at the Lord's trial sent word to her husband not to condemn the Just One she had seen in a dream.

also.name: Martyrs Capitolina and Eroteis of Cappadocia
also.summary: A noblewoman of Cappadocia and her maidservant beheaded together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyr Mark of Thasos and his companions
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of the island of Thasos crowned with a small band of fellow believers.

also.name: Saint Kyriakos II, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: A seventh-century patriarch of Constantinople under whom the Persian invasion of the empire began.

10-28.title: Martyrs Terence and Neonilla, with their seven children
10-28.summary: A Christian family of Syria under Decius who refused the sacrifice together; the parents and all seven children were tortured and finally beheaded as one.

also.name: Great-martyr Paraskeva
Great-martyr Paraskeva of Iconium
also.summary: A third-century maiden of Iconium named for the day of the Lord's Passion who endured great torture for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Firmilian, Bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia
also.summary: Third-century bishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia, disciple of Origen and convener of the synod of Antioch that excommunicated Paul of Samosata.

also.name: Venerable Malchion the Presbyter of Antioch
also.summary: A learned priest of Antioch in the third century whose disputation refuted the heresies of Paul of Samosata at the great synod.

also.name: Hieromartyr Cyriacus, Chorepiscopus of Jerusalem, and his mother Anna
also.summary: The Jewish guide of Helena who at the discovery of the True Cross was baptized with his mother and both at length received the crown under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Venerable Stephen of Mar Saba the Hymnographer
also.summary: An eighth-century monk of the great lavra of Mar Saba, nephew of John of Damascus, author of many of the canons in the Octoechos.

also.name: Hieromartyr Neophytus, Bishop of Urbnisi
also.summary: A seventh-century bishop of Urbnisi in Georgia slain for the name of Christ.

10-29.title: Virgin-martyr Anastasia the Roman
10-29.summary: A young ascetic raised in a convent of Rome in the third century, taken under Decius and after multiple torments beheaded outside the city, the first virgin-martyr to bear that name.

also.name: Martyrs Claudius, Asterius, Neon, and Theonilla of Aegae
also.summary: Four believers — three brothers and their sister — of third-century Cilicia who under Diocletian were beheaded at Aegae for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Sabbas Stratelates
Venerable Abramius the Recluse and his niece Mary of Mesopotamia
also.summary: A fourth-century recluse of Mesopotamia who, when his niece Mary fled into the world after a temptation, sought her out and brought her back to repentance.

also.name: Martyr Melitene of Marcianopolis
Venerable Anna of Mount Olympus
also.summary: A ninth-century widow of Constantinople who under the name Euphemianus lived as a monk in a community on Mount Olympus in Bithynia.

also.name: Saint Serapion of Zarzma in Georgia
also.summary: Tenth-century abbot of the monastery of Zarzma in Georgia and a builder of churches in his land.

10-30.title: Martyr Zenobius and his sister Zenobia
10-30.summary: A brother and sister of Cilicia, both physicians who served the poor, beheaded together under Diocletian after enduring the rack and the gridiron.

also.name: Apostles Cleopas, Tertius, Mark, Justus, and Artemas of the Seventy
also.summary: Five of the Seventy: Cleopas the brother of Joseph the Betrothed, to whom the Lord appeared at Emmaus; Tertius scribe of Romans; Mark cousin of Barnabas; Justus called Joseph the just; and Artemas bishop of Lystra.

also.name: Hieromartyr Marcian, Bishop of Syracuse
also.summary: A first-century bishop of Syracuse said to have been consecrated by the apostle Peter himself, slain in the very early centuries of the Sicilian church.

also.name: Virgin-martyr and Deaconess Apollonia of Alexandria
also.summary: A third-century deaconess of Alexandria who, when the pagan mob set fire to her funeral pyre, leapt into it of her own will rather than blaspheme.

also.name: Martyrs Alexander, Cronion, Julian, and Macarius of Alexandria
also.summary: Four believers of third-century Alexandria slain under Decius with thirteen more of their company.

also.name: Martyr Eutropia of Alexandria
St. Asterius, Metropolitan of Amasea
also.summary: Fourth-century metropolitan of Amasea on the Black Sea coast, a celebrated preacher whose homilies still survive.

10-31.title: Apostles Stachys, Amplias, Urban, Narcissus, Apelles, and Aristobulus
10-31.summary: Six of the Seventy named in Romans 16: Stachys became bishop of Byzantium under Andrew; Amplias and Urban at Diospolis and Macedonia; Narcissus and Apelles at Athens and Heraclea; Aristobulus, brother of Barnabas, in Britain.

also.name: Martyr Epimachus of Pelusium
also.summary: A third-century Christian of Pelusium in Egypt who, learning that the persecutors were taking believers at Alexandria, went out to meet them and freely confessed Christ.

also.name: Saint Maura of Constantinople
also.summary: A fifth-century holy woman of Constantinople who reposed in peace under Theodosius the Younger.

also.name: Saint Jacob of Nisibis
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Nisibis on the Persian frontier, a hesychast of the wilderness, father of Ephraim the Syrian, and present at the First Ecumenical Council.

11-01.title: Unmercenaries and Wonderworkers Cosmas and Damian of Asia
11-01.summary: Two brothers of third-century Asia Minor, sons of the Christian Theodota, who having received the gift of healing freely gave to all who came. They reposed in peace at Pheremma.

also.name: Cosmas and Damian, Unmercenaries of Asia
Venerable Theodota, mother of Cosmas and Damian
also.summary: Pious widow of Asia Minor who raised her sons in the faith and taught them the practice of unmercenary medicine.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Cyrenia and Juliana of Cilicia
also.summary: Two Christian maidens of fourth-century Cilicia beheaded together for the name of Christ under Maximian.

also.name: Hieromartyrs John the Bishop and James the Presbyter of Persia
also.summary: A bishop and his priest in fourth-century Persia put to death together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

also.name: Martyrs Caesarius, Dacius, Sabbas, Sabinian, Agrippa, Adrian, and Thomas of Damascus
also.summary: Seven Christians of seventh-century Damascus crowned together for the faith in the early days of the Arab conquest.

also.name: Martyrs Cyprian and Juliana of Tarsus
also.summary: A bishop and a maiden of Tarsus under Maximian whose case Justina of Antioch would echo a generation later.

11-02.title: Martyrs Acindynus, Pegasius, Aphthonius, Elpidiphorus, and Anempodistus of Persia
11-02.summary: Five Christians of the Persian court under Sapor II who endured every torture together for confessing the Lord, drawing thousands to the faith before they were beheaded.

also.name: The Holy Senators of Sebasteia: Eudoxios, Agapios, Atticus, Marinus, Oceanus, Eustratios, Karterios, Nikopolitianos, Styrax, and Tobias
also.summary: A company of noble Christians of Sebaste in Armenia burned together for the name of Christ under Licinius.

also.name: Women-martyrs Kyriaki, Domnina, and Domna
Venerable Marcian of Cyrrhus
also.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Syrian wilderness near Cyrrhus whose silent austerity drew many to seek him out.

also.name: St. Anthony the Confessor, Archbishop of Thessalonica
also.summary: Ninth-century hierarch of Thessalonica who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under the resurgent iconoclasm.

11-03.title: Martyrs Acepsimas the Bishop, Joseph the Presbyter, and Aeithalas the Deacon of Persia
11-03.summary: A bishop, priest, and deacon of fourth-century Persia under Sapor II, scourged and slowly killed over months in prison for refusing to worship the sun, the trinity of their order matching the Trinity they confessed.

also.name: Translation of the relics of Great-martyr George
also.summary: Commemoration of the dedication of the great basilica at Lydda built over the relics of the soldier-martyr.

also.name: Martyrs Atticus, Agapius, Eudoxius, Carterius, Istucarius, Pactobius, and Nictopolion of Sebaste
also.summary: A company of fourth-century soldiers at Sebaste in Armenia crowned together under Licinius.

also.name: Confessor Snandulia of Persia
also.summary: A noblewoman of fourth-century Persia who gathered the bodies of the holy martyrs by night for honorable burial.

also.name: Confessor Achaemonides (Hormisdas) of Persia
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian noble who renounced his rank for poverty and exile rather than the worship of fire.

also.name: Venerable Acepsimas the Hermit of Cyrrhus
also.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of Syria who lived sixty years walled into a cave near Cyrrhus.

also.name: Venerable Elias of Egypt
St. Theodore the Confessor, Bishop of Ancyra
also.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Ancyra who suffered exile under the iconoclast emperors for the cause of the holy images.

11-04.title: St. Joannicius the Great of Bithynia
11-04.summary: A ninth-century soldier turned monk on Mount Olympus in Bithynia, defender of the holy icons against the second iconoclasm and prophet to many who came up the mountain seeking counsel.

also.name: Righteous Abimelech of Gerar
also.summary: The Philistine king of Genesis who at God's word restored Sarah to Abraham and called the patriarch a prince of God.

also.name: Apostles Hermas, Patrobas, Linus, Gaius, and Philologus of the Seventy
also.summary: Five of the Seventy whom Paul names in the Letter to the Romans: Hermas bishop of Philippopolis, Patrobas of Pozzuoli, Linus second bishop of Rome after Peter, Gaius of Ephesus, and Philologus of Sinope.

also.name: Hieromartyr Nicander, Bishop of Myra
also.summary: A first-century bishop of Myra in Lycia and disciple of the apostle Titus, who together with the presbyter Hermas was slain for Christ.

also.name: St. Sylvia of Rome, mother of Gregory the Dialogist
also.summary: Sixth-century Roman matron and mother of Pope Gregory the Great, who outlived her husband to live as a recluse.

11-05.title: Martyrs Galaction and Episteme
11-05.summary: A young husband and wife of third-century Emesa who shortly after their marriage parted by mutual agreement for the monastic life, were arrested for the faith, and were beheaded under Decius.

also.name: Apostles Patrobas, Hermas, Linus, Gaius, and Philologus of the Seventy
also.summary: Five of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles, sent through the early decades to the great cities of the empire.

also.name: Martyrs Domninus, Timothy, Theophilus, Theotimus, Dorotheus, Eupsychius, Carterius, Silvanus, Pamphilius, Philotheos, and Nearchos of Palestine
also.summary: Eleven Christians of Palestine put to death together under Maximinus Daia at the dawn of the fourth century.

also.name: St. Gregory the Confessor, Archbishop of Alexandria
also.summary: Ninth-century patriarch of Alexandria who suffered for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: St. Jonah the Wonderworker, Archbishop of Novgorod
also.summary: Fifteenth-century archbishop of Novgorod whose prayers were credited with delivering the city from famine and his see from interference.

11-06.title: St. Paul the Confessor, Archbishop of Constantinople
11-06.summary: A fourth-century hierarch driven from his see three times by the Arian party and at last strangled with his own omophorion at Cucusus in Armenia, where he had been sent in exile.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Tecusa, Alexandra, Claudia, Matrona, Polactia, Euphrosyne, Julia, Euphrasia, Faina, and Athanasia of Ancyra
also.summary: Ten consecrated virgins of fourth-century Ancyra in Galatia drowned together under Diocletian for refusing the rites of Artemis.

also.name: Venerable Luke of Tauromenium in Sicily
also.summary: A ninth-century monk and bishop of Sicily, founder of a monastery on the slopes of Etna.

also.name: St. Demetrianus the Wonderworker, Bishop of Cytheria in Cyprus
also.summary: A tenth-century bishop of Cyprus, husband and monk before he was raised to the episcopate, remembered for his prayer for his captured flock.

also.name: Blessed Paul of Corinth, Fool-for-Christ
also.summary: A holy fool of Corinth who concealed his ascetic prayer under the disguise of madness.

11-07.title: Martyr Hieron of Melitene and thirty-three companions
11-07.summary: Thirty-three Christians of Melitene in Armenia under Diocletian, including the brave farmer Hieron who refused conscription and led his fellow soldiers to confession and execution together.

also.name: Martyrs Matronian and Anthony of Melitene
also.summary: Brothers of the martyr Hieron who shared in his confession at Melitene under Diocletian.

also.name: St. Gregory, brother of Gregory the Wonderworker
Martyrs Auctus, Taurion, and Thessalonica of Amphipolis
Martyr Theodotus the Innkeeper of Ancyra
also.summary: An innkeeper of Ancyra who took up the bodies of the seven virgin-martyrs of his city for honorable burial and was beheaded for it.

also.name: Martyr Alexander of Thessalonica
also.summary: An early-fourth-century believer of Thessalonica beheaded for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Melasippus, Cassina, and their son Antoninus of Ancyra
also.summary: A father, mother, and twelve-year-old son slain together at Ancyra under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Venerable Lazarus the Wonderworker of Mount Galesion
also.summary: An eleventh-century stylite who built three monasteries on Mount Galesion near Ephesus and lived sixty years on a pillar.

11-08.title: The Synaxis of the Archangel Michael and the other Bodiless Powers
11-08.summary: The feast of the chief of the angels — Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, Uriel, and all the heavenly hosts: thrones, dominions, principalities, authorities, powers, cherubim, and seraphim who unceasingly hymn the Trinity.

also.name: Archangel Gabriel
Archangel Raphael
Archangel Uriel
Archangel Selaphiel
Archangel Jegudiel
Archangel Barachiel
Archangel Michael

11-09.title: Martyrs Onesiphorus and Porphyrius; St. Matrona of Constantinople
11-09.summary: Two friends of fourth-century Ephesus, tortured and dragged behind horses to their deaths under Diocletian. Also commemorated: Matrona, who disguised as a man entered the monastery of St. Bassian and lived hidden for many years.

also.name: St. Matrona of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century ascetic who lived hidden in a male monastery to escape her pagan husband.

also.name: St. Nektarios of Aegina
also.summary: Late-nineteenth century Greek hierarch and modern wonderworker.

also.name: Martyrs Narses and Artemon
Martyrs Christopher and Maura
The Four Crowned Martyrs: Claudius, Castor, Sempronian, and Nicostratus of Pannonia
also.summary: Four sculptors of fourth-century Pannonia who refused to carve an idol of Aesculapius and were beaten to death together for their confession of Christ.

also.name: Venerable John the Short (Kolobos) of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Scetis whose master's barren stick blossomed at his obedient watering — the apothegm of monastic patience.

also.name: Venerables Eustolia and Sosipatra of Constantinople
also.summary: Two consecrated virgins of seventh-century Constantinople, the latter the daughter of the emperor Maurice, who together founded a convent in the imperial city.

also.name: Venerable Theoctiste of the Hundred Doors of Lesbos
also.summary: A ninth-century maiden of Lesbos who lived hidden for many years on the deserted island of Paros after fleeing Saracen captors.

also.name: Venerable Symeon Metaphrastes
also.summary: Tenth-century logothete of Constantinople who compiled and reworked the great body of saints' lives that bears his name.

also.name: Venerables Euthymius and Neophytus, founders of Docheiariou Monastery on Mount Athos

11-10.title: Apostles Erastus, Olympas, Rodion, Sosipater, Quartus, and Tertius of the Seventy
11-10.summary: Six of the seventy named by Paul in the Letter to the Romans, who served as bishops in Corinth, Rome, Patras, Iconium, Berytus, and Macedonia, and met their crowns at the hands of pagans.

also.name: Hieromartyr Demetrianus, Bishop of Antioch
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Antioch carried off into Persian captivity by Sapor I where he reposed in his exile.

also.name: Martyr Orestes the Physician of Cappadocia
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian physician of Cappadocia bound to a wild horse and dragged to his death.

also.name: Hieromartyr Milos the Wonderworker, Bishop in Persia, with the disciples Euores and Seboes
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian bishop and two of his disciples crowned together under Sapor II for the cause of Christ.

also.name: St. Nonnus, Bishop of Heliopolis
also.summary: A fifth-century bishop whose preaching outside a church in Antioch converted the celebrated dancer Pelagia.

also.name: The Ten Martyrs of Gaza
also.summary: Ten believers of seventh-century Gaza slain together by the Saracens in the early years of the conquest.

also.name: Venerable Theocteristus, Abbot of Mount Olympus
also.summary: An eighth-century abbot of a monastery on Mount Olympus in Bithynia who suffered exile for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Great-martyr Constantine-Kakhi, Prince of Georgia
also.summary: A ninth-century prince of Kartli in Georgia martyred in Samarkand by Caliph al-Mutawakkil for refusing Islam.

11-11.title: Great-martyr Menas of Egypt
11-11.summary: An officer of the Roman army under Diocletian who left his command for the wilderness, returned openly to the city to confess Christ, and was beheaded at Cotyaeum in Phrygia — his shrine in Egypt became famed for healings.

also.name: Martyrs Victor and Stephanida of Damascus
also.summary: A Christian soldier of Damascus and the young wife of a fellow soldier converted at the sight of his courage, both slain together for the faith.

also.name: Martyr Vincent of Spain, the Deacon of Saragossa
also.summary: An early-fourth-century deacon of Saragossa in Spain who endured every torment under Dacian the governor and reposed at last in the consolation of an angel.

also.name: Martyr Drakonas of Arauraka in Armenia
also.summary: A fourth-century Armenian Christian beheaded under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Theodore the Confessor, Abbot of the Stoudios Monastery
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the great Stoudios monastery of Constantinople, exiled three times by the iconoclasts and shaper of Byzantine cenobitic life.

11-12.title: St. John the Merciful, Patriarch of Alexandria; St. Nilus the Faster of Sinai
11-12.summary: John, the seventh-century patriarch whose alms-giving emptied the treasuries of his see and filled the homes of the poor. Also commemorated: Nilus, the fifth-century desert father of Sinai whose letters are read among the gathered fathers.

also.name: St. Nilus the Faster of Sinai
Holy Prophet Ahijah the Shilonite
also.summary: The prophet of Shiloh of the days of Solomon who tore his garment in twelve pieces before Jeroboam, prefiguring the division of the kingdom.

also.name: Martyr Arsacius
Martyrs Anthony, Zevinus, Germanus, Nicephorus, and Maratho of Caesarea
also.summary: Five Christians of Caesarea in Palestine slain together under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: St. Martin the Merciful, Bishop of Tours
also.summary: Fourth-century soldier-monk of Gaul who divided his cloak with a beggar at Amiens and went on to found Western monasticism at Marmoutier.

11-13.title: St. John Chrysostom, Archbishop of Constantinople
11-13.summary: The fifth-century preacher whose golden mouth shaped the Eastern liturgy that bears his name, twice exiled by an Arian empress for the faithfulness of his words; he reposed at Comana saying 'Glory to God for all things.'

also.name: Martyrs Antoninus, Nicephorus, Germanus, and the maiden Manetha of Caesarea
also.summary: Three young men and a young woman of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine who openly rebuked the prefect for the sacrifices and were given over to the fire.

also.name: Venerable Hilarion the Iberian
also.summary: Ninth-century Georgian monk of Mount Athos and later of Olympus whose journeys brought the spiritual treasures of Georgia to the Greek-speaking world.

11-14.title: Holy Apostle Philip
11-14.summary: One of the Twelve, who brought Nathanael to Christ and at the feeding of the five thousand counted the cost; he preached through Phrygia and was crucified head-downward at Hierapolis.

also.name: St. Gregory Palamas, Archbishop of Thessalonica
also.summary: Fourteenth-century defender of the divine energies (his Sunday is the Second of Great Lent).

also.name: St. Stachys, Bishop of Hierapolis
also.summary: An early disciple ordained at Hierapolis by Bartholomew after the martyrdom of the apostle Philip.

also.name: Saint Justinian I, Emperor of New Rome, and Saint Theodora the Empress
also.summary: The sixth-century emperor whose codification of the law and great churches — above all the Holy Wisdom — shaped the Christian Roman commonwealth, and the empress who stood beside him.

also.name: St. Thomas II, Patriarch of Constantinople
The Virgin-martyrs of Emesa
also.summary: A company of consecrated maidens of Emesa in Syria slain together for the name of Christ in 779 during the Abbasid persecution.

also.name: Venerable Fantinus the Younger of Calabria
also.summary: A tenth-century wonderworker of southern Italy, abbot of a Greek monastery in Calabria.

11-15.title: Beginning of the Nativity Fast; Martyrs and Confessors Gurias, Samonas, and Habib
11-15.summary: The forty-day fast in preparation for the Nativity of the Lord begins this day. Also commemorated: three confessors of Edessa, the first two who reposed in chains under Diocletian, and the deacon Habib burned under Licinius.

also.name: Confessors Gurias, Samonas, and Habib of Edessa
St. Paisius Velichkovsky
also.summary: Eighteenth-century elder who translated the Philokalia into Slavonic and seeded the Russian elder tradition.

also.name: Martyr Demetrius of Thrace
also.summary: A third-century Christian of the village of Davoudio in Thrace beheaded under the governor Pomplius.

also.name: St. Quinctian, Bishop of Seleucia
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Seleucia who took part in the First Ecumenical Council and afterwards withdrew to live as a hermit.

also.name: Martyrs Eupsychios, Nearchos, and Karterios
Martyrs Elpidius, Marcellus, and Eustochius
also.summary: Three believers slain together under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

11-16.title: Holy Apostle and Evangelist Matthew
11-16.summary: The publican called from his receipt of custom by the Lord's word 'Follow Me,' who wrote his Gospel first of all the four in Hebrew. He labored in Ethiopia and was crowned with martyrdom in his old age.

also.name: St. Fulvianus, the Prince of Ethiopia
also.summary: The Ethiopian ruler who in repentance for the martyrdom of Matthew received baptism, took the name Matthew, and ended his days in episcopal labors.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Ephigenia of Ethiopia
also.summary: An Ethiopian princess of the first century baptized by the apostle Matthew, who at length received the martyr's crown.

also.name: Hieromartyr Hypatius the Wonderworker, Bishop of Gangra
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Gangra in Paphlagonia, a father of the First Ecumenical Council, slain by Novatianists on his return from Constantinople.

11-17.title: St. Gregory the Wonderworker, Bishop of Neo-Caesarea
11-17.summary: A third-century disciple of Origen who found his see at Neo-Caesarea with seventeen Christians and left it with seventeen pagans, having converted the city by miracles, by teaching, and by a creed received in vision from St. John the Theologian.

also.name: Gregory the Wonderworker, Bishop of Neo-Caesarea
Hieromartyr Basil, Bishop of Hamah
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Hamah in Syria who suffered martyrdom for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Zachariah the Cobbler and his wife Mary
Martyrs Gregory, Victor, and Geminus of Heracleon in Thrace
also.summary: Three companions slain together at Heracleon in Thrace under Diocletian.

also.name: The One Hundred and Fifty Philosophers of Alexandria
also.summary: The pagan rhetoricians and philosophers gathered by Maximinus Daia to dispute with St. Catherine, who were converted by her words and given up to the fire together.

also.name: Venerable Longinus of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Cilicia and Egypt, abbot of Enaton near Alexandria, whose sayings stand in the apothegms.

also.name: St. Maximian, Archbishop of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople, raised to the see in place of Nestorius after his deposition.

also.name: St. Gennadius I, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Constantinople under whom the synod confirmed the canons of Chalcedon and restored discipline in the church.

also.name: Venerable Lazarus the Iconographer of Constantinople
also.summary: A ninth-century monk-iconographer whose hands were burned by the iconoclast emperor and who at the triumph painted again the icons of the Lord and the Theotokos.

also.name: Great-martyr Gobron (Michael) of Georgia and the one hundred and thirty-three soldiers with him
also.summary: Tenth-century Georgian commander and his company slain together under Sajid sultan Abu'l-Qasim for refusing Islam.

also.name: Venerable Nikon, Abbot of Radonezh
also.summary: Fifteenth-century disciple and successor of Sergius of Radonezh as abbot of the Holy Trinity Lavra.

11-18.title: Martyr Plato of Ancyra
11-18.summary: A young man of Ancyra of noble birth in the fourth century who under Galerius endured nineteen tortures and was finally beheaded for the confession of Christ.

also.name: Hieromartyr Romanus the Deacon of Caesarea
also.summary: A fourth-century deacon of Caesarea in Palestine whose tongue was cut out and who continued to confess Christ until they strangled him in prison.

also.name: Child-martyr Barulas of Antioch
also.summary: A young boy of Antioch who, asked whether the gods were many or one, named Christ and was beheaded — encouraged in his answer by St. Romanus.

also.name: Martyrs Zacchaeus the Deacon and Alphaeus the Reader of Caesarea
also.summary: A deacon of Gadara and a reader of Caesarea who together openly confessed Christ before the prefect and were beheaded.

11-19.title: Holy Prophet Obadiah
11-19.summary: The ninth-century BC prophet whose short book pronounces the judgment of God upon Edom and the day when the kingdom shall be the Lord's.

also.name: Martyr Heliodorus of Pamphylia
also.summary: A third-century believer of Pamphylia who under Aurelian was hung up and his sides torn before being given up to death.

also.name: Martyr Azes of Isauria and one hundred and fifty soldiers with him
also.summary: An officer of third-century Isauria who at his arrest converted his guards and was beheaded with one hundred and fifty of them.

also.name: Martyr Barlaam of Caesarea in Cappadocia
also.summary: An elderly peasant of Caesarea under Diocletian whose hand was held over the altar with burning coals and incense, and who endured rather than let the coals fall.

also.name: Martyr Agapius of Caesarea Maritima
also.summary: A young Christian of fourth-century Caesarea given up to the beasts under Maximinus Daia.

also.name: Venerables Barlaam and Ioasaph of India
also.summary: The hermit-teacher and the Indian prince whose dialogue, set down by John of Damascus, brings the gospel through the framework of the life of the Buddha.

also.name: Venerable Simon the Wonderworker of Calabria

11-20.title: Forefeast of the Entrance of the Theotokos; St. Gregory the Decapolite
11-20.summary: The eve of the Entrance into the Temple. Also commemorated: the ninth-century monk of Greek Decapolis whose ascetic struggle and journeys through Asia Minor and Italy left a luminous example to his disciples.

also.name: Martyr Dasius of Dorostolum
also.summary: A Christian soldier of fourth-century Dorostolum (modern Silistra) chosen by lot for the role of mock-king at the feast of Saturn, who refused the impure rites and was beheaded.

also.name: Martyrs Eustathios, Thespesios, and Anatolios of Nicaea
also.summary: Three sons of a wealthy merchant of Nicaea baptized by Bishop Anthimus, who under Maximian Galerius died together under torture.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Nerses and Joseph of Persia
also.summary: A bishop and his disciple beheaded together in fourth-century Persia under Sapor II for the cause of Christ.

also.name: Bassus and the forty-two companions with him, Martyrs of Heraclea
St. Isaac the Catholicos of Armenia
also.summary: Fifth-century catholicos of the Armenian church who with St. Mesrop directed the translation of the Scriptures into the new Armenian alphabet.

also.name: St. Proclus, Archbishop of Constantinople
also.summary: Disciple of John Chrysostom and archbishop of Constantinople in the fifth century, who in 438 translated his master's relics home from Comana.

also.name: Confessor Theoctistus the Patrician
also.summary: A ninth-century imperial official confessor of the holy icons under the second iconoclasm.

11-21.title: The Entrance of the Most Holy Theotokos into the Temple
11-21.summary: The three-year-old Mary was led by Joachim and Anna up the temple steps; the priest Zacharias received her and brought her into the Holy of Holies, where she was fed by an angel as the living temple prepared for the indwelling of God.

11-22.title: Apostles Philemon and Archippus, and Apphia, sister of Philemon
11-22.summary: The Christians of Colossae to whom Paul addressed his shortest letter, asking that the runaway slave Onesimus be received as a brother. All three were stoned to death together at Colossae.

also.name: Apostle Onesimus, the runaway slave of Philemon
also.summary: The slave for whom Paul wrote to Philemon, who became the apostle's son in the faith and at length bishop of Ephesus.

also.name: Martyr Cecilia of Rome, with Valerian, Tiburtius, and Maximus
also.summary: A Roman noblewoman of the third century who converted her husband Valerian and his brother Tiburtius and the soldier Maximus, all four crowned together for the faith.

also.name: Martyr Menignus the Tanner of Parium
also.summary: A tanner of third-century Parium beheaded under Decius for refusing to surrender his Christian books.

also.name: Martyr Procopius the Reader of Caesarea
also.summary: A reader of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine beheaded under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: St. Clement of Ohrid, Bishop of Bulgaria, Equal-to-the-Apostles
also.summary: Disciple of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century bishop of Ohrid, father of the Slavic literary tradition and enlightener of Bulgaria.

11-23.title: Sts. Amphilochius of Iconium and Gregory of Acragas
11-23.summary: Amphilochius, the fourth-century cousin of Gregory the Theologian who at the council of Constantinople defended the divinity of the Holy Spirit. Also commemorated: Gregory of Sicily, hierarch of Acragas in the sixth century.

also.name: St. Gregory of Acragas, Bishop of Sicily
Virgin-martyr Myrope of Chios
also.summary: A maiden of Chios under Decius who having given honorable burial to the martyr Isidore was found out and beaten to death by the soldiers responsible for the body.

also.name: Confessor Sisinius, Bishop of Cyzicus
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Cyzicus who endured many torments under Diocletian and afterwards reposed in peace.

also.name: Venerable Ischyrion, Bishop in Egypt
St. Elenos, Bishop of Tarsus
Martyr Theodore of Antioch
also.summary: A Christian of Antioch slain in the years just after Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

11-24.title: Great-martyr Catherine of Alexandria
11-24.summary: A noble maiden of Alexandria, peerless in learning, who at eighteen confounded fifty pagan philosophers gathered by Maximin to dispute with her; she was beheaded after the wheel of her torture was shattered by angels.

also.name: Hieromartyr Clement I, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Late-first-century bishop of Rome, named by Paul in Philippians, whose letter to the Corinthians is among the earliest writings outside the New Testament; drowned with an anchor about his neck in the Crimea.

also.name: Great-martyr Mercurius of Caesarea
also.summary: A third-century Christian officer of Caesarea in Cappadocia beheaded under Decius and afterward sent in a vision by Basil the Great to slay Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Martyr Augusta, the Empress, with Porphyrius Stratelates and the two hundred soldiers
also.summary: The empress Augusta and her commander Porphyrius and his soldiers, all converted by St. Catherine in prison and beheaded with her.

also.name: Hieromartyr Peter I, Archbishop of Alexandria
also.summary: Early-fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria, last martyr of the great persecution under Maximinus Daia, beheaded outside his city in 311.

also.name: Martyr Alexander of Corinth
also.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Corinth beheaded under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Carion of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Scetis whose sayings stand among the apothegms of the elders.

also.name: Venerable Malchus of Chalcis in Syria
also.summary: A fifth-century monk of Syria captured by the Saracens, freed by miracle, and at length restored to his solitude.

also.name: Venerable Mark of Triglia

11-25.title: Leavetaking of the Entrance; Hieromartyr Clement of Rome
11-25.summary: The closing of the eight-day feast of the Entrance. Also commemorated: Clement, a disciple of the apostles Peter and Paul and third bishop of Rome, exiled to the Crimea and drowned with an anchor about his neck.

also.name: Great-martyr Mercurius of Caesarea
also.summary: A third-century Christian officer of Caesarea in Cappadocia who in vision was sent to slay Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Great-martyr Catherine of Alexandria
also.summary: The noble maiden of Alexandria peerless in learning, beheaded under Maximinus Daia.

also.name: The Holy One Hundred and Fifty Orators of Alexandria
also.summary: The philosophers and rhetoricians converted by Catherine's words and given over together to the fire.

also.name: Hieromartyr Peter I, Archbishop of Alexandria
also.summary: Last martyr of the great persecution at Alexandria, beheaded outside the city in 311.

also.name: Venerable Peter the Silent of Galatia and Antioch
also.summary: A fifth-century hesychast born in Galatia who at twenty took to a tomb-cell at Antioch, living ninety-eight years on bread and water.

11-26.title: St. Alypius the Stylite of Adrianopolis
11-26.summary: A seventh-century deacon of Adrianopolis who lived fifty-three years upon a pillar in Paphlagonia, gathering both a monastery and a convent around its base.

also.name: Righteous Gideon, Judge of Israel
also.summary: The judge who delivered Israel from Midian with three hundred men and whose fleece bedewed with the morning dew is read as a figure of the Virgin.

also.name: Venerable James the Solitary of Syria
also.summary: A fifth-century recluse of Syria who lived shut up in a cave near Cyrrhus for fifteen years.

also.name: Venerable Chaeremon
St. Peter, Patriarch of Jerusalem
also.summary: A sixth-century patriarch of Jerusalem who took part in the deliberations against the Origenists.

also.name: Venerable Stylianos of Paphlagonia
also.summary: A sixth-century anchorite of Paphlagonia who from his youth was a friend and protector of children, often called upon for their healing.

also.name: Venerable Acacius of Mount Latros
also.summary: The young monk whose obedience unto death stands in the Ladder of St. John of Sinai as an icon of the monastic life.

also.name: Venerable Silas, Bishop of Corinth in Persia
Venerable Nikon the Metanoeite
also.summary: Tenth-century preacher of repentance who, going from town to town in the Peloponnese and Crete, called the recently reconquered Greeks back to the faith.

11-27.title: Great-martyr James the Persian
11-27.summary: A nobleman of fifth-century Persia who had denied Christ to please King Yazdegerd; reproached by his Christian mother and wife, he confessed openly, and was hacked apart limb by limb.

also.name: Venerable Moses
The Seventeen Monk-martyrs of India
also.summary: A community of fourth-century Christian ascetics in India slain together for the name of Christ by the pagans of the country.

also.name: Venerable Pinuphrius of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt who twice fled the abbacy laid upon him for the sake of obscurity.

also.name: St. Palladius of Galatia, Bishop of Helenopolis
also.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Helenopolis in Bithynia whose Lausiac History preserves for the Church the lives of the Egyptian and Palestinian fathers.

also.name: Venerable Romanus the Wonderworker of Cilicia
Venerable Nathaniel of Nitria
Venerable Palladius of Thessalonica

11-28.title: Martyr Stephen the New of Mt. St. Auxentius
11-28.summary: An eighth-century monk of Bithynia, one of the great confessors of the icons under Constantine V Copronymus, dragged through the streets of Constantinople by a mob and beaten to death.

also.name: Hieromartyr Acacius, Martyr Irenarchus, and the Seven Women-martyrs of Sebaste
also.summary: A bishop and a young man together with seven Christian women slain at Sebaste under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyrs Timothy and Theodore, Bishops of Tiberiopolis, with the company slain with them
also.summary: A great host of bishops, priests, and monks of Tiberiopolis in Macedonia slain together under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Saint Theodore, Bishop of Theodosiopolis
also.summary: A late-sixth-century bishop of Theodosiopolis (Erzurum) in Armenia.

also.name: St. Maurice, Emperor of New Rome, and his six sons
also.summary: The Roman emperor (582–602) and his six sons, slain together by the usurper Phocas, whose acceptance of death rather than betray his people is honored by the Church.

also.name: Martyrs Basil, Stephen, Gregory, and the three hundred and thirty-nine monks of Constantinople
also.summary: A great body of monastics slain in eighth-century Constantinople with Stephen the New for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Saint Romanos, Bishop in Macedonia

11-29.title: Martyr Paramonus and three hundred and seventy with him
11-29.summary: A Christian of Bithynia under Decius who openly rebuked the governor and his companions for forcing 370 Christians to sacrifice; all three hundred and seventy-one were slain together.

also.name: Venerable Nicholas, Archbishop of Thessaloniki
Hieromartyr Dionysius, Bishop of Corinth
also.summary: Second-century bishop of Corinth and the city's apostle in his day, whose pastoral letters were preserved by Eusebius.

also.name: Martyrs Philoumenos, Valerian, and Phaedrus of Ancyra
also.summary: Three Christians of third-century Ancyra each given his own torment — fire and nails for one, the sword for another, hot resin for the third.

also.name: Hieromartyr John, Presbyter in Persia
Six Holy Martyrs
Venerable Pitirim of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony the Great.

also.name: St. Tiridates III, King of Armenia
also.summary: The fourth-century king brought to faith by Gregory the Illuminator who in 301 made Armenia the first Christian kingdom.

also.name: Venerable Acacius of Sinai
also.summary: The young monk whose obedience unto death St. John of the Ladder set forth as a pattern of monastic submission.

also.name: Hieromartyr Abibus, Bishop of Nekresi
also.summary: A sixth-century Georgian bishop, one of the Thirteen Holy Fathers, slain by the Persians for breaking the fires of the Mazdean temples.

11-30.title: Holy Apostle Andrew the First-Called
11-30.summary: Brother of Peter and the first of the Twelve called by the Lord at the Jordan; he preached around the Black Sea, founded the Church at Byzantium, and was crucified on a slanted cross at Patras in Achaea.

also.name: St. Frumentius, Archbishop of Abyssinia, Equal-to-the-Apostles
also.summary: Fourth-century Tyrian merchant boy who, taken into the Ethiopian court, was sent back by Athanasius as the first bishop of Axum and the enlightener of Ethiopia.

also.name: St. Alexander, first Bishop of Methymna in Lesbos
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Lesbos and a father of the First Ecumenical Council.

also.name: St. Vakhtang I Gorgasali, King of Iberia
also.summary: Fifth-century king of Iberia who freed his land from Persian overlordship and built churches across his realm.

also.name: Sts. Peter I and Samuel I, Catholicoi of Georgia
also.summary: Two sixth-century catholicoi of the Georgian church who consolidated its independence from the see of Antioch.

12-01.title: Holy Prophet Nahum
12-01.summary: The seventh-century BC prophet of Elkosh whose three short chapters announce the fall of Nineveh — Assyria's pride brought down before the slow patience of God.

also.name: St. Onesimus, Archbishop of Ephesus
also.summary: An early-second-century successor of John the Theologian and his companions on the Ephesian throne.

also.name: Hieromartyr Ananias of Persia
also.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Persia who at his beheading under Sapor II beheld a ladder leading from earth to heaven.

also.name: St. Porphyrios, Patriarch of Antioch
also.summary: Late-fourth-century patriarch of Antioch and friend of John Chrysostom in the years before the latter's elevation to Constantinople.

also.name: St. Philaret the Merciful of Amnia
also.summary: Eighth-century landowner of Paphlagonia whose continual almsgiving impoverished his house, but whose granddaughter Maria was chosen bride of the emperor Constantine VI.

also.name: St. Anthony the New of Bithynia
also.summary: A ninth-century soldier of Bithynia turned monk at Kios who reposed near Constantinople after long ascetic struggle.

also.name: St. Theokletos, Archbishop of Sparta

12-02.title: Holy Prophet Habakkuk
12-02.summary: The prophet who watched on his tower for the answer of God to his question 'How long?' and received the word that became the keynote of Pauline theology: 'The righteous shall live by his faith.'

also.name: Virgin-martyr Myrope of Chios
also.summary: A maiden of Chios under Decius who, having given honorable burial to the martyr Isidore, was beaten to death by his guards for the lost body.

also.name: Hieromartyr Abibus of Edessa, the Deacon
also.summary: A fourth-century deacon of Edessa burned at the stake under Licinius, whose body was found uncharred by the fire.

also.name: Saints John, Heraclemon, Andrew, and Theophilus of Oxyrhynchus
also.summary: Four desert hermits of Oxyrhynchus in Egypt whose long life of stillness was discovered to the world only at their repose.

also.name: St. Jesse (Ise) of Tsilkani in Georgia
also.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Tsilkani, one of the Thirteen Syrian Fathers who came up from Antioch into Iberia.

also.name: St. Solomon, Archbishop of Ephesus

12-03.title: Holy Prophet Zephaniah
12-03.summary: A prophet of Judah under King Josiah who proclaimed the Day of the Lord and called the daughter of Zion to rejoice, for the Lord her God was in her midst.

also.name: Martyrs Agapius, Seleucus, Mamas, Indus, Domna, Glycerius, and forty companions
Venerable Theodoulos the Stylite
also.summary: A fifth-century patrician of Constantinople who renounced his rank under Theodosius the Younger and stood thirty years on a pillar near Ephesus.

also.name: Venerable Theodoulos of Cyprus
St. John the Silent, Bishop of Colonia
also.summary: A sixth-century bishop of Colonia in Armenia who at twenty-eight forsook his see for the silence of Mar Saba and lived seventy-six years there.

also.name: Hieromartyr Theodore I, Patriarch of Alexandria
also.summary: Early-seventh-century patriarch of Alexandria slain by the Persians for refusing the worship of fire.

12-04.title: Great-martyr Barbara
12-04.summary: A noble maiden of third-century Heliopolis whose pagan father, finding she had received baptism while he was away, denounced her himself and beheaded her with his own hand — and was struck dead by lightning as he came down the mountain.

also.name: St. John of Damascus
also.summary: Eighth-century monk of St. Sabbas, defender of the icons, and author of the Octoechos.

also.name: Commemoration of the Twelve Minor Prophets
Apostle Crispus of the Seventy, Bishop of Chalcedon
also.summary: An early disciple of Paul named in First Corinthians, the ruler of the Corinthian synagogue whom the apostle himself baptized.

also.name: Martyrs Christodulus and Christodula
St. John, Bishop of Polybotum in Phrygia
also.summary: An eighth-century bishop and wonderworker of Phrygia who at the second iconoclast council under Constantine V refused to deny the icons.

12-05.title: St. Sabbas the Sanctified
12-05.summary: The sixth-century father of Palestinian monasticism, founder of the Great Lavra in the Judean wilderness — the cradle of the Typikon that still shapes the daily services of the Orthodox Church.

also.name: Martyr Anastasius the Fuller of Salona
also.summary: A Roman fuller of Salona in Dalmatia who painted a cross on the door of his shop and was beheaded for refusing to deny Christ.

also.name: Venerables Gratus and Nonnus, monks
Martyrs Diogenes and Abercius
Venerables Karion and his son Zachariah of Egypt
also.summary: A father and son of fourth-century Egypt who together took up the desert life at Scetis after Karion's wife had refused to give up the world.

12-06.title: St. Nicholas the Wonderworker, Archbishop of Myra in Lycia
12-06.summary: The fourth-century hierarch whose secret almsgiving saved three sisters from sale and whose presence at the Council of Nicaea was marked by zeal even unto striking Arius. The patron of children, sailors, and the poor — beloved of the whole Church.

also.name: St. Theophilus of Antioch
also.summary: Second-century apologist and bishop of Antioch whose work To Autolycus is one of the earliest extant writings of the post-apostolic Church.

also.name: Martyr Niser
St. Nicholas, Bishop of Patara
also.summary: Late-third-century bishop of Patara in Lycia and uncle of Nicholas of Myra.

also.name: St. Abramius, Bishop of Cratea in Bithynia
also.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Cratea who labored long in the cause of Chalcedonian orthodoxy in his see.

12-07.title: St. Ambrose, Bishop of Milan
12-07.summary: The fourth-century hierarch who as the Roman governor of Milan was acclaimed bishop by the people before he was baptized, who taught Augustine the Scriptures, and who refused the Eucharist to Emperor Theodosius until he repented of the slaughter at Thessalonica.

also.name: Apostle Tychicus of the Seventy
also.summary: An early disciple of Paul named in his letters as the apostle's faithful messenger, who at length became bishop of Chalcedon.

also.name: Martyr Athenodorus of Mesopotamia
also.summary: An early-fourth-century monastic of Mesopotamia whose endurance through every torture under Diocletian shamed his judges.

also.name: Martyrs Neophytus, Dometius, Priscus, Martin, and Nicholas of Blachernae
Venerable Ammon (Amun) the Great, Father of the Monks of Nitria
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt, founder of the great monastic settlement of Nitria south of Alexandria.

also.name: St. Bassa of Jerusalem
also.summary: Fifth-century abbess at Jerusalem who founded the monastery of St. Menas.

also.name: Venerable Paul the Obedient of Cyprus
also.summary: A Cypriot monk whose perfect obedience became proverbial in the monastic tradition.

12-08.title: St. Patapius of Thebes
12-08.summary: A seventh-century Egyptian ascetic who fled the praise of men by going up to Constantinople, where he lived hidden as a healer of the sick at a small cell near the Blachernae church.

also.name: Apostles Sosthenes, Apollos, Cephas, Tychicus, Epaphroditus, Caesar, and Onesiphorus of the Seventy
also.summary: Seven of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles, sent through the early decades to the great cities of the empire.

also.name: Apostle Epaphras of the Seventy
also.summary: The Colossian disciple of Paul whom the apostle calls his fellow prisoner.

also.name: The Three Hundred and Sixty-two Martyrs of Africa
also.summary: Sixty-two priests and three hundred laypeople of fifth-century North Africa slain together by the Arian Vandals under Hunneric.

also.name: St. Sophronius I, Bishop of Cyprus
St. Damian, Bishop of Cyprus

12-09.title: The Conception by Righteous Anna of the Most Holy Theotokos
12-09.summary: Nine months and a day before the birth of the Mother of God, the Church remembers the conception by Anna after long barrenness — the answer of grace to the prayers of Joachim and his wife.

also.name: Holy Prophetess Anna (Hannah), mother of the Prophet Samuel
also.summary: The barren wife of Elkanah who at Shiloh promised her unborn son to the Lord and at his weaning brought him up to the tabernacle — sung over each year on this commemoration of barrenness undone.

also.name: Martyr Easios
St. Bassa, Igumenia of Jerusalem
also.summary: Fifth-century patrician of Jerusalem and abbess of the convent of St. Menas in the holy city.

also.name: Martyr Sositheus
St. Sophronius, Archbishop of Cyprus
Martyrs Narses and Isaac
Venerable Stephen the New Light of Constantinople
also.summary: A tenth-century ascetic of Constantinople whose holy life shone like a new lamp before the city in the days after the iconoclast struggle.

12-10.title: Martyrs Menas, Hermogenes, and Eugraphus
12-10.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Alexandria: Menas sent by Maximian to silence the Christians, who instead joined them; Hermogenes, the governor who came to judge him, likewise converted; Eugraphus, Menas's secretary. All three were beheaded together.

also.name: Martyr Hermogenes
Martyr Eugraphus
Martyr Gemellus of Paphlagonia
also.summary: A Christian of fourth-century Ancyra crucified under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

also.name: Hieromartyr Theoteknos and Martyrs Marianus and Eugenios
St. Athanasius, Bishop of Methoni in Messenia
also.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Methoni in the Peloponnese, a Catanian by birth and renowned for his pastoral care.

also.name: Venerable Thomas Dephourkinos of Mount Kyminas
also.summary: A tenth-century monk of Bithynia who at his great cell on Mount Kyminas received gifts of healing and clairvoyance.

12-11.title: St. Daniel the Stylite
12-11.summary: A fifth-century disciple of Simeon the Stylite who at Constantinople ascended his own pillar near the Black Sea coast, where he stood thirty-three years counseling emperors and crowds alike.

also.name: Martyrs Terence, Vincent, Emilian, and Bebaia
Martyrs Peter the Ascetic and Acepsimas of Persia
Monk-martyr Barsabas, Abbot of Ishtar in Persia, with ten companions
also.summary: A fourth-century abbot of a Persian monastery and ten of his community beheaded together under Sapor II.

also.name: Martyrs Aeithalas and Acepsius of Arbela
also.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Arbela in Assyria slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Mirax of Egypt
also.summary: A seventh-century Christian of Egypt who in a moment of weakness had embraced Islam, then was reconciled by repentance and crowned for his returning confession.

also.name: St. Nicephorus II Phocas, Emperor of New Rome
also.summary: Tenth-century Byzantine emperor whose victories pushed back the Arabs in the east and whose monastic friend Athanasius founded the Great Lavra of Athos under his patronage.

also.name: St. Luke the New Stylite of Chalcedon
also.summary: A tenth-century soldier turned stylite at Chalcedon, who in his lifetime came down only once from his pillar.

12-12.title: St. Spyridon the Wonderworker, Bishop of Trimythous
12-12.summary: A fourth-century shepherd-bishop of Cyprus whose simple faith at the Council of Nicaea is said to have confounded a philosopher and converted him by the parable of the brick — fire, water, and clay from the one tile, as the Trinity from the one God.

also.name: Hieromartyr Alexander, Archbishop of Jerusalem
also.summary: Third-century bishop of Jerusalem and friend of Origen who reposed in chains under Decius after long years as the city's shepherd.

also.name: Martyr Aitherius (Aetherius)
Venerables Amonathus of Pelusium and Anthus of Palestine
Holy Martyr-abbot John of Zedazeni
also.summary: A ninth-century abbot of the Zedazeni monastery in Georgia put to death together with his community by Saracen invaders.

12-13.title: Martyrs Eustratius, Auxentius, Eugene, Mardarius, and Orestes
12-13.summary: Five Christians of Armenia under Diocletian — Eustratius the noble scribe and four with him — whose endurance through the rack, the gridiron, and the pyre converted the very governor's wife.

also.name: Martyr Auxentius
Martyr Eugene
Martyr Mardarius
Martyr Orestes
St. Herman of Alaska, Wonderworker of America
St. Herman of Alaska
Virgin-martyr Lucia of Syracuse
also.summary: An early-fourth-century maiden of Syracuse in Sicily, beheaded under Diocletian after her own betrothed had denounced her, the patroness of those with diseases of the eyes.

also.name: Venerable Ares of the Egyptian desert
Venerable Arsenius the Ascetic of Mount Latros
also.summary: A Byzantine patrician who set aside his rank for the monastic life on the holy mountain near Miletus.

12-14.title: Martyrs Thyrsus, Leucius, Philemon, Apollonius, and Callinicus
12-14.summary: Five Christians of third-century Asia Minor under Decius — three killed at Caesarea in Bithynia, two at Alexandria — whose various crowns are joined in a single feast.

also.name: Martyr Leucius
Martyr Philemon
Martyr Apollonius
Martyr Callinicus
Martyrs Arianus, Philemon, and Theoctychus of Alexandria
also.summary: A governor of Alexandria converted by the patience of his prisoners under Diocletian and beheaded together with them and two of his guards.

also.name: Martyr Hypatius and thirty-six companions of the Thebaid
also.summary: A great company of believers of upper Egypt slain together for the name of Christ in the persecution of Diocletian.

12-15.title: Hieromartyr Eleutherius, Bishop of Illyria
12-15.summary: A second-century bishop of Illyrian Christians under Hadrian who was scourged, roasted on a gridiron, cast to lions, and finally beheaded — together with his pious mother Anthia and the soldier converted at his judgment.

also.name: Martyr Eleutherius of Constantinople
also.summary: A young chamberlain of fourth-century Constantinople in the days of Maximian, crowned for confessing Christ before the emperor.

also.name: Martyr Susanna the Deaconess of Palestine
also.summary: A fourth-century maiden of Palestine who under the name Joanna entered a male monastery and at her revelation was crowned for her confession.

also.name: Venerable Pardus the Hermit of Palestine
also.summary: A sixth-century muleteer of Palestine who, holding himself guilty of a child's accidental death, lived out his days in extreme penance in the desert.

also.name: St. Stephen the Confessor, Archbishop of Surozh in Crimea
also.summary: An eighth-century archbishop of Surozh in the Crimea, a fearless confessor of the icons under the iconoclast Constantine V.

also.name: Monk-martyr Bacchus the New
also.summary: An eighth-century Christian of Palestine who having denied the faith in his youth returned in penance at Mar Saba and was crowned at Jerusalem for his second confession.

also.name: Venerable Paul the New Ascetic of Mount Latros
also.summary: A tenth-century anchorite of the holy mountain near Miletus whose discipline drew many to him.

also.name: St. Tryphon of Pechenga, Enlightener of the Lapps
also.summary: Sixteenth-century missionary monk who founded the Pechenga monastery in the far north and brought the Lapps to baptism.

12-16.title: Holy Prophet Haggai
12-16.summary: The post-exilic prophet who in 520 BC stirred up Zerubbabel and Joshua the high priest to rebuild the temple, promising that the latter house would surpass the former in glory through the coming of the Messiah.

also.name: Martyr Marinus of Rome
Martyrs Promus (Probus) and Hilarion
St. Memnon, Archbishop of Ephesus
also.summary: A fifth-century archbishop of Ephesus who presided over the Third Ecumenical Council in his city in 431.

also.name: St. Modestus II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
also.summary: Seventh-century patriarch of Jerusalem who rebuilt the holy sites destroyed in the Persian sack of 614.

also.name: St. Simeon I, Patriarch of Antioch
Blessed Theophano, Empress of New Rome
also.summary: Ninth-century empress and first wife of Leo VI the Wise, who from her childhood lived in fasting and almsgiving and was canonized soon after her repose.

also.name: St. Nicholas II Chrysoberges, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Late-tenth-century patriarch of Constantinople, friend of the emperor Nicephorus II Phocas.

12-17.title: Holy Prophet Daniel and the Three Holy Youths Ananias, Azarias, and Misael
12-17.summary: The four young captives of Babylon: Daniel who in the lions' den was kept by God, and the three who in the fiery furnace sang the hymn that the Church still chants in the eighth ode of every canon.

also.name: Prophet Daniel and the Three Holy Youths
Monk-martyrs Patermuthius and Coprius of Egypt with Alexander the Soldier
also.summary: Two desert ascetics of Egypt and a soldier converted by their witness, all beheaded together under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Martyr Bacchus (Iakchos) of Triglia
Venerable Daniel the Confessor of Egypt
also.summary: A tenth-century Spanish dignitary who at length came to Egypt and lived out his days as a monk under the name Stephen-in-the-schema.

also.name: Sts. Athanasius, Nicholas, and Anthony of Vatopedi
also.summary: Three tenth-century disciples of Athanasius the Athonite and co-founders of the Vatopedi monastery on Mount Athos.

12-18.title: Martyr Sebastian and his companions at Rome
12-18.summary: A captain of the Praetorian Guard under Diocletian who secretly strengthened the imprisoned Christians, was discovered, shot through with arrows, and finally clubbed to death — the soldier-saint of the Western tradition.

also.name: Living-martyr Eubotius of Cyzicus
Martyrs Phocas and Hermylus
Hieromartyrs Zacchaeus the Deacon and Alphaeus the Reader of Caesarea
also.summary: A deacon of Gadara and a reader of Caesarea who together openly confessed Christ before the prefect and were beheaded.

also.name: Hieromartyr Modestus I, Archbishop of Jerusalem
also.summary: Late-fourth-century patriarch of Jerusalem, traditionally honored as a protector of domestic animals.

also.name: St. Florus, Bishop of Amisus
also.summary: A seventh-century bishop of Amisus on the Black Sea coast.

also.name: St. Michael Synkellos the Confessor
also.summary: Ninth-century synkellos of the patriarchate of Jerusalem who at Constantinople suffered with the brothers Theodore and Theophanes for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Saint Sophia the Wonderworker

12-19.title: Martyr Boniface of Tarsus
12-19.summary: A Roman official sent by his mistress Aglaida to bring back relics of the martyrs, who in Tarsus was so moved by their courage that he confessed Christ himself and joined them in the boiling pitch — Aglaida received his body and became a recluse.

also.name: Martyrs Boniface and Righteous Aglaida of Rome
Martyrs Meuris and Thea of Palestine
also.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Palestine slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Elias, Probus, and Ares of Egypt
also.summary: Three Egyptian Christians who under Maximian came up to Cilicia to comfort the imprisoned brethren and shared in their crowns.

also.name: Martyrs Polyeuctus of Caesarea and Timothy the Deacon
also.summary: Two early-fourth-century martyrs of the great persecution, Polyeuctus given over to the fire at Caesarea in Cappadocia and Timothy slain in Mauretania.

also.name: Hieromartyr Capito of Cherson
also.summary: One of the seven bishops sent in the fourth century to evangelize Cherson on the Black Sea, who reposed in peace there after great labors.

also.name: St. Gregentios, Archbishop of Zafar
also.summary: Sixth-century missionary bishop sent to the kingdom of the Himyarites in southern Arabia to consolidate the church after the persecution of Dhu Nuwas.

also.name: Sts. George the Scribe and Sabbas of Khakhuli
also.summary: Two eleventh-century monk-calligraphers of the Khakhuli monastery in Tao-Klarjeti in Georgia.

also.name: Venerable Elias of Murom, Wonderworker of the Kiev Caves
also.summary: Twelfth-century warrior of Russian epic who became a monk of the Kiev Caves at the end of his days.

12-20.title: Forefeast of the Nativity; St. Ignatius the God-bearer
12-20.summary: The eve of the eve of the Nativity. Also commemorated: the second-century bishop of Antioch, third successor of Peter, taken to Rome under Trajan and given to the lions, who wrote on the way the seven letters that mark the earliest pattern of the Church.

also.name: Hieromartyr Ignatius the God-bearer, Bishop of Antioch
Hieromartyr Ignatius the God-bearer of Antioch
St. John of Kronstadt
St. Philogonius the Confessor, Bishop of Antioch
also.summary: Early-fourth-century lawyer of Antioch who as bishop in the years before Nicaea defended the Church against the rising Arian party.

also.name: Venerable Paul of Latros
also.summary: Tenth-century anchorite of Mount Latros near Miletus whose pillar drew many disciples to settle around him.

12-21.title: St. Juliana of Nicomedia
12-21.summary: A noble maiden of fourth-century Nicomedia who refused the betrothal her pagan father had arranged, endured an interview with the demon in her prison cell, and was beheaded under Maximian.

also.name: Martyr Themistocles of Myra
