also.summary: A young shepherd of Lycia who at the soldiers' search for the Christian Dioscorus came forward in his place and was given over to torments and death.

also.name: The five hundred men and one hundred and thirty women martyred with Juliana
Venerable Macarius the Faster of Khakhuli
also.summary: Eleventh-century abbot of the Khakhuli monastery in Georgia, renowned for the severity of his fasting.

12-22.title: Great-martyr Anastasia the Deliverer-from-bonds
12-22.summary: A noble of fourth-century Sirmium who took her wealth to the prisons of Aquileia and Sirmium to ransom and serve the Christians there; after the slaughter she was bound, starved, and finally burned together with three sisters of her household.

also.name: Great-martyr Anastasia the Deliverer-from-bonds
Martyr Chrysogonus of Aquileia
also.summary: Catechist of St. Anastasia at Rome and Aquileia, beheaded for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyrs Theodota, Evodus, Eutychianus, and Zoilos
also.summary: Companions and household of St. Anastasia who shared in her sufferings and her crowning.

12-23.title: Ten Martyrs of Crete
12-23.summary: Ten Christians of various ages from the towns of Crete under Decius who, sent up to the governor's tribunal at Gortyna, were beheaded together at the place still called Hagioi Deka.

also.name: The Ten Martyrs by name: Theodulus, Saturninus, Euporus, Gelasius, Eunician, Zoticus, Pompeius, Agathopus, Basilides, and Evaristus
Martyr Schinon (Skinus)
St. Paul, Bishop of Neocaesarea
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Neocaesarea on the Euphrates whose hands had been disabled by the persecutors and who came so to the First Ecumenical Council.

also.name: St. Niphon, Bishop of Constantia in Cyprus
St. Chrysogonus of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Eparch of Thessaloniki who at length received the crown of martyrdom for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable David of Echmiadzin
also.summary: A seventh-century Armenian monk renowned for his ascetic life at the great see of his nation.

also.name: St. Naum (Nahum) of Ohrid, Equal-to-the-Apostles
also.summary: Disciple of Cyril and Methodius and tenth-century enlightener of the Bulgarians, founder of a great monastery on Lake Ohrid.

12-24.title: Eve of the Nativity of Christ; Virgin-martyr Eugenia of Rome
12-24.summary: A day of strict fast and the Royal Hours of the Nativity. Also commemorated: Eugenia, the daughter of the prefect of Alexandria, who disguised as a man entered a monastery and afterwards converted her family before her martyrdom in Rome.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Eugenia of Rome
Martyrs Philip, Protus, Hyacinth, Basilla, and Claudia, with St. Eugenia
also.summary: Companions of Eugenia of Rome — her father the prefect, two slaves who served her in the monastery, and others of her household — slain together for the faith.

also.name: Martyrs Sossios and Theokleios
Martyrs Castulus and Achaicus of Rome
also.summary: Castulus, chamberlain of Diocletian, buried alive in a pit for the Christians he hid in the imperial palace; Achaicus, his companion.

also.name: Venerable Betimius of Scetis
Venerable Aphrodisius of Palestine
Venerable Antiochus, Monk of Mar Saba
also.summary: A seventh-century monk of the great Lavra of Mar Saba whose Pandect provided the Christian East with its first systematic florilegium of patristic counsel.

also.name: Venerable Nicholas the Monk of Bulgaria
also.summary: An early-ninth-century soldier of Bulgaria who having endured the Bulgar persecution withdrew into the monastic life.

12-25.title: The Nativity in the Flesh of Our Lord, God, and Saviour Jesus Christ
12-25.summary: Today the Virgin gives birth to the One who is beyond all being, and the earth offers a cave to the Unapproachable. Angels with shepherds give glory, magi with a star journey on the way — for to us a Child is born, to us a Son is given.

also.name: The Adoration of the Magi: Melchior, Caspar, and Balthazar
also.summary: The wise men from the east who followed the star to the cave at Bethlehem and offered their gold, frankincense, and myrrh to the King, the God, and the One who was to die for us.

also.name: The Commemoration of the Shepherds
also.summary: The shepherds keeping watch over their flocks by night to whom the angel announced great joy and a multitude of the heavenly host sang glory in the highest.

12-26.title: The Synaxis of the Most Holy Theotokos
12-26.summary: The day after the Nativity is dedicated to the Mother of the One who was born. The Church gathers in her honor, acknowledging the woman through whom the great mystery of godliness was made visible.

also.name: Righteous Joseph the Betrothed
Holy King and Prophet David
Holy Apostle James, the Brother of the Lord
Commemoration of the Flight into Egypt
also.summary: The journey of the Holy Family from Bethlehem into Egypt at the angel's warning, that the word might be fulfilled: Out of Egypt have I called my Son.

also.name: St. Archelaus, Bishop of Harran
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Harran in northern Mesopotamia whose disputation with Mani is preserved in the Acts of Archelaus.

also.name: St. Zenon, Bishop of Maiuma in Gaza
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of the port-city of Gaza in Palestine.

also.name: Venerable Evarestus of the Stoudios Monastery
also.summary: A ninth-century monk of the great Stoudios monastery of Constantinople in the days of Theodore the Studite.

also.name: Hieromartyr Euthymius the Confessor, Bishop of Sardis
also.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Sardis exiled three times and at last beaten to death for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Venerable Constantine of Synnada
also.summary: A ninth-century convert from Judaism who became a monk and reposed in his monastery at Synnada in Phrygia.

12-27.title: Holy Protomartyr and Archdeacon Stephen
12-27.summary: The first of the seven deacons appointed by the apostles, full of grace and power; his face shone as the face of an angel before the Sanhedrin, and he was stoned by his own people, praying for them as he fell asleep.

also.name: St. Maximus the Confessor, Pope of Alexandria
also.summary: Third-century patriarch of Alexandria who took part in the synod that deposed Paul of Samosata for the heresy of dynamic monarchianism.

also.name: Martyrs Maurice, Photinus, and the seventy soldiers of Apamea
also.summary: A Christian officer of fourth-century Apamea in Bithynia and seventy of his men, given over together to wild beasts under Maximian.

also.name: St. Theodore I, Patriarch of Constantinople
Venerable Theodore Graptus the Branded
also.summary: A ninth-century confessor of the icons whose forehead was burned with iambic verses by the iconoclast Theophilus, who at last reposed in exile before his brother.

also.name: Venerable Luke, Abbot of the Monastery of the Deep Stream
also.summary: Tenth-century abbot of the Bithynian monastery near Triglia.

12-28.title: The 20,000 Martyrs of Nicomedia
12-28.summary: The great multitude of Christians of Nicomedia who, having gathered in the cathedral for the Nativity Liturgy under Maximian, were locked in by his order and burned together with the church about them.

also.name: Apostle Nicanor the Deacon, of the Seventy
also.summary: One of the seven deacons appointed by the apostles, slain at Jerusalem with two thousand others on the day Stephen was stoned.

also.name: Martyr Secundus, Enlightener of Spain
also.summary: An apostolic-age missionary to Spain whose evangelism by tradition began the Iberian church.

also.name: Martyr Ploutodoros
Venerable Babylas of Tarsus
Venerable Stephen the Wonderworker

12-29.title: The 14,000 Holy Innocents slain by Herod at Bethlehem
12-29.summary: The children of two years and under whom Herod, in his rage at the magi, slew at Bethlehem and in all its borders. The Church names them the first martyrs for Christ — slain not for confession but in His place.

also.name: Venerable Athenodorus the Leper
also.summary: A fourth-century disciple of Pachomius the Great in the cenobia of upper Egypt.

also.name: Venerable Benjamin of Nitria
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt whose body grew vast in his last sickness so that the door of his cell had to be broken open at his repose.

also.name: Venerable Marcellus, Abbot of the Monastery of the Unsleeping Ones
also.summary: A fifth-century abbot of the great akoimetai monastery near Constantinople, whose monks kept the round of psalmody unbroken day and night.

also.name: Venerable Thaddeus the Confessor of the Stoudios Monastery
also.summary: A ninth-century monk of the Stoudios who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under the second iconoclasm.

also.name: St. George, Metropolitan of Nicomedia
also.summary: Ninth-century metropolitan of Nicomedia, friend of Photius the Great and composer of many canons and troparia in the office books of the Church.

12-30.title: Martyr Anysia of Thessalonica
12-30.summary: A young Christian of Thessalonica under Maximian who was stopped on her way to the assembly by a soldier; refusing the imperial sacrifice, she was stabbed through with the soldier's sword in the street.

also.name: Apostle Timon of the Seventy, the Deacon
also.summary: One of the seven deacons appointed by the apostles, who labored in Bostra and reposed in the fire after preaching there.

also.name: Martyr Philoterus of Nicomedia and the six soldiers with him
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Nicomedia slain together with the soldiers who guarded him.

also.name: Hieromartyr Zoticus the Presbyter, Feeder of Orphans
also.summary: A fourth-century Roman noble who came to Constantinople with Constantine and there gave his wealth and his days to gathering and feeding the city's orphans.

also.name: St. Anysios, Bishop of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Late-fourth-century bishop of Thessaloniki, friend of Ambrose and defender of the Antiochene orthodoxy.

also.name: Venerable Theodora of Caesarea
also.summary: Eighth-century Cappadocian noblewoman who at her parents' urging took the monastic habit at Caesarea.

also.name: Venerable Theodora of Constantinople
also.summary: A tenth-century empress of New Rome who at the end of her life took the monastic habit.

also.name: Venerable Leo the Archimandrite

12-31.title: St. Melania the Younger of Rome
12-31.summary: A fifth-century noble of Rome who with her husband Pinianus sold their immense estates to ransom captives and endow monasteries, ending her days in a cell on the Mount of Olives.

also.name: Leavetaking of the Nativity of Christ
St. Abgar V, King of Edessa
also.summary: The first-century king of Edessa in Osroene who by tradition sent a letter to the Lord asking for healing and received the cloth of the Mandylion bearing His face.

also.name: The Ten Holy Virgin-martyrs of Nicomedia
also.summary: A company of consecrated virgins of fourth-century Nicomedia slain together under Maximian.

also.name: Martyr Olympiodora
Martyr Busiris
Hieromartyr Zoticus the Presbyter, Feeder of Orphans
also.summary: Fourth-century Roman who at Constantinople gave his wealth and his days to feeding and gathering the orphans of the city.

also.name: St. Anysius, Bishop of Thessaloniki
Venerable Gelasius of Palestine
also.summary: A fifth-century monk of Palestine whose obedience and wisdom are remembered in the sayings of the desert fathers.

also.name: Venerable Gaius
Venerable Sabiana, Abbess of Samtskhe
also.summary: An eleventh-century abbess in Georgia who led a great community of women in the Samtskhe region.

01-01.title: Circumcision of the Lord; St. Basil the Great
01-01.summary: On the eighth day from His birth the Lord was circumcised and given the name Jesus, as the angel had said. The Church remembers Him together with the fourth-century archbishop of Caesarea in Cappadocia, framer of the Liturgy still served on Sundays of Lent and on his feast.

also.name: Martyr Basil of Ancyra
also.summary: A fourth-century layman of Ancyra in Galatia beheaded under Julian the Apostate, distinct from the priest-martyr of March 22.

also.name: Martyr Theodotus
St. Gregory the Elder, Bishop of Nazianzus
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Nazianzus and father of Gregory the Theologian, brought into orthodoxy by his wife Nonna and his son.

also.name: St. Emilia, mother of Basil the Great
also.summary: Mother of Basil the Great, Gregory of Nyssa, Peter of Sebaste, Macrina the Younger, Naucratius, and Theosebia — by all reckoning the mother of saints.

also.name: Venerable Theodosius, Abbot of Tryglia

01-02.title: St. Sylvester, Pope of Rome
01-02.summary: The fourth-century Bishop of Rome who through prayer healed the emperor Constantine of leprosy and baptized him; he stood as a quiet pillar through the years of the First Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Saint Theodota, mother of Cosmas and Damian of Asia
also.summary: Third-century widow of Asia Minor who raised her physician sons in the faith and the unmercenary practice.

also.name: Martyr Sergius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
Martyr Theopistus
Hieromartyr Theogenes, Bishop of Parium
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Parium on the Hellespont, drowned in the sea for the name of Christ.

also.name: Hieromartyr Isidore, Bishop of Antioch
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Antioch persecuted by the Arian party.

also.name: Saint Amun of Tabennisi
also.summary: A fifth-century disciple of the cenobitic tradition founded by Pachomius in upper Egypt.

01-03.title: Holy Prophet Malachi; Martyr Gordius
01-03.summary: Malachi the last of the prophets of the old covenant, who foretold the messenger to come before the great and terrible day. Also commemorated: Gordius the centurion of Caesarea who under Licinius descended from his desert cave into the city's arena to confess Christ.

also.name: Martyr Gordius of Caesarea
Martyr Peter of Avlona in Palestine
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Palestine slain at Avlona near Samaria under Maximinus Daia.

also.name: Hieromartyr Titus, Bishop of Tomis
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Tomis on the Black Sea, slain for his pastoral labors among the Goths.

also.name: Venerable Meliton of Beirut
Venerable Peter of Atroa
also.summary: Ninth-century monastic father of Bithynia, surnamed the Standard-Bearer for his zeal in the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Venerable Acacius the Wonderworker of Mount Latros
Saint Thomais of Lesbos
also.summary: A tenth-century lay-saint of Lesbos who endured a long unhappy marriage with patience and almsgiving.

01-04.title: Synaxis of the Seventy Apostles; Forefeast of Theophany
01-04.summary: The Church names all seventy of the second rank of apostles whom the Lord sent out two by two before His face. Today also the eve of the eve of Theophany begins to ring out.

also.name: The Seventy Apostles
Venerable Theoctistus, abbot of Cucumo in Sicily
Saint Djan Darada the Ethiopian Eunuch
also.summary: The chamberlain of Queen Candace baptized in the chariot by Philip the Deacon on the road from Jerusalem to Gaza.

also.name: Martyrs Chrysanthus and Euphemia
Confessors Zosimas the Hermit and Athanasius the Commentarisius of Cilicia
also.summary: A desert anchorite and a prison-warden whom his prisoner converted, who together took up the ascetic life in Cilicia.

also.name: St. Theoprobus, Bishop of Karpasia in Cyprus
Venerable Apollinaria the Senator's Daughter
also.summary: A fifth-century noblewoman of Rome who slipped away from her family on pilgrimage and lived hidden as a male monk Dorotheos in the Egyptian desert.

also.name: Venerable Evagrius of Shio-Mgvime
also.summary: Sixth-century Georgian disciple of the Thirteen Syrian Fathers in the cave-monastery of Shio in Iberia.

also.name: Venerable Euthymius the Younger of Thessalonica
also.summary: Ninth-century monk of Mount Athos and founder of a monastic community at Peristera near Thessaloniki.

also.name: Venerable Timothy the Stylite of Kakhushta

01-05.title: Eve of Theophany — Strict Fast; Martyrs Theopempt and Theonas
01-05.summary: A day of strict fasting and the Royal Hours of Theophany, when the Great Blessing of Waters is appointed. Also commemorated: Theopempt the bishop and Theonas the converted magician, beheaded under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyrs Theopempt and Theonas of Nicomedia
Holy Prophet Micah
also.summary: An eighth-century BC prophet of Judah whose word the wise men quoted to Herod when they showed that the Christ would be born in Bethlehem of Judea.

also.name: Venerable Syncletica of Alexandria
also.summary: Fourth-century desert mother of Alexandria, the foundress of a community of consecrated virgins whose sayings stand among the apothegms.

also.name: Saint Apollinaria the Virgin of Egypt
also.summary: A fifth-century noblewoman of Rome who slipped away to the Egyptian desert and lived hidden as a male monk.

also.name: Saint Talida, Abbess of Antinoë
Venerable Phosterius the Hermit
Venerable Menas of Mount Sinai
also.summary: A sixth-century monk of Sinai whose body was found incorrupt years after his repose.

also.name: Venerable Gregory of Crete

01-06.title: The Holy Theophany of Our Lord, God, and Saviour Jesus Christ
01-06.summary: Today the Lord descended into the waters of the Jordan to be baptized by John; the heavens opened, the Spirit came down as a dove, and the Father proclaimed His Son. The Trinity was made manifest, and the waters were sanctified for our regeneration.

also.name: Venerable Evagrius of Nitria
also.summary: A fourth-century monk of the Egyptian desert and disciple of Macarius the Great whose treatises shaped the inner life of Eastern monasticism.

also.name: Venerable-martyr George the Persian

01-07.title: The Synaxis of the Holy and Glorious Prophet, Forerunner, and Baptist John
01-07.summary: The day after Theophany is given to the one whose hand baptized the Lord — the friend of the Bridegroom, greater than whom no man born of woman has arisen.

also.name: Saint Julian the Deacon of Aegina
also.summary: Fourth-century deacon ordained at Athens whose preaching baptized many before he withdrew to a hermitage near Lake Maggiore in Italy.

01-08.title: St. George the Chozebite; St. Domnica
01-08.summary: George the seventh-century abbot of the Chozeba monastery in the Wadi Kelt above the Jordan, and Domnica the Constantinopolitan ascetic of the fourth century who passed her last sixty years in silence at a small chapel by the harbor.

also.name: Holy Prophet Shemaiah
also.summary: A tenth-century BC prophet of Judah who warned Rehoboam against war with the northern tribes after the kingdom's division.

also.name: Martyrs Julian and Basilissa of Antinoe, with their household
also.summary: A young couple of fourth-century Egypt who by mutual vow lived as brother and sister and turned their house into a refuge before all were crowned together.

also.name: Hieromartyr Carterius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
also.summary: A teacher of fourth-century Caesarea slain under Diocletian for his confession.

also.name: Hieromartyr Theophilus the Deacon and Martyr Helladius of Libya
St. Atticus, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Constantinople who at length reconciled the followers of John Chrysostom to the see.

also.name: Venerable Theodore, founder of the Monastery of Chora
also.summary: A sixth-century founder of the Chora monastery at the walls of Constantinople, whose church holds some of the finest Byzantine mosaics.

also.name: Saint Cyrus (Kyros), Patriarch of Constantinople
Martyr Abo of Tiflis the Perfumer
also.summary: An eighth-century Arab perfumer of Baghdad who converted in Georgia and was beheaded at Tbilisi for refusing to return to Islam.

also.name: Saint Emilian the Confessor, Bishop of Cyzicus
also.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Cyzicus exiled by the iconoclast Leo V the Armenian.

also.name: Saint Gregory the Wise of Ohrid
also.summary: Bishop of Ohrid in Moesia in the early eleventh century.

01-09.title: Martyr Polyeuctus of Melitene in Armenia
01-09.summary: A Roman officer of third-century Melitene whose conversion came suddenly through the witness of his Christian friend Nearchus; he was beheaded for tearing up the imperial edict.

also.name: St. Philip the Confessor, Metropolitan of Moscow
Martyr Antonina of Nicomedia
also.summary: An early martyr of Nicomedia drowned in the sea for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Lawrence of the Arena
also.summary: A Christian slain by the pagans in the arena for refusing the sacrifices.

also.name: Saint Peter, Bishop of Sebaste in Armenia
also.summary: The youngest brother of Basil the Great, raised by his sister Macrina, who served as bishop of Sebaste at the end of the fourth century.

also.name: Saint Eustratius the Wonderworker of Tarsus
also.summary: A ninth-century abbot of Tarsus renowned for his ascetic discipline and his many miracles.

also.name: Venerables Basil and Gregory, Uncles of Eustratius of Tarsus

01-10.title: St. Gregory of Nyssa
01-10.summary: The fourth-century Cappadocian father, younger brother of Basil the Great, whose meditations on the soul's ascent to God remain among the deepest in the Christian tradition — 'every end is a new beginning.'

also.name: St. Theophan the Recluse
also.summary: Nineteenth-century Russian recluse-bishop and great translator of the Philokalia into Russian.

also.name: Blessed Theosebia the Deaconess
also.summary: Sister of Basil the Great and Gregory of Nyssa, a deaconess whose service in the church Gregory the Theologian sang of after her repose.

also.name: Venerable Ammon of Nitria
Saint Marcian the Presbyter and Economos of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century priest and economos of the Great Church whose almsgiving and care for the orphans made him a friend of the empress Pulcheria.

also.name: Venerable Domitian, Bishop of Melitene
also.summary: Sixth-century wonderworker-bishop of Melitene in Armenia, kinsman and adviser of the emperor Maurice.

01-11.title: St. Theodosius the Great, founder of cenobitic monasticism
01-11.summary: The sixth-century abbot of the great lavra near Bethlehem whose rule organized communal monastic life in Palestine and whose stand against the monophysites at the Marian shrine called the whole desert to Orthodoxy.

also.name: Martyr Mairus
Martyrs Peter, Severius, and Leucius of Alexandria
Venerable Theodosius the Wonderworker of Rhosus
also.summary: A fifth-century ascetic of Antioch and Rhosus on the Cilician coast renowned for miracles of healing.

also.name: Saint Stephen of Placidian
Venerable Theodorus and Archimandrite Agapius of Apamea
Venerable Vitalis of Gaza
also.summary: An aged monk of seventh-century Gaza who in scandalous appearance lived among the women of the city to call them to repentance.

01-12.title: Martyr Tatiana of Rome
01-12.summary: A noble deaconess of third-century Rome under Severus who endured the breaking of her teeth, the rending of her flesh, and the spectacle of the arena; the very lions licked her feet before she was beheaded.

also.name: Martyr Mertius of Mauretania
Martyr Philotheus of Antioch
Martyr Peter Apselamus of Eleutheropolis
also.summary: An early-fourth-century young Christian of Palestine, of a noble family of Eleutheropolis, burned for his confession.

also.name: Venerable Eupraxia of Tabenna
also.summary: Fourth-century maiden of Constantinople who entered a women's community at Tabenna in upper Egypt at the age of seven.

also.name: Saints Tygrius the Presbyter and Eutropius the Lector of Constantinople
also.summary: A priest and a lector of Constantinople who suffered for John Chrysostom at his second exile.

also.name: The Eight Martyrs of Nicaea
Venerable Elias the Hermit of Egypt
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt renowned for spiritual power and many miracles.

also.name: Saint Eulogia, mother of Theodosius the Cenobiarch
Venerable Theodora of Alexandria

01-13.title: Martyrs Hermylas and Stratonicus
01-13.summary: A deacon and his fellow soldier and friend at Belgrade under Licinius, whose ordeals strengthened each other rather than broke them; both were sewn into a net and cast into the Danube.

also.name: Martyr Peter of Anium
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian martyred at Hieropolis under Maximinus Daia.

also.name: Martyr Athanasius
Martyrs Pachomius and Papyrinus of Greece
Saint Jacob, Bishop of Nisibis
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Nisibis on the Persian frontier called the Moses of Mesopotamia, a hesychast of the wilderness and a father of the First Ecumenical Council.

01-14.title: Holy Equal-to-the-Apostles Nina of Georgia
01-14.summary: A young woman of Cappadocia who in the early fourth century brought the Gospel to Georgia by way of Armenia, healing the Iberian queen Nana and converting King Mirian. With her cross made of vine wood bound by her own hair, she is the apostle of the Georgians.

also.name: Holy Fathers slain at the monasteries of Sinai and Raithou
also.summary: Fourth- and fifth-century monks killed by Bedouins while at prayer in their cells.

also.name: St. Sava, First Archbishop of Serbia
Virgin-martyr Agnes of Rome
also.summary: A maiden of Rome at thirteen beheaded under Diocletian for refusing the betrothal her wealth had been arranged to seal.

also.name: Saint Joseph Analytinus of Raithu
also.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of the Sinai wilderness whose nights of prayer were attended by a heavenly light.

also.name: Venerable Theodulus, son of Nilus of Sinai
also.summary: A young Christian of Sinai taken captive by Bedouins with the other children of the slaughtered fathers, sought out and recovered by his father Nilus.

also.name: Venerable Stephen, Abbot of Chenolakkos Monastery
also.summary: Eighth-century abbot of a monastery near Chalcedon, exiled and beaten for his confession of the holy icons.

01-15.title: St. Paul of Thebes, the first hermit; St. John the Hut-dweller
01-15.summary: Paul, the third-century Egyptian who fled to the desert at sixteen and lived ninety years on its silence, fed by a raven in his old age. Also commemorated: John, the fifth-century Constantinopolitan noble who returned home as a beggar and lived in a hut at his father's gate, unrecognized.

also.name: St. John the Hut-dweller
Venerable-martyr Pansophius of Alexandria
also.summary: A third-century ascetic of Alexandria, son of the prefect Nilus, scourged to death for his Christian confession.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Charitina of Amisus
Saint Salome of Ujarma
also.summary: An Iberian princess and helper of Nina of Georgia, whose home was a refuge for the apostle of the Georgians.

also.name: Saint Isidore the Presbyter of Scete
also.summary: A fourth-century priest of Scete in Egypt whose sayings stand in the apothegms of the desert fathers.

also.name: Venerable Alexander the Ever-Vigilant
also.summary: Fifth-century founder of the akoimetai monastery in Bithynia, where the round of psalmody was kept day and night.

01-16.title: Veneration of the Honorable Chains of the Apostle Peter
01-16.summary: The chains by which Herod bound Peter and from which the angel loosed him were brought from Jerusalem to Rome and from there to Constantinople, where they continued to heal the sick.

also.name: Martyrs Speusippus, Eleusippus, Melapsippus, and their grandmother Leonilla of Cappadocia
also.summary: Three Cappadocian brothers — triplets — and their grandmother who brought them up in the faith, all crowned together in the second century.

also.name: Martyr-reader Danax of Avlona

01-17.title: St. Anthony the Great, father of monks
01-17.summary: The third-century Egyptian who, hearing the Gospel words 'sell what you have and give to the poor,' went out to the desert and there became the father of all monastic life — his life, written by Athanasius, shaped Christian asceticism for all time.

also.name: Martyrs Jonilla and her infant son Turbo
also.summary: A young mother and her child of second-century Cappadocia, crowned together in the persecution under Marcus Aurelius.

also.name: Saint Theodosius I the Great, Emperor of New Rome
also.summary: Late-fourth-century Christian Roman emperor who made the Nicene faith the religion of the empire and convened the Second Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Venerable Achilles the Confessor of Egypt

01-18.title: Sts. Athanasius and Cyril, Archbishops of Alexandria
01-18.summary: Two of the great pillars of Christology: Athanasius (4th c.) who through five exiles upheld the Son consubstantial with the Father, and Cyril (5th c.) who at Ephesus defended the title Theotokos against Nestorius.

also.name: Martyr Theodula of Anazarbus and her companions Helladius, Theodoulos, Boethius, Evagrius, and Macarius
also.summary: A maiden of fourth-century Anazarbus in Cilicia and her companions, all converted at her testimony and crowned together under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyr Xenia
Venerable Marcian of Cyrrhus
also.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Syrian wilderness near Cyrrhus whose silent austerity drew many to seek him out.

also.name: Venerable Silvanus the Ascetic of Gaza
Venerable Ephraim, Bishop of Mylasa in Caria

01-19.title: St. Macarius the Great of Egypt
01-19.summary: A fourth-century desert father of the Scetis, disciple of Anthony, whose Fifty Spiritual Homilies opened to the East the inner life of unceasing prayer — the lamp of the hesychast tradition.

also.name: St. Macarius the Great of Egypt
Virgin-martyr Euphrasia of Nicomedia
Hieromartyr Theodotus, Bishop of Cyrene
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Cyrene in Libya slain under Licinius for his confession.

also.name: Venerable Macarius the Younger of Alexandria
also.summary: Fourth-century desert father of the Cells of Nitria, disciple of Anthony and Macarius the Great.

also.name: Saints Maximos and Dometios, monks of Nitria
also.summary: Two fourth-century princes — sons of Valentinian — who slipped from the court to take up the desert life in the cells of Macarius.

also.name: Venerable Anton the Stylite of Martqopi
also.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father, one of the Thirteen who came up into Iberia, who founded monasticism on the holy mountain of Martqopi.

also.name: Martyr Anthony Rawah of Qoraisite
also.summary: Eighth-century Arab convert from Islam crowned at Damascus for refusing to deny Christ.

also.name: St. Arsenius, Archbishop of Corfu

01-20.title: St. Euthymius the Great
01-20.summary: The fifth-century Armenian-born monk of Palestine whose lavra outside Jerusalem trained Sabbas, Theoctistus, and a generation of saints, and whose stand at the Council of Chalcedon held the Holy Land in Orthodoxy.

also.name: St. Euthymius the Great
Martyrs Inna, Pinna, and Rimma of Scythia
also.summary: Three Gothic disciples of the apostle Andrew, frozen to death in the rivers of Scythia for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Eusebius
also.summary: A guard at the Roman court of Diocletian converted by the witness of the bishop Theopemptus and crowned with him.

also.name: Martyrs Bassus, Eusebius, Eutychius, and Basilides of Nicomedia
also.summary: Four noble companions of the imperial court at Nicomedia crowned together under Diocletian.

also.name: St. Leo I, Emperor of New Rome
also.summary: Fifth-century Christian Roman emperor in whose reign the great churches of the Theotokos were built at Blachernae and the Source.

also.name: Martyrs Thyrsus and Agnes of Constantinople
Saint Peter the Publican of Constantinople
also.summary: A sixth-century tax-collector at Constantinople struck by a vision of his deeds weighed against one loaf, who gave all to the poor and lived hidden as a slave.

01-21.title: St. Maximus the Confessor
01-21.summary: The seventh-century monk and theologian whose long defense of the two wills of Christ against the monothelites cost him his tongue, his right hand, and finally his life in exile in Lazica.

also.name: St. Maximus the Confessor
St. Maximus the Greek
Martyr Eugene of Trebizond and his companions Candidus, Valerian, and Aquila
also.summary: Four Christians of fourth-century Trebizond on the Black Sea coast who endured the fire and the rack for the name of Christ.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Agnes of Rome
also.summary: A noble maiden of Rome at the age of thirteen beheaded under Diocletian for refusing the betrothals her wealth had arranged.

also.name: Martyr Neophytus of Nicaea
also.summary: A young Christian of Nicaea slain under Diocletian at the age of sixteen.

also.name: Four Martyrs of Tyre
also.summary: Four believers of Tyre in Lebanon slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Apollonios of the Thebaid
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt and head of a great community of cells at the Mountain of Hermopolis.

also.name: Martyr Anastasius, Disciple of Maximus the Confessor
also.summary: Seventh-century disciple and amanuensis of Maximus the Confessor, exiled and reposing with him for the cause of the two wills of Christ.

also.name: St. Zosimas, Bishop of Syracuse
also.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Syracuse, raised against his will from the monastic life to the see.

01-22.title: Apostle Timothy of the Seventy; Martyr Anastasius the Persian
01-22.summary: Timothy, the disciple of Paul and first bishop of Ephesus, who at the great Diana-feast rebuked the city and was stoned by the mob. Also commemorated: Anastasius, a Persian convert and former magus, beheaded under Khosrau II.

also.name: Martyr Anastasius the Persian
The Martyrs of Adrianople
also.summary: A company of believers slain at Adrianople during the Bulgar invasions of the early ninth century.

01-23.title: Hieromartyr Clement of Ancyra; Martyr Agathangelus
01-23.summary: Clement the boy-bishop of Ancyra, brought before nine successive judges over twenty-eight years of confession before his final beheading at the altar. Agathangelus, his disciple, was crowned with him.

also.name: St. Ascholius, Bishop of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Thessaloniki, friend of Basil the Great, who baptized the emperor Theodosius the Great.

also.name: Venerable Eusebius the Recluse of Mount Coryphe
Venerable Salamanes the Silent of the Euphrates
also.summary: Fifth-century hesychast of the Euphrates region whose perfect silence even with his own family is remembered.

also.name: Venerable Mausimas the Syrian Presbyter of Cyrrhus
The Two Holy Martyrs of Parium

01-24.title: Blessed Xenia of St. Petersburg, Fool-for-Christ
01-24.summary: Wife of a Russian colonel widowed young, who gave her wealth to the poor and took up the unkempt clothes and behavior of a fool-for-Christ, wandering St. Petersburg for forty-five years in voluntary poverty and unceasing prayer. The patroness of the homeless and of those who pray for their husbands.

also.name: St. Xenia of Rome
also.summary: A fifth-century Roman noblewoman who fled her arranged marriage to found a convent in Caria under an assumed name.

also.name: Hieromartyr Babylas of Sicily with his disciples Timothy and Agapius
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Sicily and his two disciples martyred together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Paul, Pausirius, and Theodotian, brothers of Egypt
Hieromartyr Philippikos the Presbyter and his brother Martyr Barsimos
also.summary: A priest of Syria and his brother slain together in Persia for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Macedonius of Antioch
also.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Mount Silpius near Antioch whose long fast sustained him on barley meal mixed with water.

also.name: Saint Philon, Bishop of Karpasia in Cyprus
also.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Karpasia renowned as a wonderworker.

also.name: Saint Zosimas of Cilicia, Bishop of Babylon in Egypt

01-25.title: St. Gregory the Theologian, Archbishop of Constantinople
01-25.summary: The fourth-century friend of Basil the Great whose Five Theological Orations at Constantinople, given in the small church of the Anastasia, decided the dogma of the Trinity for the Second Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Venerable Castinus, Bishop of Byzantium
also.summary: Third-century bishop of the city of Byzantium, predecessor of the patriarchs of Constantinople.

also.name: Martyr Medula and her companions
Venerable Apollo of the Thebaid
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt and head of a great community at the Mountain of Hermopolis, renowned for many miracles.

also.name: Confessor Bretanion (Vetranion), Bishop of Tomis
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Tomis on the Black Sea who stood unmoved against the Arian Valens when the emperor came to him in his church.

also.name: Venerable Publius the Ascetic and his disciple Theodotos of the Euphrates
also.summary: A fourth-century Syrian noble who at his father's death gave away all his goods and founded a monastery, where Theodotos succeeded him.

also.name: Venerable Mares the Singer of Cyrrhus
Venerable Demetrius the Skevophylax of Constantinople
also.summary: Eighth-century keeper of the sacred vessels of the Great Church at Constantinople.

01-26.title: St. Xenophon, his wife Maria, and their sons Arcadius and John
01-26.summary: A senator of Constantinople in the fifth century, his wife, and their two shipwrecked sons — each presumed the others lost and entered monastic life independently, only to be reunited in a vision at the holy places of Jerusalem.

also.name: Martyrs Ananias the Presbyter, Peter the Prison-Keeper, and seven soldiers of Phoenicia
also.summary: A third-century priest and the guard converted by his witness, together with seven soldiers of his prison, all martyred together.

also.name: Two Holy Martyrs of Phrygia
Venerable Ammon the Hermit of Egypt
also.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony the Great.

also.name: Venerable Symeon the Ancient of Mount Sinai
Saint Paula of Rome
also.summary: Late-fourth-century Roman noblewoman, friend and patroness of Jerome, who at her widowhood took her daughters into the East and founded a community at Bethlehem.

also.name: Venerable Gabriel, Abbot of St. Stephanos in Jerusalem

01-27.title: Translation of the Relics of St. John Chrysostom
01-27.summary: Thirty years after his death in exile at Comana, the body of Chrysostom was brought back to Constantinople by his disciple-emperor Theodosius II, who laid his cheek against the coffin and asked forgiveness in the name of his mother Eudoxia.

also.name: Venerable Peter of Egypt
also.summary: A fifth-century disciple of Abba Lot in the Egyptian desert.

also.name: Saint Marciana (Euphemia), Empress of New Rome
also.summary: Wife of Justin I in the sixth century, who lies buried with her husband in the Church of the Holy Apostles at Constantinople.

also.name: Saint Demetrianos the Wonderworker, Bishop of Tamassos in Cyprus
St. Elias II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
Martyr Ashot I Kurapalates of Georgia
also.summary: Ninth-century prince of Tao-Klarjeti and founder of the Bagrationi house of Georgia, slain by the Arabs for the cause of his Christian people.

01-28.title: St. Ephraim the Syrian
01-28.summary: The fourth-century deacon of Edessa whose hymns gave the Syriac-speaking world its theology in verse, and whose Lenten prayer — 'O Lord and Master of my life…' — still bows the Orthodox to the ground each weekday of Great Lent.

also.name: Venerable Palladius the Hermit of Antioch
also.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Antiochene wilderness renowned for his miracles.

also.name: Venerable Isaac the Syrian, Bishop of Nineveh
also.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Nineveh who after a few months returned to the desert, leaving the Church his Ascetical Homilies that have shaped Orthodox prayer ever since.

also.name: Venerable James the Ascetic of Porphyreon
also.summary: A sixth-century hermit of Palestine who in penance after his fall from grace lived in a tomb until his death.

also.name: Venerable George Ugryn of Russia
also.summary: An eleventh-century page of Prince Boris of Russia slain together with his master, brother of Ephraim of Novotorzhok.

also.name: Venerable Ephraim, Founder of Novotorzhok Monastery
also.summary: An eleventh-century Hungarian-born monk of Russia who in mourning his brother George founded the Boris and Gleb monastery at Novotorzhok.

01-29.title: Translation of the Relics of the Hieromartyr Ignatius the God-bearer
01-29.summary: The bones of Ignatius, gathered after the lions of the Coliseum left only the harder parts, were carried in triumph from Rome to Antioch, the see he had ruled for forty years.

also.name: Martyr Chryse
Martyrs Sarbelus and Bebaia of Edessa
also.summary: A brother and sister of second-century Edessa who shared one confession and one martyrdom under Trajan.

also.name: Hieromartyr Barsimaeus, Bishop of Edessa
also.summary: Early-second-century bishop of Edessa who baptized Sarbelus and Bebaia and at length received the crown with them.

also.name: The Seven Martyrs of Samosata: Romanus, James, Philotheus, and their companions
Hieromartyrs Silvanus, Luke, and Mocius of Emesa
also.summary: A bishop, a deacon, and a reader of fourth-century Emesa slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Aphrahates the Persian, Hermit of Antioch
also.summary: The fourth-century Persian wisdom-writer whose Demonstrations are the earliest extant Syriac Christian writings.

also.name: Venerable Ascepsimus, Monk
Saint Ashot Kuropalates of Tao-Klarjeti

01-30.title: The Three Holy Hierarchs: Basil the Great, Gregory the Theologian, John Chrysostom
01-30.summary: Established by St. John Mauropous of Euchaita in the eleventh century after a vision of the three together, this feast settles the dispute of which of them was greatest by honoring them as equals — the joint patrons of Orthodox learning.

also.name: St. Gregory the Theologian, Archbishop of Constantinople
St. John Chrysostom, Archbishop of Constantinople
Hieromartyr Hippolytus, Priest of Antioch
also.summary: A third-century priest of Antioch who having left the Novatianist schism for the orthodox faith received the crown of martyrdom.

also.name: Hieromartyr Hippolytus of Rome and his companions
also.summary: Third-century bishop and martyr at Ostia near Rome, slain together with more than twenty fellow Christians under Claudius II.

also.name: Venerable Zeno the Hermit
also.summary: Fifth-century hermit of Antioch, a disciple of Basil the Great and later anchorite of the Antiochene wilderness.

also.name: Martyr Theophilus the New of Cyprus
also.summary: An eighth-century Christian of Cyprus martyred under the iconoclast emperors.

also.name: Venerable Kyriakos of Mar Saba
Saint Peter I of Bulgaria
also.summary: Tenth-century king of Bulgaria, son of Symeon the Great, who at the end of his reign embraced the monastic life.

01-31.title: Holy Unmercenaries and Wonderworkers Cyrus and John
01-31.summary: The Egyptian physician-monks of the early fourth century who from their cell near Alexandria healed the bodies and souls of all who came; under Diocletian they were beheaded with three holy women they had encouraged.

also.name: Martyr Tryphaena of Cyzicus
also.summary: A first-century maiden of Cyzicus given over to wild beasts for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Victorinus, Victor, Nicephorus, Claudius, Diodorus, Serapion, and Papias of Corinth
also.summary: Seven Christians of Corinth slain together for the name of Christ under Decius.

also.name: Martyrs Athanasia and her daughters Theoctista, Theodota, and Eudoxia of Canopus
also.summary: A mother and her three daughters of Egypt strengthened in their suffering by Cyrus and John and beheaded with them.

also.name: Martyrs Saturninus, Thyrsus, and Victor of Alexandria
Saint Julius of Aegina, the Missionary Priest
also.summary: An early-fifth-century priest of Aegina who with his brother Julian labored in evangelism through northern Italy.

also.name: Saint Athanasius, Bishop of Methone

02-01.title: Forefeast of the Meeting; Martyr Tryphon
02-01.summary: The eve of the Lord's Meeting in the Temple. Also commemorated: Tryphon, the gentle goose-herd of Phrygia who in his youth cast out a demon from a Roman princess and was beheaded under Decius at the age of eighteen.

also.name: Martyr Tryphon of Lampsacus
Martyr Theonas, with the Two Children
Martyr Karion
Venerable Peter of Galatia
also.summary: A fifth-century hermit of Antioch born in Galatia, whose ninety-nine years of asceticism Theodoret of Cyrus remembered.

also.name: Venerable Vendemanius the Hermit of Bithynia
Saint Anthony the Hermit of Georgia
Great-martyr Elijah of Heliopolis
also.summary: Eighth-century Christian of Damascus called Elias the New, slain by the Saracens for refusing Islam.

also.name: Confessors David, Symeon, and George of Mytilene
also.summary: Three brothers of Mytilene who in the eighth and ninth centuries gave their long episcopal and ascetic labors to the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Saint Basil I the Confessor, Archbishop of Thessaloniki
Saint Basil II the Synaxaristis of Thessaloniki

02-02.title: The Meeting of Our Lord, God, and Saviour Jesus Christ in the Temple
02-02.summary: On the fortieth day after His Nativity the Child was brought to the Temple, where the elder Simeon received Him in his arms and Anna the prophetess gave thanks — the old age of the Law receiving the youth of grace.

also.name: Martyr Agathadorus of Tyana in Cappadocia

02-03.title: Righteous Symeon the God-receiver and Anna the Prophetess
02-03.summary: The day after the Meeting honors the elder to whom it had been revealed that he would not see death until he had seen the Lord's Christ, and the widow of fourscore years and four who served God day and night in the Temple.

also.name: Simeon the God-Receiver
Anna the Prophetess
Holy Prophet Azariah
also.summary: A tenth-century BC prophet whose name signifies 'whom the Lord has helped.'

also.name: Martyrs Papias, Diodorus, and Claudianus of Perge
also.summary: Three Christians of Pamphylia slain together for the name of Christ under Decius.

also.name: Martyr Blaise of Caesarea in Cappadocia
also.summary: A third-century Christian of Caesarea remembered for the healing of bodily and spiritual blindness.

also.name: Martyrs Paul and Simon
Martyr Paul the Syrian
Martyrs Adrian and Eubulus of Caesarea
also.summary: Two Christians of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine who at Maximinus Daia's tribunal openly confessed Christ and were given over to the beasts.

also.name: Venerable Claudius the Ascetic

02-04.title: St. Isidore of Pelusium
02-04.summary: The fifth-century Egyptian ascetic and disciple of Chrysostom whose two thousand surviving letters of spiritual counsel show the desert's wisdom translated into the address of the world's needs.

also.name: Martyrs Jadorus and Isidore
Hieromartyr Phileas, Bishop of Thmuis
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Thmuis in Egypt slain together with the magistrate Philoromus under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyr Philoromus the Magistrate of Egypt
Martyr Theoctistus
Venerable John, Bishop of Irenopolis
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Irenopolis in Cilicia, one of the three hundred and eighteen fathers at the First Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Hieromartyr Abramius, Bishop of Arbela in Assyria
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian bishop slain under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

also.name: Saint Evagrius of Georgia
also.summary: Sixth-century disciple of Shio of Mgvime in the Iberian wilderness.

also.name: Venerable Nicetas of Pythiae
Venerable Iasimos the Wonderworker
Venerable Nicholas the Confessor, Abbot of the Stoudios Monastery
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the great Stoudios monastery at Constantinople who twice fled his see in the cause of the holy icons.

02-05.title: Martyr Agatha of Sicily
02-05.summary: A young noblewoman of third-century Catania whom the Roman governor Quintianus pursued for her wealth and beauty; refused, he had her breasts torn off and at last killed her in prison — the apostle Peter appeared to heal her in her cell.

also.name: Martyr Agatha of Sicily
Martyr Theodula of Anazarbus, with Helladius, Macarius, Boethos, and Evagrius
also.summary: A maiden of fourth-century Anazarbus in Cilicia and her companions, all crowned together under Diocletian.

also.name: Venerable Theodosius of Skopelos in Cilicia
St. Polyeuctus, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Tenth-century patriarch of Constantinople in the reigns of Constantine VII and John Tzimiskes.

also.name: Venerable Sabbas the New of Sicily
also.summary: Tenth-century abbot of a monastery in Sicily and southern Italy who at last reposed at Rome after long years of monastic labor.

02-06.title: St. Photius the Great, Patriarch of Constantinople; St. Bucolus of Smyrna
02-06.summary: Photius, the great ninth-century patriarch of vast learning who defended Orthodoxy against the Filioque and confirmed the mission to the Slavs. Also commemorated: Bucolus, the disciple of John the Theologian and second bishop of Smyrna.

also.name: St. Bucolus of Smyrna
Photios the Great, Patriarch of Constantinople
Blessed Xenia of St. Petersburg, Fool-for-Christ
Martyrs Theophilus, Saturninus, and Revocatus of Spain
Virgin-martyr Dorothea of Caesarea, with the lawyer Theophilus
also.summary: An early-fourth-century maiden of Caesarea in Cappadocia who at her execution sent roses from paradise to the mocking lawyer Theophilus, who was at once converted and crowned with her.

also.name: Martyrs Christina and Callista of Caesarea
Virgin-martyr Fausta of Cyzicus, with Evilasius and Maximus
also.summary: A thirteen-year-old maiden of fourth-century Cyzicus who at her endurance converted the priest Evilasius and the magistrate Maximus, all three crowned together.

also.name: Martyr Julian the Physician of Emesa
Venerable John of Lycopolis
also.summary: Fourth-century anchorite of the Thebaid renowned for his gift of prophecy.

also.name: Saint James the Ascetic of Syria
Venerables Barsanuphius the Great and John the Prophet of Gaza
also.summary: Sixth-century elders of Gaza whose surviving letters of spiritual direction are among the treasures of the desert tradition.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Martha, Mary, and Lycarion of Tanis

02-07.title: St. Parthenius, Bishop of Lampsacus
02-07.summary: A fourth-century bishop of the Hellespont known for his power over demons, whom he cast out from a young madman at the bidding of Constantine the Great.

also.name: Martyr Agathangelus of Damascus
The One Thousand and Three Martyrs of Nicomedia
also.summary: A great company of believers of Nicomedia slain together under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Theopemptus and the Six Martyrs of Phrygia
also.summary: Seven Christians of Phrygia given over together to the fire for their confession.

also.name: Martyr Audatus of Phrygia
Saint Mastridia of the Jerusalem Desert
Venerable Luke the Younger of Mount Steirion
also.summary: Tenth-century founder of the great monastery of Hosios Loukas in central Greece, renowned for his miracles and the gift of clairvoyance.

also.name: Saint Aprionus, Bishop of Cyprus
Venerable Peter of Monovatia
Venerable Sarapion of Cyprus

02-08.title: Great-martyr Theodore the General (Stratelates)
02-08.summary: A fourth-century officer of Heraclea on the Black Sea who under Licinius openly broke the city's idols, was crucified, pierced through with arrows, and beheaded — the elder of the two great Theodores.

also.name: Holy Prophet Zechariah
also.summary: Post-exilic prophet of Judah whose visions of the priest crowned with the diadem of kingship pointed forward to the Messiah.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Martha and Mary with their brother the monk Lykarion
Martyrs Nicephorus and Stephen
Martyr Pergetos
Saint Agathangelus, Bishop of Damascus
Venerables Philadelphus and Polycarp the Monastics

02-09.title: Martyr Nicephorus of Antioch
02-09.summary: A layman of Antioch who, having quarreled grievously with the presbyter Sapricius and afterwards humbled himself even to lying at his feet, took up the martyr's crown that Sapricius had spurned.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Marcellus, Philagrius, and Pancratius
also.summary: Three first-century disciples of the apostle Peter who labored as bishops in Sicily, Cyprus, and Taormina.

also.name: Martyrs Ammonius and Alexander of Solia in Cyprus
Virgin-martyr Apollonia of Alexandria
also.summary: An elderly deaconess of Alexandria in the persecution under Decius who at the pagan mob's threat of fire walked of her own will into the flames rather than blaspheme.

also.name: Venerable Romanus the Wonderworker of Cilicia
Venerable Shio of Mgvime
also.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father of Georgian monasticism, one of the Thirteen who came up into Iberia, who founded the cave-monastery of Shio.

also.name: Hieromartyr Peter of Damascus
also.summary: Eighth-century bishop and writer of Damascus whose tongue was cut out by the Saracens for the cause of orthodoxy.

02-10.title: Hieromartyr Charalampias the Wonderworker, Bishop of Magnesia
02-10.summary: A bishop of second-century Magnesia in Thessaly who at one hundred and thirteen years old endured many torments under Septimius Severus and converted his judges before being beheaded.

also.name: Hieromartyr Charalampias of Magnesia
Martyrs Porphyrius and Baptus, companions of Charalampias
Virgin-martyrs Ennatha, Valentina, and Paula of Palestine
also.summary: Three maidens of fourth-century Palestine slain together at Caesarea for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Zeno the Postman of Antioch
also.summary: A fourth-century courier of the emperor Valens who at his discharge sold his goods and retired into a tomb-cell near Antioch for forty years of prayer.

also.name: Saint Anastasius II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
Saint Anna of Novgorod
also.summary: Swedish princess Ingegerd, wife of Yaroslav the Wise of Kiev, who at the end of her life took the monastic habit at Novgorod.

02-11.title: Hieromartyr Blaise, Bishop of Sebaste in Armenia
02-11.summary: A fourth-century bishop and physician who fled Diocletian's persecution into a cave where wild animals came to him for healing; arrested and tortured with wool-combs, he was finally beheaded.

also.name: The Seven Women-Martyrs and Two Youths with St. Blaise of Sebaste
also.summary: Followers of the bishop who at his witness confessed Christ and shared in his crown at Sebaste.

also.name: Hieromartyr Lucius of Adrianople
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Adrianople who suffered long imprisonment and at length reposed in chains under the Arian Constantius.

also.name: Saint Theodora the Empress
also.summary: Ninth-century empress of New Rome, wife of the iconoclast Theophilus, who at her widowhood restored the holy icons through the synod of 843.

also.name: Hieromartyr Blaise (Vlasios) of Sklavaina
also.summary: Eleventh-century ascetic of Acarnania in Greece slain by Saracen pirates together with his fellow monks.

02-12.title: St. Meletius, Archbishop of Antioch; St. Alexis, Metropolitan of Moscow
02-12.summary: Meletius of Antioch (4th c.), who at his very enthronement preached against the Arians and was sent into three exiles. Alexis of Moscow (14th c.), bishop and statesman, healed the wife of the Tatar khan and held the Russian church together in dark days.

also.name: St. Alexis, Metropolitan of Moscow
also.summary: Fourteenth-century hierarch who healed the wife of the Tatar khan and held the Russian Church together.

also.name: St. Alexius, Metropolitan of Moscow
Martyrs Prima, Ampelius, Dativus, Plotinus, Saturninus, Fabius, Felix, and companions of Carthage
also.summary: A company of believers of fourth-century Carthage in north Africa slain together under Diocletian.

also.name: Saint Mary (Marinus) and her father Eugene of Alexandria
also.summary: A sixth-century maiden of Egypt who at the death of her mother took her father's name and lived as a monk Marinus in his monastery beside him.

also.name: St. Antony II, Patriarch of Constantinople
St. Sisinnios, Bishop of Ephesus

02-13.title: St. Martinianus the Hermit
02-13.summary: A fifth-century ascetic of Caesarea in Palestine who burned himself slightly to drive out the temptation a wandering woman had set in him, and fled to a deserted island where he reposed in peace.

also.name: Apostles Aquila and his wife Priscilla of the Seventy
also.summary: Companions of the apostle Paul named in his epistles, makers of tents at Corinth and afterwards teachers at Ephesus of Apollos.

also.name: Two Holy Martyrs, father and son
St. Timothy, Archbishop of Alexandria
also.summary: Late-fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria, brother of Peter II, who presided in the great see in the years after the Second Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Venerable Zoe of Bethlehem and the Virgin Photini
Saint Eulogius, Patriarch of Alexandria
also.summary: Sixth- and seventh-century patriarch of Alexandria, friend of Pope Gregory the Great.

also.name: Righteous Martin the Merciful

02-14.title: St. Cyril, Equal-to-the-Apostles, brother of Methodius
02-14.summary: The younger of the brothers from Thessalonica, whose love of learning earned him the surname 'the Philosopher' and whose translations gave the Slavs their Scriptures. He reposed at Rome at forty-two.

also.name: St. Peter II, Patriarch of Alexandria
also.summary: Fourth-century patriarch of Alexandria, successor of Athanasius the Great, who in his short tenure suffered exile under the Arian Valens.

also.name: Venerable Maron of Cyrrhus
also.summary: Fifth-century hermit of Syria whose disciples gave rise to the Maronite tradition.

also.name: Venerable Auxentius of Bithynia
also.summary: A fifth-century ascetic of Mount Auxentius near Chalcedon, attendant of the Fourth Ecumenical Council, after which the mountain bore his name.

also.name: Venerable Abraham, Bishop of Charres in Mesopotamia
Hieromartyr Philemon, Bishop of Gaza

02-15.title: Apostle Onesimus of the Seventy
02-15.summary: The runaway slave of Philemon at Colossae whom Paul met in his Roman imprisonment and sent back to his master as a brother; later bishop of Ephesus after Timothy, and crowned with martyrdom at Rome.

also.name: Martyr Major of Gaza
Venerables Paphnutius and his daughter Euphrosyne of Alexandria
also.summary: Fourth-century Alexandrian father and daughter whose paths of monastic asceticism, hidden from each other, were one in heart.

also.name: Venerable Eusebius the Hermit of Asikha in Syria
Saint Theognius, Bishop of Bethelia near Gaza

02-16.title: Martyrs Pamphilus of Caesarea and his twelve companions
02-16.summary: A presbyter of Caesarea in Palestine and pupil of Origen who established the great library there, and was beheaded with eleven others under Maximinus Daia after long imprisonment.

also.name: Saint Maruthas of Martyropolis
also.summary: Late-fourth- and early-fifth-century bishop of Sophene whose city of Martyropolis was named for the bones of the Persian martyrs he gathered for honorable burial.

also.name: St. Flavian I, Archbishop of Antioch
St. Flavian the Confessor of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople deposed by the Robber Synod of Ephesus and beaten so severely that he died of his wounds; vindicated at Chalcedon.

also.name: Venerable Flavian the Hermit
Saint Mary the New of Byzia in Thrace
also.summary: A ninth-century laywoman of Thrace whose patient suffering of her husband's cruelty is honored by the Church.

02-17.title: Great-martyr Theodore the Tyro (the Recruit)
02-17.summary: A young recruit of the Roman army at Amasea under Maximian who burned down the temple of the Mother of the Gods and was burned alive himself; commemorated also on the Saturday of the First Week of Lent for the miracle of the boiled wheat.

also.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Mariamne, sister of the apostle Philip
also.summary: Sister of the apostle Philip who labored with him and Bartholomew in the evangelization of Hierapolis and afterwards of Lycaonia, where she reposed in peace.

also.name: St. Auxibius, Bishop of Soli in Cyprus
also.summary: First-century disciple of the apostle Mark who at Mark's word went to Soli in Cyprus and was bishop there fifty years.

also.name: Martyrs Donatus, Romulus, Secundian, and 86 companions of Concordia
also.summary: A great company of believers near Venice slain together under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Theodoulos of Caesarea in Palestine
also.summary: A fourth-century slave of the prefect Firmilian who having visited the imprisoned Christians of Caesarea openly confessed Christ and was crucified.

also.name: Saint Mesrop Mashtots, Enlightener of Armenia
also.summary: Fifth-century Armenian doctor who with Sahak the Catholicos devised the Armenian alphabet and translated the Scriptures into the language of his nation.

also.name: Saints Marcian, Emperor of New Rome, and Pulcheria the Empress
also.summary: The fifth-century Christian Roman emperor and his consort, under whose patronage the Fourth Ecumenical Council was gathered at Chalcedon.

also.name: Venerable-martyr Theocteristus, Abbot of Pelekete Monastery
also.summary: An eighth-century abbot of a monastery near Prusa in Bithynia, scourged in the cause of the holy icons.

02-18.title: St. Leo the Great, Pope of Rome
02-18.summary: The fifth-century bishop of Rome whose Tome to Flavian became the dogmatic foundation of the Fourth Ecumenical Council at Chalcedon: two natures, perfect God and perfect man, in one Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Leo and Parigorius of Patara
Confessor Agapitus the Wonderworker, Bishop of Synnada in Phrygia
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Synnada who under Licinius confessed Christ before the magistrates and was returned in peace to his see.

also.name: Martyrs Victor, Dorotheus, Theodoulus, and Agrippa of Synnada
Martyr Piulius (Publius)
St. Flavian the Confessor, Archbishop of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople deposed and beaten at the Robber Synod of Ephesus, vindicated posthumously by the Council of Chalcedon.

also.name: Saint Blaise of Amorion on Mount Athos

02-19.title: Apostle Archippus of the Seventy
02-19.summary: Disciple of Paul named in his letter to Philemon ('our fellow soldier'), bishop of Colossae after Epaphras, who there worked through the earthquakes that twice destroyed the city's church.

also.name: Apostle Philemon and Martyr Apphia of Colossae
also.summary: The recipient of Paul's shortest epistle and his wife, both stoned to death at Colossae together with Archippus.

also.name: Martyrs Maximus, Theodotus, Hesychius, and Asclepiodota of Adrianopolis
also.summary: Four believers of Adrianople in Thrace slain together under Diocletian.

also.name: Venerables Eugene and Macarius, Confessors of Antioch
also.summary: Two priests of Antioch exiled by Julian the Apostate to the wilderness of Mauretania, where they ended their days in fast and prayer.

also.name: St. Mesrop Mashtots the Translator of Armenia
also.summary: Fifth-century Armenian doctor who devised the Armenian alphabet and translated the Scriptures into the language of his nation.

also.name: Venerable Rabulas of Samosata
Venerable Conon, Abbot of Palestine
Venerable Dositheus of Gaza
also.summary: Seventh-century disciple of Abba Dorotheus of Gaza whose perfect obedience to his elder is set forth in the Discourses.

also.name: Venerable Sophronios, Bishop

02-20.title: St. Leo, Bishop of Catania in Sicily
02-20.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Catania remembered for his miracle of binding the sorcerer Heliodorus, whom he led to his own execution in the flames at the bath-house and was preserved untouched.

also.name: Hieromartyr Eleutherius of Byzantium
also.summary: Second-century bishop of the city of Byzantium slain for the name of Christ in the days of Hadrian.

also.name: Martyrs Didymos, Nemesios, and Potamios of Cyprus
Martyr Eutropius
Hieromartyr Sadoc, Bishop in Persia, and the one hundred and twenty-eight with him
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian bishop and a great company of his flock slain together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

also.name: Venerable Bessarion the Great of Egypt
also.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Egypt and disciple of Anthony, whose sayings stand among the apothegms.

also.name: St. Agatho, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Rome whose synodical letter against the monothelites was received by the Sixth Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Venerable Cindeus, Bishop of Pisidia
Venerable Plotinus the Monk

02-21.title: St. Timothy of Symbola
02-21.summary: A sixth-century monk of Bithynia who as a young man was a disciple of the elder Theoctistus and afterwards became himself an abbot, founding the monastery of Symbola at the foot of Mount Olympus.

also.name: St. Eustathius, Archbishop of Antioch
also.summary: Fourth-century archbishop of Antioch and an early adversary of the Arians at the First Ecumenical Council, deposed afterwards and exiled to Trajanopolis.

also.name: Hieromartyr Severian, Bishop of Scythopolis
Venerables Andreas and Anatolios of Jerusalem
also.summary: Two fifth-century monks of Palestine, disciples of Euthymius the Great.

also.name: Saint Maximianus, Bishop of Ravenna
also.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Ravenna whose imperial patronage built the great basilica of San Vitale.

also.name: St. John Scholasticus, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Constantinople and canonist whose Synagoge of canons shaped the church's law.

also.name: St. Zachariah, Patriarch of Jerusalem
also.summary: Seventh-century patriarch of Jerusalem who saw his city taken by Khosrau II of Persia and was led away into Persian captivity.

also.name: St. George the Confessor, Bishop of Amastris
also.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Amastris on the Black Sea, hymnographer and defender of his people from a great Russian raid.

02-22.title: Finding of the Relics of the Holy Martyrs at the Gate of Eugenius
02-22.summary: In the reign of Arcadius the bones of unknown martyrs of earlier persecutions were uncovered near the Eugenian Gate of Constantinople; one of the martyrs appeared to declare himself the apostle Andronicus and his companion Junia.

also.name: St. Abilius, Bishop of Alexandria
also.summary: First-century bishop of Alexandria, second pastor of the see after the apostle Mark.

also.name: St. Papias, Bishop of Hierapolis
also.summary: Second-century bishop of Hierapolis in Phrygia, a disciple of John the Theologian whose Exposition of the Sayings of the Lord is partially preserved.

also.name: Martyr Synesius (Synetus)
Martyrs Maurice, Photinus, Theodore, and Philip of Apamea, with seventy soldiers
also.summary: A father and son and a company of soldiers of Apamea in Syria, all crowned together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Anthusa with her twelve servants
Saint Titus, Bishop of Bostra in Arabia
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Bostra renowned for his treatise against the Manichees.

also.name: St. Ariston the Wonderworker, Bishop of Arsinoe in Cyprus
Venerable Baradates of Antioch
also.summary: A fifth-century anchorite of the Antiochene wilderness who lived shut up in a wooden box exposed to the elements.

also.name: Venerables Thalassius and Limneus of Cyrrhus
The Nine Children-Martyrs of Kola in Georgia
also.summary: Nine Georgian children of the sixth century stoned by their pagan kinsmen for receiving baptism: Guram, Adarnase, Baqar, Vache, Bardzim, Dachi, Dzhuansher, Ramaz, and Parsman.

also.name: Venerable Athanasius the Confessor of Constantinople
also.summary: Ninth-century monastic confessor of Constantinople who suffered for the cause of the holy icons under Leo V the Armenian.

02-23.title: Hieromartyr Polycarp, Bishop of Smyrna
02-23.summary: The disciple of John the Theologian and bishop of Smyrna for sixty-five years, who under Marcus Aurelius answered the proconsul's demand to revile Christ: 'Eighty and six years have I served Him, and He has done me no wrong; how then can I blaspheme my King who saved me?'

also.name: Hieromartyr Polycarp of Smyrna
The Seventy-three Martyrs of Sirmium
also.summary: A great company of believers of Sirmium in Pannonia slain together under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyrs Clement and Thea
Righteous Gorgonia, sister of Gregory the Theologian
also.summary: A fourth-century laywoman of Cappadocia whose holy household and patient witness Gregory the Theologian sang in his great funeral oration over her.

also.name: Saint Alexander, Founder of the Monastery of the Unsleeping Ones
also.summary: Fifth-century Constantinopolitan abbot who established the akoimetai community where the round of psalmody was kept day and night.

also.name: Saints John, Antioch, Antoninus, Moses, Zebinas, Polychronius, Moses, and Damian, Ascetics of the Syrian Desert

02-24.title: First and Second Findings of the Honorable Head of John the Baptist
02-24.summary: The head of the Forerunner, hidden by Herodias in a clay vessel and lost, was rediscovered first by two pilgrims of Edessa near Sebaste, and a second time during the iconoclasm at Emesa.

also.name: Saint John the Harvester (Theristus) of Calabria
also.summary: A ninth-century Sicilian who escaped Saracen captivity to live as a monk in Calabria, where the wheat-fields are said to have been gathered for his community by his prayer.

02-25.title: St. Tarasius, Archbishop of Constantinople
02-25.summary: A ninth-century patriarch who under the Empress Irene presided over the Seventh Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, which restored the holy icons to the Church.

also.name: Martyr Alexander of Marcianopolis in Thrace
Martyr Anthony, burned alive
Saint Theodore the Fool-for-Christ
Hieromartyr Reginus, Bishop of Skopelos
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of the Aegean island of Skopelos, slain by Arian heretics under Constantius for his Nicene faith.

also.name: Saint Marcellus, Bishop of Aipeia in Cyprus
Venerable Paphnutius of Kephala
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of Egypt, contemporary and acquaintance of Anthony the Great.

02-26.title: St. Porphyrius, Bishop of Gaza
02-26.summary: A fifth-century bishop who through the long-prayed labor of years uprooted the entrenched paganism of Gaza, bringing down the great temple of Marnas by imperial decree after a famine breaks at his intercession.

also.name: Photini the Samaritan Woman
Martyrs Phota, Photis, Parasceva, and Cyriaca, sisters of Photini
Martyrs Victor Stratelates (Photinus) and Joses, sons of Photini
also.summary: Two sons of the Samaritan woman who under Nero shared in their mother's martyrdom for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Christodoulos, with the household of Photini
Martyrs Sebastian the Duke and Anatolius the Officer
also.summary: A duke and an officer of the Roman court who joined Photini in her confession and her crown.

also.name: Martyr Theoclitus the former Sorcerer
also.summary: A magician of the imperial household who at the witness of Photini was converted and shared in the company's crown.

also.name: Saint Nicholas of Katopinos

02-27.title: St. Procopius the Confessor of Decapolis
02-27.summary: An eighth-century monk of Asia Minor and defender of the holy icons under Leo the Isaurian, beaten in prison and exiled with his disciple Basil until the death of the emperor restored him.

also.name: Martyrs Julian and his disciple Eunos of Alexandria
also.summary: An aged Christian of third-century Alexandria, so weakened with gout that he had to be carried into court, and his disciple Eunos who shared in his crown.

also.name: The Holy Nine Martyrs of Rome: Abundius, Alexander, Antigonus, Calanus, Januarius, Makarios, Severianus, Titianus, and Fortunatus
Martyr Gelasius the Actor of Heliopolis
also.summary: Third-century actor of Heliopolis in Lebanon who while mimicking Christian baptism on the stage was suddenly converted in earnest and confessed Christ; the audience tore him to pieces on the spot.

also.name: Martyr Nesius
Saint Macarius, Bishop of Jerusalem
also.summary: Fourth-century bishop of Jerusalem in whose time and at whose direction Helena uncovered the True Cross and Constantine built the basilica of the Resurrection.

also.name: Venerable Thalelæus of Gabala in Syria
Saints Asclepius and Jacob, Monks of Cyrrhus
Saint Stephen the Monk of Constantinople
Saint Timothy the Monk of Caesarea

02-28.title: St. Basil the Confessor, disciple of St. Procopius
02-28.summary: The fellow sufferer and disciple of Procopius of Decapolis, who endured the same prisons and scourging for the icons and lived to teach in freedom after the great persecution had subsided.

also.name: Apostles Nymphas and Eubulus of the Seventy
also.summary: Two of the Seventy whom Paul names in his epistles, sent through the early decades to the great cities of the empire.

also.name: Hieromartyr Nestor of Magydos, Bishop of Perge in Pamphylia
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Magydos in Pamphylia who at Perge confessed Christ before the governor Pollio and was crucified.

also.name: Martyrs Macarius, Rufinus, Justus, and Theophilus of Rome
Nuns Marina, Kyra, and Domnica of Beroea
also.summary: Three Syrian women of fifth-century Beroea (Aleppo) who together founded a women's community and lived shut up in cells the rest of their days.

also.name: Hieromartyr Proterius, Patriarch of Alexandria, with six companions
also.summary: Fifth-century patriarch of Alexandria after the deposition of Dioscorus, slain by the monophysite mob in the baptistery of his cathedral.

also.name: Saint Germanus of Dacia Pontica
also.summary: Fifth-century desert father of the lower Danube in modern Romania.

also.name: Venerable John Cassian, Abbot of St. Victor in Marseille
also.summary: Fifth-century Latin abbot whose Institutes and Conferences brought the wisdom of the Egyptian desert into the western monastic tradition.

also.name: Venerable John Barsanuphius of Nitria
Saint George the Confessor, Bishop of Deifeltos
Saint Leo of Cappadocia
Venerable-martyr Theocteristus, Abbot of Pelekete in Prusa

02-29.title: Venerable John Cassian, Abbot of St. Victor in Marseille
02-29.summary: The fifth-century Latin abbot whose Institutes and Conferences brought the wisdom of the Egyptian desert into the western monastic tradition. The Church commemorates him only every fourth year, as the Lenten quiet of his life merits at long intervals a quiet feast.

also.name: Saint Germanus of Dacia Pontica
also.summary: Fifth-century desert father of the lower Danube, beside whom Cassian set out as a young man for Bethlehem and Egypt.

also.name: Venerable John Barsanuphius of Nitria
Saint George the Confessor, Bishop of Deifeltos
also.summary: Seventh-century bishop and confessor in the days of the monothelite controversy.

also.name: Venerable-martyr Theocteristus, Abbot of Pelekete near Prusa
also.summary: An eighth-century abbot of a monastery in Bithynia, scourged for the cause of the holy icons under Constantine V.

also.name: Saint Leo of Cappadocia

03-01.title: Virgin-martyr Eudokia of Heliopolis
03-01.summary: A Samaritan woman of Heliopolis in Phoenicia, immensely wealthy and famously beautiful, who at the witness of a Christian monk gave away all her possessions and entered a convent — beheaded for the faith under Trajan.

also.name: Martyrs Nestorianus, Tribimius, Marcellus, and Anthony of Perge
also.summary: Four Christians of Pamphylia slain together for the name of Christ under Decius.

also.name: Martyr Antonina of Nicaea
also.summary: An early-fourth-century maiden of Nicaea drowned in a marsh for her confession of Christ.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Domnina of Syria
Martyrs Antonius, Marcellus, Silvester, and Sophronius of Palestine
Martyrs Agapius, Nicephorus, and Charisius
Saint Synesius the Ascetic of Lysos in Cyprus

03-02.title: Hieromartyr Theodotus, Bishop of Cyrene
03-02.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Cyrenaica under Licinius who, scourged and racked, was finally beheaded after his judge had himself converted at the spectacle of his patience.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Nestor and Tribiminus of Perge
Martyr Troadius of Neo-Caesarea
Virgin-martyr Euthalia of Sicily
Confessor Cointus (Quintus) the Wonderworker of Phrygia
also.summary: A third-century confessor of Phrygia known for his many miracles, who at his release after long torture reposed in peace.

also.name: Martyr Hesychius the Senator of Antioch
also.summary: An imperial official of fourth-century Antioch under Maximian, drowned in the river for the name of Christ.

also.name: Hiero-Confessor Theodotus, Bishop of Cyrenia in Cyprus
Venerables Andronicus and Athanasia of Egypt
also.summary: A fifth-century husband and wife of Antioch who at the death of their children left the world for the Egyptian desert.

also.name: Venerable Agathon of Egypt
also.summary: Fifth-century desert father of Egypt whose sayings stand among the apothegms of the elders.

03-03.title: Martyrs Eutropius, Cleonicus, and Basiliscus
03-03.summary: Three companions in arms of the great-martyr Theodore the Tyro who, after his crowning, were arrested at Amasea and crucified together for the same confession.

also.name: Venerable Piama the Virgin
Hieromartyr Theodoretus of Antioch
also.summary: A fourth-century priest of Antioch beheaded under Julian the Apostate for refusing to surrender the church treasures.

also.name: Venerable Alexandra of Alexandria
Venerable saints Zenon and Zoilus, ascetics
Venerable Shio of Mgvime
also.summary: Sixth-century Syrian father of Georgian monasticism, one of the Thirteen who came up with John of Zedazeni into Iberia.

also.name: Sts. John IV and John V Chrysostom, Catholicoi of Georgia
also.summary: Two eleventh-century catholicoi of the Georgian church who guided their nation through troubled times.

03-04.title: St. Gerasimus of the Jordan
03-04.summary: A fifth-century desert father of the Jordan valley near Jericho who is the saint of the lion — he drew the thorn from a wild lion's paw and the beast became his servant until his death.

also.name: Saint Julian, Bishop of Alexandria
also.summary: A second-century bishop of Alexandria, eleventh in succession from the apostle Mark.

also.name: Martyrs Paul and his sister Juliana of Ptolemais, with Quadratus, Acacius, and Stratonicus
also.summary: A brother and sister of third-century Egypt and three companions converted by their witness, all crowned together under Aurelian.

also.name: Saint Gregory of Constantius, Bishop in Cyprus
Saint James the Faster of Phoenicia
also.summary: A sixth-century Syrian ascetic whose fast and discipline are remembered in the Lausiac History.

03-05.title: Martyr Conon the Gardener of Isauria
03-05.summary: A simple gardener of Isauria under Decius, baptized by Michael the Archangel and known for his quiet boldness; he was burned alive in a fire he calmly walked into himself.

also.name: St. Mark the Ascetic
Martyr Conon of Isauria and his father Nestor
also.summary: A first-century Christian of Isauria and his father, both beheaded together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Onisius (Onesimus) of Isauria
St. Theophilus, Bishop of Caesarea in Palestine
Martyrs Archelaus, Kyrillos, Photios, and Virgin-martyr Irais, with one hundred and fifty-two companions of Egypt
also.summary: A great company of believers of Egypt slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: Venerable Conon of Cyprus
Martyrs Eulogius and Eulampius of Palestine
Venerable Hesychius the Faster of Bithynia
also.summary: An eighth-century ascetic of Mount Olympus in Bithynia renowned for his fasting.

03-06.title: The Forty-two Martyrs of Amorion
