03-06.summary: Forty-two officers of Byzantine Amorion taken to Samarra in 845 after the city's fall to the Caliph Mu'tasim; after seven years of imprisonment they were beheaded together for refusing Islam.

also.name: Monk-martyrs Conon and his son Conon of Iconium
also.summary: A father and son of third-century Iconium beheaded together under Aurelian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Commemoration of the Finding of the Precious Cross by the Empress Helena
also.summary: The uncovering at Jerusalem in 326 by Constantine's mother Helena of the True Cross together with the nails.

also.name: Venerable Arcadius of Cyprus and his disciples Julian and Euboulos
also.summary: Fourth-century desert father of Cyprus and the two monks he trained.

also.name: St. Arkadios, Archbishop of Cyprus
also.summary: Sixth-century archbishop of Cyprus whose long episcopate spanned the reign of Justinian.

03-07.title: The Hieromartyrs of Cherson (Basil, Ephraim, Eugenius, Capito, Agathodorus, Etherius, Elpidius)
03-07.summary: Seven bishops sent from Jerusalem in the time of Diocletian to evangelize the Crimea who, in the fierce paganism of Cherson, were one by one stoned, dragged behind chariots, or beheaded.

also.name: Martyrs Codratus, Saturninus, and Rufinus of Nicomedia
Martyr Aemilian the Roman, with Jacob and Marianos
also.summary: Three companions of third-century Rome martyred together under Valerian.

also.name: Venerable Paul the Simple
also.summary: Fourth-century Egyptian farmer of sixty years who came to Anthony the Great for the monastic life and surpassed his master in obedience.

also.name: Saints Nestor and Arcadius, Bishops of Tremithous in Cyprus
Venerable Ephraim, Patriarch of Antioch
also.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Antioch who as governor of the east before his elevation had defended his city through earthquake and Persian assault.

also.name: St. Paul the Confessor, Bishop of Plousias in Bithynia
also.summary: A ninth-century bishop of Bithynia exiled for the cause of the holy icons.

03-08.title: St. Theophylact the Confessor, Bishop of Nicomedia
03-08.summary: A ninth-century friend of Tarasius of Constantinople and bishop of Nicomedia who under Leo the Armenian was driven from his see to thirty years of exile in a fortress on the Bosphorus for the icons.

also.name: Apostle Hermas of the Seventy, Bishop of Philippopolis
also.summary: One of the Seventy whom Paul names in his Letter to the Romans, by tradition the author of the Shepherd.

also.name: Martyr Dius
Martyrs Quinctilian and Capatolinus of Nicomedia
Venerable Dometius
Hieromartyr Theodoretus the Presbyter of Antioch
also.summary: A fourth-century priest of Antioch beheaded under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: St. Paul the Confessor, Bishop of Plousias
Righteous Tarasius the Wonderworker of Lycaonia

03-09.title: The Forty Holy Martyrs of Sebaste
03-09.summary: Forty Roman soldiers of the legion at Sebaste in Armenia under Licinius who, refusing the imperial sacrifice, were left naked on a frozen lake. One forsook them for the warm bath at the shore; one of the guards leapt in to take his place, and the forty crowns remained intact.

also.name: Martyr Urpasian the Senator of Nicomedia
also.summary: An imperial senator of fourth-century Nicomedia burned alive under Maximian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Saint Caesarius of Nazianzus
also.summary: Brother of Gregory the Theologian and physician at the court of Julian the Apostate, who at the brink of apostasy was reclaimed by his brother's prayers.

also.name: Confessor Philoromus of Galatia

03-10.title: Martyr Quadratus and his companions at Corinth
03-10.summary: Five Christians of Corinth under Decius — Quadratus chief among them — born in a forest where their mothers had hidden during the persecution, and beheaded together when they came of age to confess.

also.name: Martyrs Victorinus, Victor, Nicephorus and five companions of Corinth
Martyrs Codratus, Saturninus, and Rufinus of Nicomedia
Martyr Marcian
Saint Anastasia the Patrician of Alexandria
also.summary: A sixth-century noblewoman of Constantinople who fled the attentions of Justinian to the Egyptian desert and lived twenty-eight years disguised as a male monk.

also.name: Saint George Arselaites
Venerable Agathon of Aleppo
also.summary: An ascetic of the monastery of St. Symeon Stylites near Aleppo in Syria.

03-11.title: St. Sophronius, Patriarch of Jerusalem
03-11.summary: The seventh-century hierarch who at the surrender of Jerusalem to the Caliph Umar received from him the covenant protecting the holy places, and lived just long enough to grieve the loss before his repose.

also.name: Hieromartyr Pionius, Presbyter of Smyrna
also.summary: A third-century priest of Smyrna who, before he was burned in the same place where Polycarp had been crowned, taught the gathered crowd from his pyre.

also.name: Saints Asclepiades and Linus, with Martyr Sabina of Smyrna
Hieromartyrs Trophimus and Thalus, Priests of Laodicea
also.summary: Two priests of Laodicea crowned together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: Holy Syrian Martyrs
Venerable George, Abbot of Mount Sinai
also.summary: A sixth-century abbot of the Sinai monastery, brother of John Climacus.

also.name: Venerable John Moschos
also.summary: Seventh-century monk of Palestine whose Spiritual Meadow gathers the stories of the Eastern desert fathers.

also.name: Saint George the New, Wonderworker of Constantinople

03-12.title: St. Theophanes the Confessor of Sigriane
03-12.summary: A ninth-century chronicler-monk of Bithynia whose Chronographia preserves the events of his age, and who under Leo the Armenian was beaten and exiled to Samothrace for the holy icons, dying there of his torments.

also.name: St. Symeon the New Theologian
Righteous Aaron the High Priest, brother of Moses
also.summary: The first high priest of Israel, brother of Moses, who interceded for the people in the wilderness.

also.name: Righteous Phineas, grandson of Aaron
also.summary: The zealous priest of Israel whose covenant of peace is set forth in Numbers.

also.name: The Nine Holy Martyrs of Persia
Saint Cyrus (Abba-Cyr) of Alexandria
St. Gregory the Dialogist, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Sixth- and seventh-century bishop of Rome whose Dialogues taught the East of the Western desert and whose Liturgy of the Presanctified is appointed in Lent.

also.name: Venerable Symeon the Studite
also.summary: Tenth-century elder of the Stoudios monastery in Constantinople, spiritual father of Symeon the New Theologian.

03-13.title: Translation of the Relics of St. Nicephorus, Patriarch of Constantinople
03-13.summary: Nineteen years after his death in exile, the bones of the confessor Nicephorus were carried in triumph from his island monastery back to the city he had ruled before iconoclasm drove him out.

also.name: Hieromartyr Publius, Bishop of Malta and Athens
also.summary: Second-century bishop of Athens, the chief man of Malta in the Acts who received the shipwrecked Paul.

also.name: Martyrs Alexander, Dionysius, and Frontonus of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Thessaloniki of the imperial household, slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Christina of Persia
Venerable Euphrasia of the Thebaid
Martyr Abibus (Sabinus) of Hermopolis
Saint Marios (Marius), Bishop of Sebasteia
Venerable Theoktistos of Mar Saba

03-14.title: St. Benedict of Nursia
03-14.summary: The sixth-century father of Western monasticism whose Rule for the cenobium of Monte Cassino joined the desert's wisdom to the moderate work of community — 'pray and work,' in everything seeking the love of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Basil, Euphrasius, Florentius, and Alexander of Thessaloniki
Martyr Eustathius of Carrhae and his companions
also.summary: An eighth-century Christian of Carrhae in Mesopotamia and his companions slain together by the Saracens.

also.name: Venerable Euschemon the Confessor, Bishop of Lampsacus
also.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Lampsacus exiled by the iconoclast Theophilus for his confession of the holy icons.

03-15.title: Martyr Agapius and his seven companions at Caesarea
03-15.summary: Eight Christians of Caesarea in Palestine under Galerius Maximianus, who at separate audiences before the governor each refused to sacrifice and were given to the beasts together in the arena.

also.name: Apostle Aristobulus of the Seventy, first Bishop of Britain
also.summary: An apostolic-age disciple by tradition sent to the British Isles, identified with the Aristobulus whom Paul greets in Romans.

also.name: Hieromartyr Alexander of Side
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Side in Pamphylia who was put to death by fire for his confession.

also.name: Martyr Nicander of Egypt
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Egypt slain under Diocletian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Saint Hebarestes of Jerusalem

03-16.title: Martyr Sabinus of Egypt
03-16.summary: A nobleman of fourth-century Hermopolis under Diocletian who from a hidden cell in the marshes was betrayed by a beggar to whom he had given alms, and was drowned in the Nile for confessing Christ.

also.name: Apostle Aristobulus, Bishop of Britain
Hieromartyr Alexander I, Pope of Rome
also.summary: A second-century bishop of Rome who according to tradition added the institution-words to the canon of the Mass.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Eventius and Theodoulus, Presbyters of Rome
The Ten Martyrs of Phoenicia
Hieromartyrs Trophimus and Thalus, Priests of Laodicea
Martyr Papas of Lycaonia
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Lycaonia tortured and nailed to a tree under Magnentius.

also.name: Martyr Julian of Anazarbus
also.summary: A young Christian of fourth-century Cilicia sewn into a sack with serpents and cast into the sea, whose tomb at Antioch Chrysostom honored in homily.

also.name: Venerable Aninas the Wonderworker of Mesopotamia
Hieromartyr Romanos the Deacon of Parium

03-17.title: St. Alexis the Man of God
03-17.summary: A fifth-century noble of Rome who on his wedding day fled to Edessa and lived seventeen years as a beggar at a church door, then returned home unrecognized to spend another seventeen years as the family's anonymous servant under his father's stairs.

also.name: Righteous Lazarus, the Friend of Christ
also.summary: The friend of the Lord whom Jesus raised from the dead at Bethany, and who afterwards reposed bishop of Kition in Cyprus.

also.name: Martyr Marinus of Caesarea in Palestine
also.summary: A third-century Roman soldier of Caesarea Maritima at the rank of centurion, beheaded for his confession when his pagan rival exposed him.

also.name: Saint Ambrose, Deacon of Alexandria
also.summary: A fifth-century disciple of Didymus the Blind in the catechetical school of Alexandria.

also.name: Monk-martyr Paul of Cyprus
also.summary: An eighth-century monk of Cyprus burned alive by iconoclasts for refusing to trample on a holy icon.

also.name: Monk-martyr Paul of Crete
also.summary: An eighth-century monk of Crete slain under Constantine V the dung-named for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Venerable Theosterictus the Confessor of Pelekete
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the monastery of Pelekete near Prusa exiled for the holy icons.

03-18.title: St. Cyril, Archbishop of Jerusalem
03-18.summary: The fourth-century catechist of the newly baptized whose lectures from the Anastasis preserve the rites and teaching of the Jerusalem church; exiled three times by Arian opposition, restored by the Second Ecumenical Council.

also.name: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
St. Nikolai of Žiča and Ohrid
The Ten Thousand Martyrs of Nicomedia
Martyrs Trophimus and Eucarpion of Nicomedia
also.summary: Two soldiers of fourth-century Nicomedia under Maximian who at the witness of their victims converted and were crowned with them.

also.name: Venerable Ananias the Wonderworker of the Euphrates
Venerable Daniel the Monk of Egypt

03-19.title: Martyrs Chrysanthus and Daria
03-19.summary: A young convert of Roman Alexandria and the Vestal virgin sent to dissuade him; instead she became his wife in faith and they lived in chastity together. Both were buried alive in a sand-pit under Numerian.

also.name: Martyrs Claudius the Tribune, Hilaria, Jason, Maurus, Diodorus, and Marianus, companions of Chrysanthus and Daria
also.summary: A military officer of Rome, his wife, their two sons, and three more believers who at the witness of Chrysanthus and Daria converted and shared in their crown.

also.name: Martyr Pancharius of Nicomedia
also.summary: A noble courtier of fourth-century Nicomedia under Diocletian who at his Christian mother's letter returned to the faith he had renounced and was crowned for it.

03-20.title: The Holy Fathers slain at the monastery of St. Sabbas
03-20.summary: Forty-four monks of the Great Lavra of St. Sabbas martyred in 614 by a band of Saracens — burned in their cells or driven into a cave where their bones were left in heaps.

also.name: Righteous Abel, the first martyr
also.summary: The second son of Adam and Eve, slain by his brother Cain — by the Church reckoned the first witness of the blood of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Photina the Samaritan Woman
also.summary: The Samaritan woman at the well of Sychar to whom the Lord revealed Himself, who afterwards under Nero shared in the crown of her sons and sisters.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Alexandra, Claudia, Euphrasia, Matrona, Juliana, Euphemia, and Theodosia of Amisus
also.summary: Seven Christian maidens of fourth-century Amisus on the Black Sea coast martyred together under Maximinus Daia.

also.name: Martyrs Akyla the Eparch, Emmanuel, Rodian, and Lollian the Elder
Hieromartyr Tadros of Edessa with Michael the Sabbaite
also.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Edessa and a monk of Mar Saba slain together at Jerusalem by the Saracens.

also.name: Martyr Archil II, King of Georgia
also.summary: Eighth-century king of Iberia beheaded by an Arab emir for refusing Islam.

also.name: Venerable Fathers John, Sergius, Patrick, and others of the Monastery of St. Sabbas
Saint Nicetas the Confessor, Bishop of Apollonias in Bithynia
also.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Bithynia exiled and persecuted for his confession of the holy icons.

03-21.title: St. James the Confessor, Bishop of Catania
03-21.summary: A ninth-century monk of the Studion monastery in Constantinople, raised to the see of Catania in Sicily, who endured beating, imprisonment, and exile under Leo the Armenian for the holy icons.

also.name: Martyrs Philemon and Domninus of Thessalonica
Martyr Maria of Perge
also.summary: An early Christian of Pamphylia honored by the Church for her witness.

also.name: Saint Serapion, Bishop of Thmuis
also.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Thmuis in Egypt, companion of Anthony the Great and friend of Athanasius the Great, whose Sacramentary preserves the earliest extant Egyptian eucharistic prayers.

also.name: Venerable Serapion the Sindonite
also.summary: Fifth-century Egyptian father remembered for traveling clothed in nothing but a linen sheet, giving even his cloak to the poor.

also.name: Venerable Sophronius, Abbot of the Monastery of St. Theodosius
also.summary: Sixth-century abbot of the great monastery of Theodosius the Cenobiarch in the Judean desert.

also.name: St. Thomas, Patriarch of Constantinople

03-22.title: Hieromartyr Basil, Presbyter of Ancyra
03-22.summary: A fourth-century priest of Ancyra under Julian the Apostate who openly broke the imperial idols and was tortured slowly through seven days of progressively crueler ordeals before his beheading.

also.name: Virgin-Confessor Drosida of Antioch with five virgin-martyrs
also.summary: The daughter of Emperor Trajan who at her secret baptism led five fellow consecrated virgins to martyrdom; she gave up her own soul in penance for forty days at her father's discovery.

also.name: Martyrs Kalliniki and Vasilissa of Rome
Saint Isaac the Confessor, Founder of the Dalmatian Monastery
also.summary: Fourth-century anchorite who in coming to Constantinople rebuked the Arian Valens to his face and afterwards founded the great Dalmatian monastery there.

03-23.title: Martyr Nicon of Sicily and his hundred and ninety-nine disciples
03-23.summary: A second-century convert from a centurion's household in Naples who fled to Sicily, gathered 199 fellow soldiers as monks, and was beheaded with them all under the persecution of Decius.

also.name: Martyr Philetas the Senator, with his wife Lydia, their sons, and companions
also.summary: A senator of second-century Illyria and his household, all crowned together under Hadrian for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Dometius of Phrygia
also.summary: An early-fourth-century Christian of Phrygia slain under Julian the Apostate's restoration of paganism.

03-24.title: Forefeast of the Annunciation; St. Artemon
03-24.summary: The eve of the Annunciation. Also commemorated: Artemon, presbyter of Laodicea in the second century, ordained by Paul on his last journey through Phrygia and afterwards bishop in his old age.

also.name: Saint Artemon, Bishop of Seleucia in Pisidia
also.summary: A first-century bishop ordained by the apostle Paul on his journey through Pisidia.

also.name: Martyr Timolaus and the Seven Companions of Caesarea
also.summary: Eight Christians of fourth-century Caesarea in Palestine who came forward together at the trial of the bishop and were beheaded with him.

also.name: Venerable Zachariah the Recluse of Egypt
Venerable Martin of the Thebaid
Venerable Abraham of Mount Latros
Saint Thomas, Abbot of the St. Euthymius Monastery
Venerable Jacob the Confessor of Catania
also.summary: Eighth-century bishop of Catania in Sicily who under Leo the Isaurian suffered for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Saint Severus, Bishop of Catania

03-25.title: The Annunciation to Our Most Holy Lady the Theotokos and Ever-Virgin Mary
03-25.summary: Today is the beginning of our salvation, and the revelation of the eternal mystery. The Son of God becomes the Son of the Virgin, and Gabriel proclaims the good tidings of grace.

also.name: Saint Dismas the Good Thief
also.summary: The thief crucified at the Lord's right hand who confessed Him in his agony and heard the promise: 'This day shalt thou be with Me in paradise.'

also.name: Martyrs Pelagia, Theodosia, and Dula of Caesarea in Palestine
also.summary: Three Christian maidens beheaded together under Julian the Apostate for the name of Christ.

also.name: The Holy Martyr formerly an Executioner
also.summary: An imperial executioner who at the spectacle of his prisoner's patience confessed Christ on the spot and was slain with the very sword by which he had served.

also.name: Venerable Sennouphios of the Latomos Monastery in Thessalonica
also.summary: A ninth-century standard-bearer of the imperial guard guided in a vision to take the monastic habit at the Latomos monastery.

also.name: Venerable Timon the Hermit

03-26.title: The Synaxis of the Archangel Gabriel
03-26.summary: The day after the Annunciation honors the one who first declared the mystery — Gabriel, who told Zacharias of John, and the Virgin of her Son, and now is called the announcing Archangel of the great counsel of God.

also.name: Leavetaking of the Annunciation
Martyrs Codratus, Emmanuel, Theodosius, and forty companions
Hieromartyr Theodore, with the Deacon Irenaeus, of Pentapolis in Libya
Hieromartyr Irenaeus, Bishop of Sirmium
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Sirmium in Pannonia beheaded on the bridge of the Sava under Diocletian.

also.name: Hieromartyr Montanus the Presbyter and his wife Maxima of Sirmium
also.summary: A priest and his wife of Sirmium drowned together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Eutychius the Subdeacon of Alexandria
The Twenty-six Martyrs of Gothia
also.summary: Twenty-six Crimean Gothic Christians, including catechumens and clergy, burned together in their wooden church under king Jungerich.

also.name: Venerable Malchus of Chalcis in Syria
Venerable Stephen the Confessor, Wonderworker of Tryglia
Venerable Basil the Younger of Constantinople
also.summary: Tenth-century anchorite near Constantinople whose disciple Gregory wrote his life and the great vision of the Aerial Toll-houses.

03-27.title: Martyr Matrona of Thessalonica
03-27.summary: A Christian slave girl of Thessalonica under Maximian whose Jewish mistress, finding her at prayer in secret, beat her to death over the course of three days.

also.name: Holy Prophet Hanani (Ananias)
also.summary: A ninth-century BC prophet who rebuked King Asa of Judah and was imprisoned for his words.

also.name: Martyr Philetas the Senator with his wife Lydia and their sons
Martyrs Baruch and John
Venerable Eutychios
Venerable Cyricus (Quiricus) of Apros in Thrace
Martyrs Manuel and Theodosius
Saint John the Clairvoyant of Lycopolis
also.summary: A fourth-century anchorite of upper Egypt to whom Theodosius the Great and many other rulers sent for prophetic counsel.

also.name: Saint Paphnutius of Thebes
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of the Thebaid and disciple of Anthony the Great.

also.name: Saint Paul the Standard-bearer of Corinth

03-28.title: St. Hilarion the New, abbot of Pelecete
03-28.summary: An eighth-century abbot of the monastery of Pelecete on the Black Sea, defender of the icons under Leo the Isaurian and his successors, who repeatedly endured imprisonment and torture for the cause.

also.name: Apostle Herodion of Patras of the Seventy
also.summary: One of the Seventy whom Paul greets in Romans, kinsman of the apostle, who reposed bishop of Patras in Achaea.

also.name: Martyrs Priscus, Malchus, and Alexander of Caesarea in Palestine
also.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Caesarea who came of their own will to the magistrate and were given to the beasts for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Jonah and Barachisius of Persia, with eight companions
also.summary: Two brothers of fourth-century Persia and the companions who confessed Christ with them under Sapor II.

also.name: Venerable Hesychios the Theologian of Jerusalem
also.summary: Fifth-century priest of Jerusalem whose commentaries on Leviticus and the Twelve Prophets, and whose collected centuries on watchfulness in the Philokalia, became classics of the Eastern tradition.

also.name: Saint Stephen the Confessor, Wonderworker of Tryglia
Hieromartyrs George, Parodus, and Peter of Bulgaria, with Prince Enravota-Boyan
also.summary: A bishop, two priests, and a Bulgarian prince all crowned together in the ninth century at the hands of the Bulgar khan Malamir.

03-29.title: Hieromartyrs Mark of Arethusa and Cyril the Deacon
03-29.summary: A bishop in Syria under Julian the Apostate who in his youth had broken a pagan temple, and the deacon Cyril of Heliopolis; both were tortured by their enraged neighbors when Julian came to power, neither yielding to the smear of incense.

also.name: Martyrs Jonas and Barachisius of Persia, with nine companions
Venerable John of Egypt, the Anchorite
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt renowned for his gift of clairvoyance.

also.name: St. Diadochos, Bishop of Photiki
also.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Photike in Epirus whose Hundred Chapters on spiritual perfection stand at the head of the Philokalia.

also.name: Venerable Hesychios the Sinaite, Abbot of St. Catherine's
also.summary: Seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose two centuries On Watchfulness and Holiness shape the hesychast tradition.

also.name: St. Eustathius the Confessor, Bishop of Kios in Bithynia

03-30.title: St. John Climacus, author of The Ladder of Divine Ascent
03-30.summary: The seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose Ladder maps the soul's ascent to God in thirty steps — the work commemorated on the fourth Sunday of Great Lent and read in monasteries throughout the season.

also.name: Holy Prophet Joad of Bethel
Apostles Sosthenes, Apollos, Cephas, Caesar, and Epaphroditus of the Seventy
also.summary: Five of the Seventy named by Paul in his epistles, sent through the early decades to the great cities of the empire.

also.name: Saint Eubula, mother of Panteleimon
also.summary: A Christian noblewoman of Nicomedia who raised her son Panteleimon in the faith.

also.name: Venerable John the Hermit of Cilicia
Saint John II, Patriarch of Jerusalem
Saint John the Silent, Bishop of Colonia
also.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Colonia in Armenia who at twenty-eight set aside his see to live seventy-six years at Mar Saba.

also.name: Saint Zosimas, Bishop of Syracuse

03-31.title: Hieromartyr Hypatius, Bishop of Gangra
03-31.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Paphlagonia who took part in the First Ecumenical Council at Nicaea, and on his return journey was set upon by Novatian heretics and stoned to death.

also.name: St. Innocent of Alaska, Apostle of America
Righteous Joseph the Fair, son of Jacob
also.summary: The patriarch son of Jacob whose chastity in Egypt and providence over Pharaoh's grain saved his people — the Old Testament icon of patience under wrong.

also.name: Saint Acacius the Confessor, Bishop of Melitene
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Melitene in Armenia who endured imprisonment and torture under Decius.

also.name: Martyr Theophilos of Crete and his companions
Martyrs Menander, Sabinus, and the forty companions of Hermopolis
also.summary: Forty Christians of fourth-century Egypt slain together under Julian the Apostate.

also.name: Venerable Apollonius (Apollo) of the Thebaid
also.summary: A fourth-century desert father of upper Egypt and head of a great community on the Mountain of Hermopolis.

also.name: Hieromartyr Abdas, Bishop in Persia, with Deacon Benjamin
also.summary: A fifth-century Persian bishop and his deacon slain together under Yazdegerd I for destroying a fire-temple.

also.name: Venerable Hypatius, Abbot of Rufinus near Chalcedon
Venerable Blaise of Amorium and Mount Athos
Venerable Stephen the Wonderworker

04-01.title: St. Mary of Egypt
04-01.summary: The fifth-century desert ascetic whose long penitence in the wilderness beyond the Jordan is the icon of Lenten transformation; she lived forty-seven years unseen until the priest Zosimas found her, gave her communion, and buried her.

also.name: St. Justin Popović of Ćelije
Martyrs Hermes and Theodora of Rome
also.summary: A brother and sister of second-century Rome martyred together under Hadrian.

also.name: Saint Melito, Bishop of Sardis
also.summary: A second-century bishop of Sardis whose apology to Marcus Aurelius and homily On Pascha shaped early Christian thought.

also.name: Martyrs Gerontius and Basilides
Martyrs Alexander, Dionysius, Ingeniani, Panteros, Parthenios, and Saturninus
Martyr Polynikos
Righteous Ahaz
Saint Macarius the Confessor of Pelekete
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the monastery of Pelekete near Prusa, exiled for the holy icons under Leo V the Armenian.

04-02.title: St. Titus the Wonderworker
04-02.summary: A monk of the eighth century at a monastery near the Hellespont, known both for the gift of healing and for the meek silence that healed many more than his miracles did.

also.name: Martyrs Amphianus and Aedesius of Patara in Lycia
also.summary: Two brothers of early-fourth-century Lycia who at Caesarea in Palestine in turn rebuked the prefect at the public sacrifices and were crowned together under Galerius.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Theodora of Palestine (Theodosia of Tyre)
Martyr Polycarp of Alexandria
Venerable Stephen the Wonderworker of Ascalon
also.summary: An eighth-century anchorite of Palestine who at the monastery of St. Sabbas spent his final years in silence and prayer.

also.name: Saint George of Atsquri
also.summary: Ninth- and tenth-century bishop of Atsquri in Georgia.

04-03.title: St. Nicetas the Confessor, abbot of Medikion
04-03.summary: An eighth-century abbot of the monastery of Medikion in Bithynia, scourged and exiled to a fortified rock under Leo the Armenian for confessing the holy icons.

also.name: Martyr Elpidephorus
Martyrs Dius, Bithonius, and Galycus
Martyrs Cassius, Philip, and Eutychius of Thessaloniki
Virgin-martyrs Irene, Agapia, and Chionia of Aquileia
also.summary: Three sisters of Aquileia under Diocletian who in their parents' country house had hidden away copies of the Scriptures and refused to surrender them.

also.name: Martyr Ulphianus of Tyre
Virgin-martyr Theodosia of Tyre
also.summary: An early-fourth-century maiden of Tyre who at Caesarea in Palestine saluted condemned Christian confessors and was given over to the same fate.

also.name: Martyrs Evagrius, Benignus, Chrestus, Arestus, Kinnudius, Rufus, Patricius, and Zosima of Tomis
also.summary: Eight believers of Tomis on the Black Sea coast slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Illyrius of Mount Myrsinon
also.summary: An ascetic of the Peloponnese remembered for his quiet labors of prayer.

also.name: Venerable Joseph the Hymnographer of Sicily
also.summary: Ninth-century Sicilian monk in Constantinople whose canons fill the Menaion and Octoechos.

04-04.title: St. Joseph the Hymnographer
04-04.summary: The ninth-century monk of Sicily, exiled to Constantinople by the Arab conquest, whose hymns are appointed in every canon of the Octoechos and whose canons fill the Menaion through every saint's day.

also.name: Martyrs Agathopodes the Deacon and Theodulus the Reader of Thessalonica
also.summary: An aged deacon and a young reader of fourth-century Thessalonica drowned together under Diocletian.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Pherbutha of Persia, with her sister Mekadoshta and a maidservant
also.summary: A fourth-century maiden of the Persian court, sister of the patriarch Symeon Bar Sabbae, slain together with her household under Sapor II.

also.name: Venerable George of Mount Maleon
also.summary: A fifth- or sixth-century anchorite of the Peloponnese remembered for his austerity.

also.name: Venerable Publius the Egyptian
Hieromonk Zosimas of Palestine
also.summary: Sixth-century priest-monk of the Jordan wilderness who found St. Mary of Egypt and at her death buried her.

also.name: Venerables Theonas, Symeon, and Fervinus
Saint Platon the Confessor, Abbot of the Sakkudion
also.summary: Eighth- and ninth-century elder of the Stoudios monastery, uncle of Theodore the Studite, exiled and beaten for the cause of the holy icons.

04-05.title: Martyrs Theodulus the Reader and Agathopodes the Deacon
04-05.summary: A young reader and an aged deacon of fourth-century Thessalonica under Diocletian, bound together back-to-back and cast into the sea, where their bodies came ashore intact for burial.

also.name: Martyrs Claudius, Diodorus, Victor, Victorinus, Pappias, Serapion, and Nicephorus of Corinth
also.summary: Seven believers of Corinth slain together under Decius for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Zenon
Martyrs Maximus, Terentius, and Pompeius
Martyr Thermos (Fermus, Firmus)
Holy Noble Lady and her Servant Martyrs
Five Young Virgin-Martyrs of Lesbos
Virgin-martyr Theodora and Martyr Didymus the Soldier of Alexandria
also.summary: A young maiden of Alexandria sent to a brothel and rescued at the disguised entrance of Didymus the soldier, both beheaded together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Venerable Publius of Egypt
Venerable Mark the Anchorite of Athens
also.summary: A fifth-century recluse of the Libyan mountain whose long solitude was found out only at his death by Abba Serapion.

also.name: Saint Plato the Confessor, Abbot of the Stoudios
Saint Theodora of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Late-ninth-century nun of Thessaloniki whose body was found incorrupt and worked many wonders.

04-06.title: St. Eutychius, Archbishop of Constantinople
04-06.summary: A sixth-century patriarch who presided over the Fifth Ecumenical Council at Constantinople, was deposed and exiled by Justinian for theological reasons, and was triumphantly restored under Justin II.

also.name: Hieromartyr Archilias and Martyr Jeremiah of Rome
Venerable Platonis (Platonida) of Nisibis
Martyrs Platonides and her companions of Ascalon
Hieromartyr Irenaeus, Bishop of Sirmium
also.summary: An early-fourth-century bishop of Sirmium in Pannonia beheaded on the bridge of the Sava under Diocletian.

also.name: The One Hundred and Twenty Martyrs of Persia
also.summary: A great company of believers of Persia slain together under Sapor II for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Timothy and Diogenes of Macedonia
also.summary: Two believers of fourth-century Macedonia slain by the Arians in the doctrinal struggles after Nicaea.

also.name: Equal-to-the-Apostles Methodius, Archbishop of Moravia
also.summary: Ninth-century brother of Cyril, archbishop of Moravia, who together with him gave the Slavs their alphabet and the liturgy in their tongue.

04-07.title: St. George the Confessor, Metropolitan of Mytilene
04-07.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Lesbos who under the iconoclast Leo the Armenian endured beatings and exile rather than surrender the icons of the Church.

also.name: Saint Hegesippus the Chronicler
also.summary: Second-century Christian historian of Jewish descent whose Memoirs preserve early traditions of the Church of Jerusalem.

also.name: Hieromartyr Rufinus the Deacon with Aquilina and the two hundred soldiers of Sinope
also.summary: A deacon, a noblewoman, and two hundred soldiers of third-century Sinope crowned together under Decius.

also.name: Martyr Calliopios of Pompeiopolis
also.summary: A young Christian of Cilicia under Maximian crucified head downwards in mockery of his Lord.

also.name: Saint Serapion the Sindonite
also.summary: A fifth-century Egyptian father remembered for traveling clothed in nothing but a linen sheet.

also.name: Saint George, Patriarch of Jerusalem
Saint George the Standard-Bearer of Mytilene and Kherson
also.summary: A ninth-century bishop exiled for his confession of the holy icons.

04-08.title: Apostles Herodion, Agabus, Rufus, Asyncritus, Phlegon, and Hermes
04-08.summary: Six of the Seventy, all named in Romans 16: Herodion bishop of Patras and kinsman of Paul; Agabus the prophet of the famine; Rufus of Thebes; Asyncritus of Hyrcania; Phlegon of Marathon; Hermes of Philippopolis.

also.name: Martyr Pausilipus of Heraclea in Thrace
Monk-martyrs Josiah and Joseph of Mount Kharasam in Persia
also.summary: Two fourth-century Persian monks slain together under Sapor II for refusing the worship of the sun.

also.name: St. Celestine I, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Rome whose legates at the Council of Ephesus stood beside Cyril of Alexandria against Nestorius.

also.name: Saint Niphon, Bishop of Novgorod
Venerable Rufus the Recluse of the Kiev Caves

04-09.title: Martyr Eupsychius of Caesarea in Cappadocia
04-09.summary: A newly-married noble of Caesarea under Julian the Apostate who together with his bridal companions broke down the temple of Fortune; for this he was beheaded, and a famine which followed the deed lifted only at the emperor's death.

also.name: Martyrs Fortunatus, Donatus, twelve virgins, and six laymen of Sirmium
also.summary: A great company of believers of fourth-century Sirmium in Pannonia slain together under Diocletian.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Desan, Mariabus, Abdiesus, and two hundred and seventy companions of Persia
also.summary: A bishop, a priest, a deacon, and a great company of clergy and laypeople of fourth-century Persia slain under Sapor II.

also.name: Venerable Hieromartyr Bademus (Vadim), Archimandrite, with his seven disciples
also.summary: A fourth-century Persian abbot of a great monastery and seven of his community beheaded together under Sapor II.

also.name: Venerable Acacius, Bishop of Amida
also.summary: Fifth-century bishop of Amida in Mesopotamia who melted down the church's sacred vessels to ransom seven thousand Persian captives.

04-10.title: Martyrs Terence, Pompeius, Africanus, Maximus, Zenon, Alexander, and Theodore of Africa
04-10.summary: Seven Christians of Carthage in the third century under Decius who, refusing in turn each form of induced sacrifice, were beheaded together in the city's amphitheater.

also.name: Prophetess Huldah
also.summary: The prophetess whom King Josiah's emissaries consulted at the finding of the book of the law in the temple.

also.name: Martyrs Terence, Africanus, Maximus, Pompeius, and the thirty-six companions of Carthage
also.summary: Forty Christians of Carthage shut up with snakes and scorpions and at last beheaded under Decius.

also.name: Saint Miltiades, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Early-fourth-century bishop of Rome under whose pontificate Constantine issued the Edict of Milan.

also.name: Hieromartyrs James the Presbyter, Azadanes, and Abdicius of Persia
also.summary: A priest and two deacons of fourth-century Persia slain together under Sapor II.

04-11.title: Hieromartyr Antipas of Pergamum
04-11.summary: Disciple of John the Theologian and bishop of Pergamum mentioned by name in the Revelation as 'My faithful witness'; he was roasted alive in a brass bull-shaped idol under Domitian.

also.name: Martyrs Processus and Martinian of Rome
also.summary: Two prison-keepers of first-century Rome converted at the witness of Peter and Paul under Nero and crowned with them.

also.name: Hieromartyr Domninus, Bishop of Salona, with eight soldiers
also.summary: First-century bishop of Salona in Dalmatia and the soldiers of his guard, all martyred together under Domitian.

also.name: Saint Philip, Bishop of Gortyna in Crete
also.summary: A second-century bishop of Gortyna whose treatise against the Marcionites is mentioned by Eusebius.

also.name: Venerable Pharmuthius the Recluse of Egypt
Venerable-martyr Bacchus of Mar Saba
Venerable John, Disciple of Gregory of Decapolis
also.summary: A ninth-century monastic of Constantinople, unyielding confessor of the holy icons.

also.name: Venerables Tryfaini and Matrona of Cyzicus

04-12.title: St. Basil the Confessor, Bishop of Parium
04-12.summary: An eighth-century bishop of Parium on the Hellespont who refused to sign the iconoclast decree of the council of Hieria and was beaten and exiled, surviving to die in peace.

also.name: Hieromartyr Artemon, Presbyter of Laodicea
Martyrs Demas and Protion
Martyr Sabbas the Goth
also.summary: A fourth-century Christian of Wallachia drowned in the river Buzău for refusing to share in the pagan rites of his Gothic kinsmen.

also.name: Saint Isaac the Syrian, Abbot of Spoleto
also.summary: A sixth-century Syrian monk who came west to Spoleto in Umbria and founded a great cenobitic community there.

also.name: Venerable Monk-martyrs David, John, and Menas of Palestine
also.summary: Three monks of seventh-century Palestine slain together by the Saracens in the first decades after the conquest.

also.name: Venerable Anthusa of Constantinople
Venerable Athanasia the Wonderworker of Aegina
also.summary: Ninth-century abbess of Aegina who at her widowhood took up the monastic life with her companions.

also.name: St. Sergius II, Patriarch of Constantinople

04-13.title: Hieromartyr Artemon, Presbyter of Laodicea in Pisidia
04-13.summary: A second-century priest of Pisidia under Diocletian, an aged man of eighty-nine, who having destroyed pagan idols was given to two ravenous lions and instead tamed them.

also.name: Saints Theodosia the Princess and the Eunuch Gerontios
also.summary: A daughter of Emperor Hadrian and the chamberlain who served her, both honored as second-century saints.

also.name: Martyrs Dadas, Quinctillian, and Maximus the Readers
Martyr Crescens of Myra in Lycia
also.summary: A third-century Christian of Myra given over to the fire for his confession.

also.name: Hieromartyr Artemon of Laodicea
also.summary: A second-century priest of Laodicea in Syria slain for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Eleutherius and Zoilus of Persia
Martyr Theodosius
Woman-martyr Thomais of Alexandria
Saint Martyrius, Patriarch of Jerusalem
Saint Martin the Confessor, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Seventh-century bishop of Rome who at the Lateran council condemned monothelitism, was abducted by imperial troops, and reposed in exile at Cherson.

04-14.title: St. Martin the Confessor, Pope of Rome
04-14.summary: The seventh-century bishop of Rome who at the Lateran council condemned the heresy of monothelitism and was for this seized by imperial troops, brought to Constantinople, tried, and exiled to Cherson where he died of starvation.

also.name: Apostles Aristarchus, Pudens, and Trophimus of the Seventy
also.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul: Aristarchus of Thessalonica, Pudens the Roman senator, and Trophimus the Ephesian.

also.name: Martyr Ardalion the Actor of Rome
also.summary: A Roman stage-actor under Maximian who while mocking Christians on the stage was suddenly converted, climbed off the platform, and was given over to the fire.

also.name: Martyr Azat the Eunuch of Persia
also.summary: A fourth-century courtier of Persia and one of a thousand Christians slain together under Sapor II.

also.name: Martyr Thomais of Alexandria
also.summary: A young Christian wife of fifth-century Alexandria slain by her own father-in-law for refusing his evil desires.

also.name: Venerable-martyr Christopher the Sabbaite
also.summary: Eighth-century monk of the Lavra of Mar Saba slain by the Saracens for the cause of Christ.

04-15.title: Apostles Aristarchus, Pudens, and Trophimus of the Seventy
04-15.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul in his letters: Aristarchus the Macedonian of Thessalonica who shared Paul's Roman imprisonment, Pudens the Roman senator who hosted Paul at his house, and Trophimus the Ephesian who fell ill at Miletus.

also.name: Martyrs Basilissa and Anastasia of Rome
also.summary: First-century disciples of the apostles Peter and Paul at Rome who gathered the bodies of the martyrs for burial under Nero and at length received the crown themselves.

also.name: Martyr Sukia and his nineteen companions of Armenia
also.summary: Twenty believers brought from Georgia and Armenia to martyrdom in the days of the apostolic preaching in the east.

also.name: Hieromartyr Theodore and Martyr Pausilippus of Thrace
Martyrs Maximus and Olympiada of Persia
also.summary: A father and son of Persia slain together under Decius for the name of Christ.

also.name: Saint Leonidas, Bishop of Athens
also.summary: A third-century bishop of Athens slain together with eleven companions for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyr Crescens of Myra in Lycia
Martyr Sabbas the Goth of Wallachia
Saint Ephraim the Great of Atsquri
also.summary: Ninth-century Georgian monk and bishop of Atsquri.

04-16.title: Virgin-martyrs Agape, Irene, and Chionia of Aquileia
04-16.summary: Three sisters of Aquileia under Diocletian who in their parents' country house had hidden away copies of the Scriptures; on their refusal to surrender them, the elder two were burned and the third pierced with arrows.

also.name: Martyr Leonidas of Nea Epidavros and his companions
also.summary: A third-century Christian of the Peloponnese and his fellows slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Virgin-martyrs Charissa, Nika, Galina, Callista, Nunechia, Basilissa, Theodora, and Irene of Corinth
also.summary: Eight Christian maidens of Corinth slain together with Leonidas of Nea Epidavros.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Felix, Bishop of Spoleto; the Presbyter Januarius; and Martyrs Septeminus and Fortunatus of Lycaonia
Confessors Agatho, Eutychia, Cassia, and Phillippa of Thessaloniki
also.summary: Four believers of Thessaloniki imprisoned under Diocletian whose witness brought sisters Agape, Irene, and Chionia to their crown.

04-17.title: Hieromartyr Symeon of Persia, with the martyrs of Persia
04-17.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Seleucia and Ctesiphon under the Persian Sapor II, beheaded with a hundred and fifty fellow Christians the day after Pascha for refusing to gather tax from the Christians or worship the sun.

also.name: Hieromartyr Anicetus, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Second-century bishop of Rome whose meeting with Polycarp of Smyrna over the date of Pascha became proverbial of charity holding apart from agreement.

also.name: Martyr Adrian of Corinth
also.summary: A third-century Christian slain in Persia for the name of Christ.

also.name: Hieromartyrs Abdechalas the Priest and Ananias the Priest of Persia
also.summary: Two priests of fourth-century Persia who shared in the martyrdom of Symeon Bar Sabbae.

also.name: Martyrs Chusdazat (Usphazanes) and Azat the Eunuchs of Persia
Saint Acacius II, Bishop of Melitene
also.summary: A fifth-century bishop of Melitene in Armenia who took part in the Council of Ephesus against Nestorius.

also.name: Saint Agapitus I, Pope of Rome
also.summary: Sixth-century bishop of Rome who at Constantinople deposed the Monophysite patriarch Anthimus before his own repose.

also.name: Saint Ephraim the Great, Bishop of Atsquri

04-18.title: St. John, disciple of St. Gregory the Decapolite
04-18.summary: A ninth-century disciple of Gregory of Decapolis at Constantinople, an unyielding confessor of the icons under the second iconoclasm, who reposed in peace at the monastery his teacher had founded.

also.name: Martyrs Victor, Zoticus, Zeno, Acindynus, and Severian of Nicomedia
also.summary: Five Christians of fourth-century Nicomedia slain together under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyr Sabbas the Goth of Wallachia
Saint Acacius II, Bishop of Melitene
Saints Cosmas and Auxentius of Chalcedon
also.summary: Ninth-century bishop and his fellow ascetic, both confessors of the holy icons.

also.name: Venerable Naucratius, Abbot of the Stoudios Monastery
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the great Stoudios monastery of Constantinople, successor of Theodore the Studite.

also.name: Venerable Athanasia the Wonderworker of Aegina with Matthew (Matthias)
Venerable Euthymius the Wonderworker

04-19.title: St. John of the Ancient Caves
04-19.summary: A monk of the lavra of St. Chariton in Palestine in the eighth century, called of the Ancient Caves because the place he settled had been used by hermits before him going back to the third century.

also.name: Martyrs Theodore of Perge, his mother Philippa, with Dioscorus, Socrates, and Dionysius
also.summary: A young Christian of Pamphylia and his mother who at his witness confessed Christ, along with three companions, all crowned together under Antoninus.

also.name: Hieromartyr Paphnutius of Jerusalem and the five hundred and forty-six with him
Martyrs Hermogenes, Caius, Expeditus, Aristonicus, Rufus, and Galatas of Melitine
Martyrs Christopher, Theonas, and Antoninus of Nicomedia
Saint George the Confessor, Bishop of Antioch in Pisidia
also.summary: Eighth-century bishop who under Constantine V the dung-named was driven from his see for the cause of the holy icons.

also.name: Saint Tryphon, Patriarch of Constantinople
Venerable Nicephorus, Abbot of Katabad

04-20.title: St. Theodore Trichinas
04-20.summary: A fifth-century recluse of Constantinople who from his cell at Asomatos lived in such severe asceticism that his hair-shirt (whence his name) wore against his skin for forty years; many miracles followed at his shrine.

also.name: Apostle Zacchaeus of the Seventy, Bishop of Caesarea
also.summary: The publican of Jericho whom the Lord called down from the sycamore tree, who in the apostolic preaching became bishop of Caesarea in Palestine.

also.name: Martyrs Acindinus, Antoninus, Victor, Zenon, Zoticus, Theonas, Caesareus, Severian, and Christophoros
also.summary: Nine believers of the great persecution under Diocletian slain together for the name of Christ.

also.name: Saint Theotimus, Bishop of Tomis in Moesia
also.summary: An early-fifth-century bishop of Tomis on the Black Sea coast whose holy life so impressed the Huns that they called him 'the god of the Romans.'

also.name: Blessed Gregory, Patriarch of Antioch
Blessed Anastasius I, Patriarch of Antioch
Hieromartyr Anastasius II, Patriarch of Antioch
also.summary: Seventh-century patriarch of Antioch slain by Jews enraged at the imperial edict for their forced baptism.

also.name: Venerable Anastasius Sinaita
also.summary: Seventh-century abbot of Sinai whose Hodegos shaped Christology in the controversies of his age.

also.name: Venerable Ioannis Palaiolavritis of the Judean Desert

04-21.title: Hieromartyr Januarius of Beneventum and his companions
04-21.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Beneventum in Italy under Diocletian who with six others was beheaded at Pozzuoli; the cooled blood preserved in a vial at Naples has been observed to liquefy at its annual exposition.

also.name: Martyrs Theodore of Perge, his mother Philippa, and their companions
Martyr Alexandra the Empress
also.summary: Wife of Diocletian who at the witness of St. George openly confessed Christ and was beheaded with her attendants.

also.name: Martyrs Isaacius, Apollo, and Codratus of Nicomedia
Saint Maximianus, Archbishop of Constantinople
also.summary: Fifth-century archbishop of Constantinople, successor of Nestorius after the Third Ecumenical Council.

also.name: Venerable Anastasius Sinaita

04-22.title: St. Theodore the Sykeote, Bishop of Anastasiopolis
04-22.summary: A sixth-century Galatian who from boyhood took up the monastic life, became a great wonderworker, was made bishop against his will, and reposed back in the small cave-cell that had been his first hermitage.

also.name: Holy Apostle Nathaniel (Bartholomew) of the Twelve
also.summary: The disciple whom Philip brought to Christ at the beginning of the public ministry, by tradition Bartholomew of the Twelve.

also.name: Apostles Apelles, Luke, and Clement of the Seventy
also.summary: Three of the Seventy named by Paul: Apelles bishop of Heraclea, Luke bishop at Smyrna (not the Evangelist), and Clement of the Philippian church and third bishop of Rome.

also.name: Hieromartyr Leonides, Bishop of Alexandria
Martyr Nearchus
Saint Vitalis of Gaza
also.summary: An aged seventh-century monk of Gaza who in scandalous appearance lived among the women of the city to call them to repentance.

04-23.title: Great-martyr George the Trophy-bearer
04-23.summary: The young Cappadocian officer of the Roman army under Diocletian whose endurance through every torture and final beheading made him the icon of the soldier-saint of all the East; his cult spread from Lydda throughout the world.

also.name: Martyrs Donatus and Therinus of Bothrotus in Epirus
Saint Polychronia, mother of George the Trophy-bearer
also.summary: A pious Christian mother of Cappadocia who raised her son George in the faith before his martyrdom at Lydda.

also.name: Martyrs Anatolius and Protoleon of Nicomedia
also.summary: Two Roman officers converted at the spectacle of George's endurance and beheaded with him.

also.name: Martyrs Glycerius and Athanasius the Magician of Nicomedia
also.summary: A Christian of Nicomedia and a converted magician slain together with George.

also.name: Martyr Valerius
Martyr Alexandra the Empress
also.summary: The wife of Diocletian who at the witness of St. George confessed Christ and was beheaded.

04-24.title: Martyr Sabbas the Stratelates and seventy soldiers with him
04-24.summary: A Gothic officer in the Roman army at Rome under Aurelian who together with seventy of his fellow soldiers was beheaded in 272 for confessing Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Pasicrates, Valentine, and Julius of Dorostolum
also.summary: Three Christians of fourth-century Moesia slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyrs Eusebius, Neon, Leontius, Longinus, and four companions of Nicomedia
also.summary: Eight Christians of fourth-century Nicomedia slain together under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyr Eutexios
Saint Innocent the Priest of the Mount of Olives
Venerable Thomas the Fool-for-Christ of Syria
also.summary: A sixth-century holy fool of Syria whose disciplined poverty drew the ridicule of all who did not see what he saw.

also.name: Venerable Elizabeth the Wonderworker of Constantinople
Venerable Thaumastos the Wonderworker
Saint Xenophon, Founder of Xenophontos Monastery on Mount Athos

04-25.title: Holy Apostle and Evangelist Mark
04-25.summary: The companion of Peter who wrote the second Gospel from the apostle's preaching at Rome, and the first bishop of Alexandria — dragged through the streets of the city by a rope until he gave up his soul.

also.name: Saint Anianas, second Bishop of Alexandria
also.summary: The cobbler whom Mark by miracle converted in Alexandria and afterwards ordained as his successor on the throne of the see.

also.name: Martyr Nike
Hieromartyr Stephen II, Patriarch of Antioch
also.summary: A fifth-century patriarch of Antioch who, having confessed the faith of Chalcedon against the monophysite party, was cast into the Orontes.

also.name: Saint Macedonius II, Patriarch of Constantinople
also.summary: Sixth-century patriarch of Constantinople, defender of the Council of Chalcedon, exiled by Anastasius the emperor.

also.name: The Eight Holy Anchorites

04-26.title: Hieromartyr Basil, Bishop of Amasea
04-26.summary: A fourth-century bishop of Amasea on the Black Sea who tried to deliver the empress Glaphyra from the unwanted attentions of Licinius; the emperor in his rage had Basil beheaded on the shore of the Bosphorus.

also.name: Martyrs Cyril, Chindeu, and Tasie of Axiopolis
Righteous Virgin-martyr Glaphyra of Nicomedia
also.summary: Fourth-century handmaid of the empress Constantia who, fleeing Licinius's pursuit, escaped to St. Basil at Amasea and reposed in his protection.

also.name: Saints Andrew and Anatolius, disciples of Euthymius the Great
also.summary: Two fifth-century monastics of Palestine trained at the lavra of Euthymius.

also.name: Saint Justa the Nun
Venerable Nestor
Saint Leo of Samos, the Wonderworker
also.summary: Ninth-century bishop of Samos renowned for the gift of healing.

04-27.title: Hieromartyr Symeon, kinsman of the Lord, second Bishop of Jerusalem
04-27.summary: Son of Clopas the brother of Joseph the Betrothed, who succeeded James as bishop of Jerusalem after his martyrdom. He was crucified under Trajan at the age of one hundred and twenty.

also.name: Martyrs Publius and Lollion the Younger
Saint Pollion the Reader of Cibalis
also.summary: An early-fourth-century reader of the church at Cibalis in Pannonia burned alive under Diocletian.

also.name: Saint Eulogius the Hospitable of Constantinople
also.summary: A sixth-century Christian of Constantinople so given to hospitality that the angels in vision were said to seek out his lodging.

also.name: Saint Nicon, Abbot of the Monastery of St. Gerasimus
also.summary: A sixth-century abbot of the monastery of Gerasimus on the Jordan.

also.name: Venerable Simeon Stylites the Younger of Cilicia
also.summary: A sixth-century stylite of Mount Admirable near Antioch who from boyhood lived on a pillar and reposed there at the age of seventy-five.

also.name: Saint John of Cathares
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the Cathares monastery at Constantinople, confessor of the holy icons under the second iconoclasm.

04-28.title: Apostles Jason and Sosipater of the Seventy
04-28.summary: Two kinsmen of Paul named in Romans 16, who together evangelized the island of Corfu; their preaching converted a number of brigands at the king's prison, and they reposed in peace after long labors.

also.name: Martyrs Saturninus, Jakischolus, Faustianus, Januarius, Marsalius, Euphrasius, Mammius, the Seven Former Thieves
also.summary: Seven robbers of first-century Corfu converted in prison by Jason and Sosipater and beheaded for the name of Christ.

also.name: Virgin-martyr Cercyra of Corfu
also.summary: Daughter of the king of Corfu converted at the witness of Jason and Sosipater and slain by her father for the name of Christ.

also.name: Martyrs Zeno, Eusebius, Neon, and Vitalis of Corfu
Venerable Memnon the Wonderworker

04-29.title: The Nine Martyrs of Cyzicus
04-29.summary: Nine Christians from various cities of Asia Minor who, gathered at Cyzicus on the Sea of Marmara, were beheaded together for refusing the imperial sacrifice in the fourth century.

also.name: Apostles Jason and Sosipater of the Seventy
The Seven Former Thieves, with Cercyra of Corfu
Martyrs Zeno, Eusebius, Neon, and Vitalis of Corfu
Martyrs Vitalius and Valeria of Milan
also.summary: First-century husband and wife of Milan, parents of Gervasius and Protasius, who together received the crown of martyrdom.

also.name: Venerable Memnon the Wonderworker
Martyrs Diodorus and Rhodopianus the Deacons of Aphrodisia
also.summary: Two deacons of Aphrodisia in Caria slain together for the name of Christ under Diocletian.

also.name: Martyrs Atticus and Cyntianus
Saint John Talaia, Patriarch of Alexandria
Saint Nicetas, Abbot of Synnada in Phrygia
Saint Nicephorus of Sebaze

04-30.title: Holy Apostle James, brother of John the Theologian
04-30.summary: One of the sons of Zebedee, who left his boats with his brother at the Lord's call and stood with him on Tabor; the first apostle to be martyred, slain by Herod at Jerusalem in the year 44.

also.name: St. Ignatius Brianchaninov
Hieromartyr Maximus of Ephesus
also.summary: A third-century Christian of Ephesus crowned under Decius for his confession.

also.name: Hieromartyr Aphrodisius the Priest and his thirty companions of Alexandria
Saint Donatus, Bishop of Euroea in Epirus
also.summary: A late-fourth-century bishop of Epirus whose holy life flourished under the emperor Theodosius the Great.

also.name: Saint Clement the Hymnographer, Abbot of the Stoudios
also.summary: Ninth-century abbot of the Stoudios monastery in Constantinople, composer of many of the canons of the Octoechos.
